experiments.title: Can a lower, non-preselected gift array improve conversion rate and revenue across acquisition-focused content offers?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believed that using a lower gift array without preselecting an amount for acquisition audiences would achieve higher conversion rates and competitive revenue because reducing perceived commitment and decision friction would encourage first-time donors to complete a gift.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/22/2025
The Hoover Institution continues to grow its fundraising program by standardizing high-impact donation form elements while preserving flexibility by offer and channel. As part of this effort, Hoover evaluated gift array size and preselection as scalable levers that influence both donor conversion and revenue performance across predominantly acquisition-focused content offers.
This write-up summarizes aggregate performance across multiple offers, focusing on conversion rate, revenue, and average order value (AOV). Four variants were tested: a higher gift array ($50/100/250) and a lower gift array ($25/50/100), each with and without preselecting the second gift amount. The goal was to identify a default configuration that maximizes acquisition efficiency without materially compromising gift value.
experiments.keyLearnings:
At the aggregate level, the experiment produced a statistically valid conversion rate winner, with directional but informative revenue and AOV patterns.
📊 Conversion Rate (Statistically Evaluated)
By individual variant
Low array, no selectConversion rate: 6.2%Lift vs. high array, no select: +32.1%Confidence: 96.0% (statistically valid) ✅High array, no select (control): 4.7%Low array, select: 5.6% (not statistically valid)High array, select: 4.3%
By aggregated comparison
Low array vs. high array (all traffic combined)Low array CR: 5.93%High array CR: 4.52%Confidence: ~99.5% (statistically valid) ✅No select vs. selectNo select CR: 5.48%Select CR: 4.96%Confidence: ~70% (not valid) ❌
Takeaway:
The lower gift array is a statistically proven driver of higher conversion rate. Preselection does not provide a reliable conversion benefit at the aggregate level.
💰 Revenue (Directional)
Revenue results did not reach statistical validity and should be interpreted directionally.
High array, no select: $3,951Low array, no select: $4,249 (highest-performing no-select variant)High array, select: $4,309Low array, select: $3,634
While the high array with preselection generated the most total revenue, it did so with lower conversion efficiency and without statistical confidence. The low array without preselection delivered strong total revenue while also maximizing donor acquisition, making it the most balanced option for acquisition-focused goals.
🎯 Average Order Value (AOV – Directional)
High array (combined): ~$46.40Low array (combined): ~$34.73Select (combined): ~$40.73No select (combined): ~$39.05
Preselection and higher arrays increase AOV, but these gains consistently come with conversion tradeoffs, particularly for acquisition traffic.
✂️ Aggregate Interpretation
The lower gift array without preselection is the only variant that is statistically validated to improve conversion rateRevenue differences are directional, but the low array/no select performs competitively while acquiring more donorsPreselecting any amount introduces friction and does not produce consistent gains in conversion or revenue at scaleHigher arrays may be situationally valuable, but should be deployed intentionally and tested by offer
Next Steps
Roll out the lower gift array without preselection as the default for predominantly acquisition-focused content offersDo not preselect a gift amount in the standard configurationUse this result as a new baseline, not a permanent winnerTest deviations by offer and channel, requiring ≥90% statistical significance to override the defaultPlan future tests during offer launches and explore donation page copy and design as higher-impact leversRe-test as the program, traffic mix, and donation forms evolve
Rather than treating A/B testing as a one-time selection of a winner, this approach supports a living optimization framework, where standards are periodically challenged as strategies and audiences change.

experiments.title: How reinforcing security on a campaign donation page donor conversion and overall revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a statement of security to the year end donation page will increase donor conversion and overall revenue, as it enhances clarity regarding how a potential donor’s data is being used.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/23/2025 - 04/30/2025
America Needs Fatima ran a calendar year-end campaign during December. With increasing concerns about privacy and user data, the team wanted to test whether adding a statement about the organization’s privacy commitment on the donation page would affect transactions and overall campaign revenue. The next step is to analyze the test results to determine whether the privacy statement affected donor behavior or revenue. The test revealed that including a privacy statement had a measurable effect on donation page conversions and total year-end campaign revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a statement of security on the donation page significantly increased donor conversion and overall revenue. The treatment group, which included the statement of security, saw a 50.5% increase in revenue compared to the control group. This result was statistically significant at a 98.5% confidence level.
This suggests that potential donors highly value transparency and clarity regarding the use of their data. By addressing their concerns about privacy and security, we were able to gain their trust and increase their willingness to make a donation.
In future experiments, consider adding a statement regarding security or privacy on donation pages. Use this on any page where user data is collected. This can help alleviate concerns and increase conversion rates. Further explore other ways to enhance transparency and build trust with donors. Regularly test and iterate on these strategies to boost revenue.

experiments.title: How adding an animated heart on a tribute widget affects tribute revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an animated heart to tribute widget CTA’s for profile visitors will achieve an increase in revenue because adding the animated heart would capture the attention of the user and stand out, bringing more clarity to what the widget is and what the ask is.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/29/2025 - 08/01/2025
For CaringBridge, we wanted to test adding an animated heart to the CTA of tribute widgets to see how that would affect tribute donations and revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding an animated heart to the CTA of tribute widgets for profile visitors resulted in a significant 13.0% increase in Revenue - Tribute for All Traffic with a 100.0% statistical level of confidence. This means that the treatment was successful in capturing the attention of users and driving more donations.

In future experimentation, we should consider incorporating more visual elements or animations into our CTAs to make them more engaging and attention-grabbing. It's important to continue testing different design elements and messaging to see what resonates best with our audience and drives the most donations. Additionally, we should apply the learnings from this experiment to other areas of our website or campaigns to optimize our efforts and increase overall revenue for our cause.

experiments.title: How adding an impact statement below the donate button during a Challenge Grant affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the copy on the donate button and adding a statement highlighting an impact statement for donation page visitors will achieve an increase in revenue and donations because highlighting the impact of their gift and reminding them that the impact of the gift will double would encourage users to donate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/04/2025 - 08/06/2025
Time of Grace has a challenge grant every year in August. This allows users to double the impact of their gift up to the goal amount that TOG sets, with each challenge grant highlighting a problem that TOG is trying to tackle. For this test, we wanted to test changing the copy on the donate button and adding a statement below the donate button highlighting the impact of the user’s gift. The statement was dynamic and would pull in the amount that the user was planning on donating. So for example, if the user were to donate $25, the statement would read as “Your $25 gift connects more people with the healing and freedom found only in Christ when we are equipped to confess and confront the struggles we don’t talk about.”
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that changing the copy on the donate button and adding a personalized impact statement below the button significantly increased revenue and donations. The treatment group, which included the updated copy and impact statement, saw a substantial 187.7% increase in revenue compared to the control group. This result was statistically significant at a 100.0% confidence level.

It is also important to note that we saw a slight increase in transactions as well, making sure that this experiment did not cause a decrease.

In future experimentation, it is crucial to continue emphasizing the impact of the donation and the concept of doubling the impact through the challenge grant. Highlighting the tangible outcomes of a user's donation can motivate them to contribute more and feel a stronger connection to the cause they are supporting. Personalized impact statements based on the user's intended donation amount can also be a powerful way to make the donation feel more meaningful and relevant to the donor.

Overall, this experiment demonstrates the importance of effectively communicating the impact of a donation to potential donors and how small changes to the donation process can lead to significant increases in revenue. These learnings should be applied to future experiments and donation campaigns to continue optimizing donation processes and driving more support for Time of Grace Ministry.

experiments.title: How clarifying the perception of the problem on a main donation page impacted revenue and donor conversions

rows.name: Treatment - Improved Perception of the Problem

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that strengthening the perception of the problems that the organization is working to solve for visitors to the main donation page will achieve an increase in donor conversion because urgent, problem-oriented framing enhances perceived relevance and impact.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/18/2025 - 07/09/2025
Focus on the Family’s main donation page presents a high-leverage opportunity for revenue growth—if we can identify changes that lift donor conversion. Despite this potential, nearly every prior test on the page has either validated a decrease or yielded no significant improvement.
This experiment aimed to break that pattern by testing a new value proposition. The control copy offered a general appeal to “empower families with biblical resources,” while the treatment made the problem more vivid and urgent. It introduced specific pain points families face—financial strain, broken communication, cultural confusion, and spiritual drift—and positioned the donor’s gift as a direct response to these needs.
The test ran on the primary donation page for users who clicked the main “Donate” button in site navigation. This was our first test in a planned series of experiments to refine how FOTF frames its impact—and to better understand what kind of value proposition most effectively motivates donor action.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test showed that deepening the emotional urgency and specificity of the value proposition can meaningfully shift donor behavior—particularly in prompting recurring giving.
The treatment, which emphasized the family crisis and FOTF’s role in meeting those needs, lifted overall donor conversion by 11.9% at an 82.4% level of confidence. While not at the desired 95% validation threshold, these results show that the reframed value proposition resonates more strongly with site visitors.
The most notable shift came in recurring gifts, where the treatment produced a 248.7% lift at a 97.1% confidence level. This indicates that donors exposed to the new framing may not only be more likely to give—but more likely to commit long-term. This behavioral shift could reflect an increased sense of urgency, credibility, or alignment with the organization’s long-term mission.
Interestingly, we observed a -3.8% decrease in one-time gifts, statistically validated at 98.4% confidence. However, the higher average gift size ($56.42 vs. $47.03) and greater total revenue ($51.6k vs. $42k) from the treatment suggest a net positive financial outcome, despite the slight decline in single transactions.
What does this mean?
The results point toward the power of emotionally resonant, problem-focused messaging to increase commitment and revenue—even if it slightly narrows broad appeal. Future tests might explore how to preserve or recover one-time volume while building on the strong gains in recurring giving and total revenue.Next steps: We will test this winning treatment language against a new version of the donation page copy that approaches the value proposition from an identity-based angle, to see which version works best for converting page visitors into donors

experiments.title: How reordering the Tribute and Requests tab affect tribute revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reordering the Tribute and Requests tab for profile visitors will achieve an increase in tribute revenue because reordering the tabs so that the tribute widget is on the end would give more prominence to that tab and draw more attention to it.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/07/2025 - 06/11/2025
For this CaringBridge experiment, we wanted to test whether reordering the Requests and Tributes tab would affect the tribute revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:
Reordering the Requests and Tributes tab to prioritize the Tribute tab resulted in a 17.3% increase in revenue from tribute donations. This indicates that giving more prominence to the Tribute tab can lead to a significant boost in revenue.
 	Despite the increase in revenue, there was a 91% decrease in Tribute Tab clicks. This suggests that while fewer people clicked on the Tribute Tab, those who did were more likely to make a donation.
 	We should consider the overall revenue impact rather than just focusing on click-through rates when analyzing the effectiveness of tab placement.
 	Future experimentation could involve testing different placements of the Tribute tab to optimize for both click-through rates and revenue generation. It is also important to continue monitoring user behavior and donation patterns to make data-driven decisions for improving the user experience and maximizing donations on the platform.

experiments.title: How retargeting direct mail recipients with Facebook ads during year-end impacts total revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that utilizing Facebook ads to retarget direct mail recipients will achieve an increase in donations because it helps reinforce that the mail piece is incoming – keeping the ask and year-end giving to Time of Grace top of mind.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/03/2025
During Time of Grace’s calendar year-end campaign, we hypothesized that retargeting ads in Facebook that referenced the incoming direct mail piece would increase donor conversion rate. It is important to note that these ads did not contain an ask or a donation page to give. Instead, direct mail donors were retargeted with ads that just reinforced the timing of year-end and that their direct mail piece was in the mail. Our hypothesis is that this second-channel approach would keep the direct mail piece (and Time of Grace’s ministry) top of mind during this critical fundraising period – resulting in more donors looking out for the piece and donating to it. The second related hypothesis was that if efforts did not increase donations directly to the mail piece, treatment donors would still give more overall to the organization (in either offline or online).
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Learnings from the Experiment:

First, it was worth noting that the treatment did not increase overall donations and revenue directly to the offline mail piece. However, when expanding the definition to include giving in the control and treatment (both online and offline) the increase in revenue is statistically significant. This means that this multi-channel targeting, if not directly impactful upon offline giving, still increases overall giving to the ministry across channels.

Here is an additional breakdown of key facts: 

1. Utilizing Facebook ads to retarget direct mail recipients resulted in a significant increase in revenue for Time of Grace Ministry.

2. The treatment group, which received the retargeting ads referencing the incoming direct mail piece, showed a 16.9% increase in revenue compared to the control group.

3. The increase in revenue was statistically significant at a 100.0% confidence level, indicating that the results were not due to chance.

4. The hypothesis that using Facebook ads to remind donors of the incoming direct mail piece would increase donations and keep the organization top of mind during the critical year-end fundraising period was validated.

experiments.title: How a sliding bar on a campaign donation page allowing donors to adjust their impact influences conversion rate and revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that allowing donors to select the level of their impact dynamically will increase revenue during a high-urgency campaign by increasing the perceived clarity and appeal of the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/05/2024
During ACI’s Giving Tuesday campaign, we wanted to increase the tangibility and clarity of the donor’s impact on the campaign’s donation page. The page already highlighted the number of people that would be reached with a donor’s gift. However, we hypothesized that allowing the donors to drag a slider that dynamically changes the number of people reached would increase the clarity of the offer when compared with the current  experience.
We used VWO to create a graphical, interactive slider on the page that dynamically changed the gift amount and the corresponding number of people the gift would reach. We split traffic 50/50 and all campaign sources were directed to the test.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that introducing a graphical, interactive slider on the donation page, allowing donors to dynamically select the level of their impact, can significantly increase revenue during a high-urgency campaign. The treatment group saw a 28.9% increase in revenue for all traffic with a 95.7% level of confidence.
Although we saw a valid increase in revenue, we actually saw a 19.5% decrease in transactions with an 86.6% level of confidence.
In future experimentation, we should continue to explore interactive elements that provide donors with a clear and tangible understanding of the impact of their donations. This includes incorporating dynamic features that allow donors to see in real-time how their contributions will make a difference. Additionally, we should aim to reach the minimum sample size required for statistical significance to ensure the reliability of the results.
Overall, it is crucial to prioritize clarity, personalization, and interactivity in donation pages to engage donors effectively and drive increased contributions, especially during high-urgency campaigns like Giving Tuesday.

experiments.title: How humanized letter copy with photo alignment impacts revenue on main donate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that humanized letter copy with photo alignment for users on the main donation page will achieve an increase in donor conversion and revenue because it causes the main donation page to read like it’s from a real person, humanizing the experience.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/07/2024 - 08/13/2024
For CaringBridge’s main donation page, we had tested a series of copy iteration on the main donation page that removed the photo background in favor of a cleaner design, with copy that was stylized to read like a humanized letter. This included a greeting, copy being updated so that it read like an address from Kelly, as well as her sign-off and photo a the bottom. Initially, this no difference when it was tested previously.
However, we hypothesized that by simply updating this letter copy treatment to also include Kelly’s photo aligned at the top of the letter versus at the bottom with her signature would help drive home the humanization of the treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Learnings from the Experiment:

1. Humanization of the donation page: By aligning Kelly's photo at the top of the humanized letter copy, the treatment created a more personal and engaging experience for users. This likely contributed to the significant increase in donor conversion and revenue.

2. Importance of personalized communication: Addressing users as if they were receiving a letter from Kelly added a personal touch to the donation page. This approach resonated well with users and led to a higher conversion rate.

3. Visual hierarchy matters: Placing Kelly's photo at the top of the letter helped to establish a visual hierarchy that drew users' attention to the humanized content. This likely played a role in driving the increased engagement and conversion.

4.Additionally, this also had the impact of acting as a social proof since in the copy Kelly also shares how she herself has been impacted by CaringBridge and donor generosity.

In conclusion, the success of this experiment highlights the importance of humanizing the donation experience and personalizing communication with donors. By leveraging these learnings and continuing to test and optimize the user experience, CaringBridge can further improve donor engagement and ultimately drive more significant impact for the nonprofit organization.

experiments.title: How adding a separate “Give Monthly” button on the website menu impacts donation conversions and revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a “Give Monthly” button for the main website will achieve an increase in donations and overall revenue because it increases clarity for people wanting to make a gift to Care Net.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/25/2023 - 12/20/2023
Care Net has a robust recurring donor program that has grown significantly over the years. Recently, due to a variety of factors, they have seen a plateau in the conversion of recurring donors.
On their main website, they have a “Donate” button in the top right-hand corner. For this test, we wanted to see how adding a “Give Monthly” button next to the “Donate” button impacted three factors: one-time donations, recurring donations, and overall revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a "Give Monthly" button alongside the existing "Donate" button on the main website resulted in a significant increase in revenue for all traffic. The treatment group saw a 74.8% increase in revenue compared to the control group, with a high level of statistical confidence at 95.4%.

This suggests that the presence of a "Give Monthly" button provided clarity for people wanting to make a recurring donation, leading to a higher conversion rate and ultimately more revenue.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to further explore the impact of different call-to-action buttons and messaging on donation conversion. This could involve testing variations of the button design, placement, and wording to determine the most effective combination. Additionally, analyzing the data from this experiment can provide insights into the specific donor segments that responded most positively to the "Give Monthly" button, allowing for targeted marketing efforts in the future.

experiments.title: How offering a book as a premium with a donation vs at retail price impacts conversion rate and overall revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that offering a new book as a premium gift in exchange for a donation rather than as a product for purchase for Time of Grace’s donor file will achieve an increase in transactions and revenue because users are more likely to be motivated by the generosity of the organization, and the value of receiving the gift for a donation.. because users are more likely to be motivated by the generosity of the organization, and the value of receiving the gift for a donation..
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/16/2024
For Time of Grace’s monthly book promotion, we hypothesized that offering the book in exchange for a donation would result in increased transactions and revenue versus just offering it for purchase. Historically, Time of Grace has promoted their new books on their Shopify store at retail price. This test was accomplished by making a change to two areas of the funnel. The control vs treatment emails would have a change in the call-to-action copy. The control (for purchase) would lead them to Time of Grace’s Shopify page, whereas the treatment would lead them to a donation page that had the book as a premium on it.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that offering a new book as a premium gift in exchange for a donation, instead of selling it at retail price, significantly increased the number of donations received. The treatment group saw a 92.2% increase in donations compared to the control group, with a high statistical level of confidence.

Not only did this result in an increase in donor/purchaser conversion rate, it also resulted in a valid 199% increase in revenue with a 98.9% level of confidence.

Control: 27 Purchases | $996 in Revenue | $36.89 Average Gift

Treatment 52 Donations |$2,985 in Revenue | $57.40 Average Gift

This approach also did not limit the ministry's impact in actual copies "sold" with 53 units being purchased under the control and 55 units being requested as premiums with a donation.

This indicates that users are more motivated by the generosity of the organization and the perceived value of receiving a gift in exchange for their donation. Therefore, in future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue offering premium gifts as incentives for donations.

This could be applied to other products or promotions as well, where offering a premium gift or exclusive content in exchange for a donation or purchase may motivate users to take action and increase overall transactions and revenue. The organization should consider implementing this strategy more frequently to leverage the increased motivation and generosity of their audience.

experiments.title: How adding a relevant premium in an offer affects giving
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a relevant premium for offer downloaders will achieve an increase in average gift because they would be incentivized to give a larger gift when they receive something in return.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/23/2023 - 07/03/2023
Navigators has historically had mixed results with the use of premiums on an offer. We have seen the most success with premiums that are directly correlated with the digital product they’re receiving. In a new offer (an online course offer) we added a relevant premium (the book version of the ebook included) for a gift of $50 or greater. We wanted to test to see if we would get a larger average gift with a relevant premium with this particular offer. Increasing the average gift would positively impact the ROI of the offer, which had been very low.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a relevant premium for offer downloaders did not lead to an increase in average gift. In fact, the treatment group saw a significant decrease in revenue compared to the control group. This experiment resulted in a valid 75% decrease in revenue with a level of confidence of 95%.
It is important to note that the experiment did not reach the minimum sample size needed for statistical significance. We only had 9 transactions and did not meet the 50 transaction minimum.
A few things to note:
The offer in it of itself did not perform well. This offer had a high cost per name and did not get a lot of traffic which resulted in a low number of transactions before we had to turn off the experiment. In the treatment we wanted to include $50 in the gift array because there is that specific ask for the premium. Unfortunately, the starting amount was significantly lower than the control's and it's likely that this lower gift array actually resulted in a lower average gift. There was no visual way to call out the premium section. The platform limits our capability and the book on the left hand side is the only place we can add an image.
It's suggested we retest this with the following adjustments:
The same gift array for control/treatmentA visual callout of the premium language so it stands out to the reader (like a box outline around the test)
Based on these findings, it would be recommended to conduct a larger experiment with a sufficient sample size to get a more accurate understanding of the impact of the premium.

experiments.title: How adding a book as a premium offer increases the average gift on an eBook instant donation page.
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a premium for ebook donors will achieve a higher average gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/11/2022 - 10/12/2022
The Avila Foundation has created a website, SpiritualDirection.com, where anyone can go and find answers to questions they might have about their spiritual journey. As part of the efforts to educate the laity through authentic Catholic teachings, Avila has created a series of ebooklets.
For one of these ebooklets, Insights on the Contemplative Rosary, we wanted to test whether adding a premium offer, in this case a physical copy of the book upon which the ebooklet was based, would help increase the average gift. Our hypothesis was that the combination of the two–using the ebooklet as a study guide to the book–would entice donors to contribute more to Avila.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While there was a slight decline in the total number of donations while we ran this experiment (38 to 37), the average gift on the premium treatment page increased by 58%, which also resulted in a 58% increase in total revenue with a 94% level of confidence.
We believe that by positioning the ebooklet as a companion piece to the book, those donors who were brand new, or had not yet gotten a chance to get the book, were convinced to give a gift of a higher amount.
While the average gift for the treatment version was below the $50 we were asking in order to receive a copy of the book, there was enough data to suggest that further refinement of this offer, as well as further testing with other premiums, is warranted.

experiments.title: How increasing the force of the value proposition affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a stronger value proposition increase donations and revenue, in spite of a different design?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/09/2016 - 03/15/2016
The Heritage Foundation was sending lots of traffic to their “Conservative Declaration”, a document that asked conservatives to unite under shared principles. Immediately after signing, the visitors were presented with an offer to become a member of The Heritage Foundation by making a donation. The entire experience took place within a single design.
Heritage had another membership page that had been performing extremely well. However, design was a key part of this page, and it didn’t match the design of the Conservative Declaration. Knowing that congruency is a key part of conversion, they decided to test it anyways to see if increasing the force of the value proposition outweighed design consistency.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment produced a 460% increase in revenue, despite breaking from the design of the original offer. This is a powerful learning that shows that the message is more important than the container. This also points the way to a future test to see if revenue can be further increased by working the new value proposition into the existing design and increasing congruency.
experiments.fluxMetrics:

experiments.title: How lowering the gift array options for an acquisition offer affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that lowering the gift array options for potential donors who just signed up for an online course will achieve a higher donor conversion rate on the instant donation page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/29/2022 - 10/20/2022
Luther Seminary has been running an acquisition offer on Facebook for a free online course for the book of Revelation. The other acquisition offers they are offering are free study guides for the Book of Proverbs and Book of Revelation, so this online course was something different and new to give to their audience.
The performance of this online course shortly after launching it was very good with the ICR averaging at 4.7% and the GCPD averaging at $110.
We’ve been testing the lower gift array on both study guide funnels since the control gift array seems to be a high ask for an offer with a lower value. We decided to test this same concept on this online course since, historically, online courses are perceived to provide more value to potential donors.
We wanted to see if this audience would react any differently to seeing a lower gift array when offered a higher value free resource.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The lower gift array did not have an effect on donor conversion rate as we saw a 13.1% decrease in conversion rate with only a 43.3% LoC. Although, the lower gift array did have a large impact on average gift resulting in a 45.4% decrease in revenue with a 97.6% LoC.

We believe this happened because the donors on the control page saw the value of this online course therefore the lowest ask of $50 did not sway them from giving a gift. But the donors who saw the treatment page were more likely to give a smaller gift of $25.

We now can assume that donors are more likely to give higher gifts when presented the option to do so when the offer they received is of high value.

Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
New visitors had a 34.7% decrease in revenue with an 89% level of confidence.
 	Email visitors had a 50.8% decrease in revenue with a 98% level of confidence.
 	Returning visitors had a 72.2% decrease in revenue with a 97% level of confidence.

				gift).
				This experiment produced the following results:
0% increase in traffic
				× 13.1% decrease in conversion rate
				× 37.3% decrease in average gift
									45.5% decrease in revenue

experiments.title: How additional payment options impacted long-term revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: How will the ACH form impact donor giving?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/20/2016 - 01/10/2017
This test was launched on a recurring donor acquisition campaign for HAFA’s Liberty Brigade. For this campaign, a recurring donation was the pre-selected value on the form.
As far as conversion rate is concerned, there was no significant difference between the two forms (3.99% for the control and 3.94% for the ACH form). This is a good sign since there was concern that the ACH option would add friction to the donation process.
When we analyze the impact on revenue, the value of ACH recurring gifts resulted in an overall 55% lift to long-term revenue (this includes both immediate gifts and gifts based upon the average lifespan of the recurring gift).
What is interesting is that of the recurring donors, only 15% selected the ACH giving option but that 15% led to that significant increase to long-term revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Heritage Action for America recently implemented an upgrade to their donation form. Historically, the online donation forms had only been able to capture credit cards. A recent upgrade provided the ability to also capture ACH transactions (donors could still make credit card gifts if they preferred).

The additional value provided by ACH comes from recurring gifts. The average lifespan of a credit card recurring gift is roughly 12 months. For ACH recurring gifts, the average lifespan expands to be 5 years. However, our concern was that adding in the additional option of ACH may create friction in the donation process which may reduce overall donor conversion. As a result, we decided to test the new form against the control.

				× 13.6% decrease in average gift

experiments.title: How changing the landing page design affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Which landing page design produces the most donor conversions and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/09/2015 - 06/23/2015
The Heritage Foundation had been using the same landing page design for their search engine marketing (SEM) donor campaigns for the past three years. During those three years, we had run multiple experiments on the main organizational donation page which had gradually evolved into something that looked completely different.
We were in the process of moving the SEM page to the same domain as the main donation form and realized that it would be a good time to validate that the old design was still effective for the SEM audience segment.  We created an experiment by duplicating the page content but displaying it on the two different page designs.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The new design treatment caused an 85.1% drop in revenue by decreasing conversion rate by 67.6% and decreasing average gift by 54.1%. 

The simplified design used in the treatment was developed for a page with minimal copy. When we added the long value proposition text that had been developed for this specific landing page, it became difficult to read.

Landing pages must be designed so that they are easily scannable by the visitor. In this situation, the sub-headlines didn't stand out enough and the lack of page border made the page seem longer than it was.  All of these elements caused enough friction in the mind of the donor to have a negative impact on both conversion rate and average gift.

				gift).

0% increase in traffic
				× 67.6% decrease in conversion rate
				× 54.1% decrease in average gift
									85.1% decrease in revenue

experiments.title: How a lesser ask impacts revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by asking nondonors to give a lesser amount, they will be more likely to make a gift.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 09/14/2022
Pathway to Victory promotes a premium each month through its website, tv, radio, and email. The goal is to get resources to strengthen one’s faith and equip one to pierce a dark and fallen culture. But also to raise revenue to keep PTV on the air and expand worldwide.
In the last several months PTV has seen a decline in new donor conversions and overall revenue. This led us to test asking for a lower amount for the promoted premium.
The control group was asked for a gift of $25 or more, while the treatment was asked for a gift of $10 or more.
We kept this test segmented only to PTV nondonors that are on their email list.
We conducted this test over the course of 3 emails (email 1, resend of email 1 to those that did not click the link, and email 2) while keeping the control and treatment audiences separated. And suppressing those that made a gift with each send.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Not only did this test increase the average gift by 24.7% we observed the donors that received the $10 ask were more likely to make a larger gift.
Break down of actual gift amounts from the $25 ask
2 - $11 - $51 - $89 - $101 - $2018 - $251 - $352 - $502 - $100
Break down of actual gift amounts from the $10 ask
1 - $22 - $532 - $106 - $155 - $2013 - $251 -  $303 - $502 - $754 - $1001 - $2001 - $400
Since PTV premiums can be acquired with a gift of any amount, they have struggled with donors that give very low $1-5 gifts for the premium. With this test, we not only saw an increase in donations for those that received the $10 ask, we saw a decrease in the very low gift amounts compared to the $25 ask.
Our hypothesis is that when asked for a $10 donation, this ask was low enough that the very low gift donors decided to give the full ask. For an individual donor, the ask for $10 and meeting it when they were expecting to make a low donation is a small sacrifice. But this pays off greatly for the organization when so many people are able to make this small step up from $1-5 to $10.
The number of donor conversions saw a 94.6% increase with a LOC 99.9%
Link clicks in the email saw a 13.3% increase with a LOC 98.1%

									142.7% increase in revenue

experiments.title: The revenue impact of shorter and more efficient value proposition copy on a premium offer donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that shorter and more efficient value proposition copy will reduce friction and increase donor conversion on the donation page
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/26/2022 - 08/14/2022
FamilyLife is a Christian ministry serving families worldwide. They use premium offers on Facebook to acquire new donors and have been very successful with this approach. For their most popular offer, Seeing Beautiful Again, they wanted to increase the donor conversion on the donation page. In reviewing the copy, we found that it appeared quite long on mobile devices, which were the primary devices visiting the page.
We hypothesized that shorter and more efficient value propositional copy would increase donor conversion by reducing friction on the page.
We developed a treatment version that reduced the word count from 307 words on the control to 154 on the treatment while retaining what we felt was the core value proposition of the premium and of donating to FamilyLife. We split traffic and ran the experiment almost two months through Optimize
experiments.keyLearnings:
There wasn't a significant source of traffic that experienced a decrease from the shorter treatment. We also verified that there were no larger gifts skewing the results. Donor conversion also increased, however we were not able to validate the increase on donor conversion. However, once again, the increase was being found on desktop, mobile, new, and returning visitor segments so we had no concerns with rolling out the shorter text on the page and ending the test.
We will be using this approach going forward.

experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that focusing on a daily goal of donors for the digital audience will achieve a higher conversion rate.

During the Missionary Oblates’ calendar year-end campaign, we ran an experiment where we would be testing a focus on a revenue goal ($100,00 raised by the end of the year) versus a focus on getting a specific number of donors per day (92) to be able to hit the goal. This campaign was tested across multiple emails.
experiments.keyLearnings: Focusing on a daily donor goal led to an increase in conversion rates.

experiments.title: How getting straight the point in your email copy leads to an increase in conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that justifying why we’re sending the email sooner in the copy for our email housefile will achieve more ebook downloads.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/09/2020 - 12/14/2020
We had previously run an a/b test where we saw that telling a longer personal story was more effective at generating clicks than a brief personal story. But I wondered if actually getting to the point of why we’re emailing right off the bat might be even more effective – while still utilizing a personal tone and format.
Version A led with a personal note about the holiday season and how our family was getting in the Christmas spirit. Version B jumped right to the primary reason I was emailing – to give them a free ebook to increase their year-end revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Getting straight to the point and justifying why we are emailing right away led to a 36% increase in people downloading the ebook. In fact, it also led to a valid increase in every key metric: opens, clicks, and conversions (see below).
While we have clearly seen the power of being human and personal in email marketing, this experiment shows us that our subscribers are looking at their inbox and engaging with emails that they consider to be important, helpful, and valuable. As a result, our email envelope and copy need to clearly convey the value of the content we're sharing.
7.1% increase in email opens (99% LOC)
 	32.4% increase in clicks (99% LOC)

experiments.title: How adding a personalized call-out for recurring giving impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding a handwritten note that called out the benefit of making a recurring gift, improve conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/25/2020 - 09/10/2020
Focus on the Family offers one-time and recurring giving on their primary donation page. In an effort to increase recurring gifts, we hypothesized that adding a personalized call-out around recurring that communicate the value proposition might increase conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The handwritten note decreased overall giving by 14%. The most significant decrease was seen on desktop devices with a 22% decrease. That said, mobile devices actually increased giving by 18% (88% LoC).

Ultimately, the personal call-out added friction to the conversion pathway and negatively effected overall revenue by 13%. Continued testing is needed around this to see if we can highlight recurring giving in a different way and improve the number of recurring donors we get from the primary donation page.

experiments.title: How showing the impact of a donor’s gift on each array button impacted revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that showing the impact on the gift array buttons for prospective donors will achieve increase revenue.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/22/2021 - 01/28/2021
The Canadian Taxpayer Federation recently launched a petition and in an attempt to improve revenue. The donation page outlined how a gift would be used to promote the petition to others so they could sign the petition, too.
We decided to experiment with showing the number of people their gift could put the petition in front of on the gift array buttons to see if doing the math for the donor would increase their generosity.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The gift array with impact was a significant improvement in revenue, driven by a +22% increase in average gift, along with a +15.3% increase in donor conversion rate (LoC: 70%).

These two metrics compounded together to drive a +40.7% increase in revenue with this approach.

This is a massive win for the organization, and where possible, pairing impact with a gift array is key to driving increased generosity for the donor.

Some other observations made from this experiment are:
Email visitors had a 47.6% increase in revenue with a 98% level of confidence.
 	Desktop visitors had a 42.6% increase in revenue with a 96% level of confidence.
 	New visitors had a 25.6% increase in revenue with an 86% level of confidence.
 	Returning visitors had a 59.3% increase in revenue with a 95% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How an offer-focused welcome series impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that focusing on promoting similar content offers for newly acquired emails in a welcome series will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/01/2020 - 11/24/2020
When The Fund for American Studies was just beginning to focus on building their online program, they began promoting a downloadable PDF resource for prospecting targets to request. They would market this offer with digital ads, which directed people to click to a landing page with an email signup form to have the resource sent to them. Finally, when they completed the registration form on the landing page, they would be directed to an instant donation page.
Initially, they did not have many content offers to provide to prospects, which forced them to initially launch with a welcome series that was focused on telling the story of the organization, or “proving the concept” of their impact by sharing story-based appeals that focused on high-profile alumni. This series also ended with a direct appeal, which was trying to get them to become a first-time donor.
Over time, the program began to build very quickly, and the organization expanded the number of available “content offers” to the market.
They wondered if moving them from the org-centered welcome series to a series that was focused on promoting more content offers like the one they had downloaded would improve their ability to produce first-time donors that had just been acquired.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a confidence level of 99.8%, we observed that the new offer-focused welcome series improved the donor conversion rate by +920%.

Beyond that, here are some additional metrics that were achieved along with this new approach during the study period:
Email Click Rate: +2.8% (LoC: 79.5%)
 	Email Open Rate: +86.1% (LoC: 100%)
 	Unsubscribe Rate: -42.0% (LoC: 100%)
 	Revenue: +1,091.4% (LoC: 99.9%)
The "control" welcome series ran for a substantially longer period of time (a year) vs. the new "treatment" (offer-focused) welcome series only ran for around 45-days.
 	The "treatment" experience overlapped the "control" experience for the time that the "treatment" experience was running.
 	We split the contacts by adding a pre-checked checkbox on the signup forms. If the box remained checked, they would go to the "control" experience, whereas if it were unchecked, those emails would go into the "treatment" experience.
 	During this time of overlapping experiences that were running, roughly 89% of the people that signed up went into the "control" experience, and only 11% went to the "treatment" experience.
 	It can also be assumed that the people that unchecked the box were presumably a less motivated audience than those that left the box checked upon signing up—why? Because they intentionally took a step to say "I don't want anything else from you" when they signed up. Even so, this much smaller and assumed to be less motivated audience outperformed the "control" experience not only for the 45-day period of time when they were running simultaneously, but also when the performance data for the "control" experience was expanded to include a year's worth of results, which is what is included in the study data above.
Why would this new welcome series be so much more effective than the control?

experiments.title: How communal language on a donation page affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that employing communal language for tribute donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/28/2021
CaringBridge had failed to validate a lift on their tribute donation page for many months—the embedded control which used cause and effect language had beaten many treatments. The team wanted to try to use language that built up the user, employing “communal language” to reduce the transactional nature of the copy in order to increase conversion and revenue.
The treatment started by thanking the donor for their support, and then expounding on how their support not only encompasses the donation that the page is asking for, but also the comments, hearts and well wishes they leave on their friend’s journal as engagement.
The call-to-action increases the autonomy of the donor by handing them control of the choice to give, or not to give.
They tested these two pages against each other to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment produced an 11.9% increase in revenue through the combination of a 5.1% increase in conversion and a 6.5% increase in average gift.

This lift in revenue is powerful because it activates two parts of the Flux Capacitor of Online Revenue Maximization—both getting more people to say yes and getting those people who do say yes to give more generously.

This shows that communal language—as opposed to transactional language—can be a powerful force in conversion. Additionally, with CaringBridge users, it suggests that giving the donor more perceived autonomy through giving them the option to give rather than the command to give can help more people say yes.

0% increase in traffic
				× 5.1% increase in conversion rate
				× 6.5% increase in average gift
									11.9% increase in revenue

experiments.title: How a “one click” donation experience impacted giving from current donors
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing friction for returning donors will achieve an increase in conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/09/2020 - 12/21/2020
During The Heritage Foundation’s year-end campaign, we wanted to streamline the process to capture gifts from active members. To reduce friction from filling out multiple form fields, and simplify the gift processing option for existing donors, we presented a “one click” experience similar to what one might experience with other online transactions as a repeat visitor, like Amazon or Paypal. Instead of the donor having to re-enter payment details, we presented them with a message indicating the last 4 digits of the most recent card they used on a past donation while still giving them the option to change or update their payment method. We also created a second treatment that allowed the donor to “swipe” the button to make a gift using their past payment details.
experiments.keyLearnings:
By acknowledging our pre-existing relationship with the donor and reducing friction for mobile visitors we saw a 122.1% increase in donations with a 100% level of confidence. Since we know the year-end giving donation page receives highly motivated traffic during the month of December, we would want to re-test this experience during other campaigns as well to compare results but given the increase in both conversion rate and revenue, we will move forward with this approach for future appeals to current members.

Notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment:
Revenue experienced a 242.9% increase with a 98% level of confidence.
 	New visitors had a 95.7% increase in donations with a 99% level of confidence.
experiments.fluxMetrics:

				gift).
				This experiment produced the following results:

				× 81.7% increase in conversion rate

experiments.title: How the order placement of the credit card fields impacts conversion on a primary donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that moving the credit card form fields later in the donation pathway for donors and prospective donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion on the page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/11/2020 - 12/03/2020
On Care Net’s primary donation page, we have been focused on optimizing the donation form in an effort to increase donor conversion. Through past research studies, and in working with many different non-profit organizations, we have found that the order in which information is acquired on the donation form varies greatly. To better understand the optimal order placement of the form fields on the page – specifically the credit card section – we decided to test it with Care Net. Care Net’s primary donation page asks for the credit card information immediately after they ask for the gift amount. This piece of information would be the second step in the donation pathway. We hypothesized that we may be able to increase donor conversion if we asked for that piece of information at the very end.
experiments.keyLearnings:
By moving the credit card section to the very end of the giving form, we increased donor conversion by 36% on desktop devices. There was little to no impact on mobile devices.

Notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment:
Revenue experienced a 112.2% increase with a 95% level of confidence.
25-34 visitors had a 700.0% increase in donations with a 98% level of confidence.

				× 35.8% increase in conversion rate

experiments.title: How a softer call-to-action might increase donor conversion during high urgency campaigns on mobile devices
experiments.researchQuestion: Can we increase donor conversion with a soft CTA of signing the Declaration for Life?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/23/2019 - 05/05/2019
Focus on the Family was running a campaign geared towards their pro-life initiative. To promote the campaign and get people to give, they featured a banner at the top of their site that asked people to give towards it. In an earlier experiment, where they test the call-to-action on the banner from a hard ask to a softer CTA, they found that it had little to no impact on donor conversion. To continue to test into this idea, they hypothesized testing a different soft CTA that would engage users, present an email acquisition opportunity, and give people the option to give. For the new experiment they tested the hard ask against asking people to sign their “Declaration for Life” pledge. They split their mobile traffic and tested this approach.
experiments.keyLearnings: The soft CTA decreased donor conversion by 53.9%. And while we were able to acquire email addresses through the declaration, the decrease in donor conversion and revenue is too great of a loss. We will continue to test into this banner on mobile devices during campaigns to try and better understand the motivation of people for these campaigns. Maybe a value-add content offer would perform better than the options we have already tested and the control which is a hard ask?

experiments.title: How a collapsed banner on a mobile device impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How would the collapsed banner impact conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/26/2018 - 07/10/2018
Focus on the Family had a high urgency campaign that gave people the opportunity to have twice the impact of their gift through a match. In analyzing their campaign revenue from this year to last year, there was a 35% decrease in revenue. They hypothsized that the decrease in revenue might be, in part, due to the campaign banner at the top of their website. On mobile devices, specifically, the campaign was initially launched with the banner collapsed. But historically, an expanded version of the banner is what they have used in the past.  In an effort to better understand if the collapsed banner was a contributing factor to the decrease in revenue, they proposed testing the expanded banner against the collapsed banner on mobile devices.
experiments.keyLearnings: We weren't able to validate the experiment off of donations, but we were able to validate it off of clicks to the donation page. The collapsed banner decreased clicks by 47%. This significant decrease in the number of people getting to the donation page can impact the number of people converting and making a gift. While we did see less donations from the collapsed banner, the total number of donations being made from the banner (expanded and collapsed) was really low. While the collapsed version did decrease the number of people getting to the donation page and had fewer gifts attached to it, there's a different element impacting the decrease in revenue overall.

experiments.title: How a custom Facebook conversion event to target recurring purchases impacted recurring donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Would a custom “recurring purchase” event type increase a campaign’s recurring donor conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/26/2020 - 09/25/2020
As a part of experimenting with Facebook’s custom conversion events, we wanted to experiment with whether the custom-defined conversion event which specifically identified and send payment details back to Facebook’s algorithm to differentiate between a single gift and a monthly recurring gift purchase type would impact the recurring donor conversion rate.
For this campaign, we partnered with Wiland to identify a custom audience of lookalike donors who are most likely (per the Wiland model) to become a recurring donor for The Heritage Foundation.
The only variable in this experiment was (a) the standard, out of the box, “purchase” event from Facebook vs. (b) the custom-defined “recurring purchase” event that we created and installed in Facebook.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 99.5% level of confidence, we observed a significant decrease of -63.9% in recurring donor conversion rate. We observed a -62% decrease in revenue (essentially, a proportionate decrease relative to the lesser quantity of donors coming through the treatment experience) with a 100% level of confidence, as well.
It should be noted that we turned this experiment off only about 72% of the way through the minimum number of transactions we would like to see in a donor conversion study of this type (and even less—about 35%—of a revenue lift study), but the volume was still high enough and the spread great enough between the control and treatment experiences that we believed it would be best to not finish the study with the target minimum number of gifts we would normally like to see.
Further experimentation may be required with the purchase event programming and audience targeting combinations, but this certainly seems to point towards a decrease in donor conversion for recurring donor-focused campaigns.

The Flux Metrics analyze the three primary metrics that affect revenue (traffic, conversion rate, and average
				gift).
				This experiment produced the following results:
0% increase in traffic
				× 63.9% decrease in conversion rate
				× 0% increase in average gift

experiments.title: How promoting a gift premium impacted donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Would expanding the value proposition of the incentive increase donors at least at the qualifying gift level without negatively impacting the overall donor conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/02/2020
The Canadian Taxpayer Federation offers donors at the $100 gift level a print magazine called “Taxpayer Magazine” as a premium (or incentive) to give at or above that level. We wondered whether or not expanding the value proposition of the incentive would increase donors at or above that level, without negatively impacting the overall donor conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 98.1% level of confidence, we observed an overall donor conversion rate decrease of -44.8% for the treatment.
Unfortunately, expanding the value proposition of the incentive offer did decrease the donor conversion rate overall. However, we did observe a valid increase (98.6% level of confidence) of +196% at the $100+ gift level (which the premium was offered at).
Even with this increase of donors at a much higher average gift level—we did observe a near-directional decrease in revenue of -21% (the level of confidence was only 78%).
Because of the revenue drop overall, the organization decided to take the higher volume of donors (and overall higher revenue total) of the control experience, rather than the fewer (but more valuable) donors through the treatment experience.
In reviewing the treatment and results, our hypothesis is that the overall donor conversion rate was down for the treatment because donors who weren't capable of giving at that level felt that their gift wasn't valued if not at that level.
Perhaps finding another way to expand the value proposition of Taxpayer Magazine could keep the overall donor conversion rate while also increasing the rate of $100+ gift donors that are capable of giving at that level.

experiments.title: How removing branding and social proof to simplify a petition impacted donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does removing branding (and the corresponding social proof) impact email signup, donor conversion or revenue rates?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/20/2020
As a part of optimizing petitions used for the acquisition of new activists and donors, the Canadian Taxpayer Federation looked to experiment with the branding, which also included a social proof element, from the petition signing page. How would this impact email signup rate, and downstream donor and revenue metrics?
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 98.3% level of confidence, we observed a decrease in instant donor conversion rate of 65.7% when the organization’s logo and the corresponding social proof was removed from the top of the petition signature page.
There are two other interesting observations, which are:
The email signup rate slightly increased — showing a +2.8% (LoC: 96.7%), but the decrease in subsequent donor conversion rate also drove a …
Decrease in revenue of -60.6% (LoC: 95.4%).
Could the organization’s branding and social proof (showing the size of the community they would be joining if they made a gift on the subsequent donation page after signing the petition) decrease their likelihood to give a gift at all?
Ultimately, we decided to turn off the experiment because the increase in email signup rates did not jump high enough to offset the big decreases we witnessed in donor conversion and revenue.
Further experimentation may be required, but it certainly appears that when you have social proof to publicize, that it is far more important to publicize it all of the way through the funnel instead of removing it, as it appears to have negatively impacted subsequent giving.

experiments.title: How a smaller recurring gift ask impacts overall revenue on article pages
experiments.researchQuestion: Would a smaller recurring gift ask convert more donors and result in more overall revenue on articles pages?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 09/29/2020
Focus on the Family gets significant traffic to their article pages on their website. During a special campaign they were running, they wanted to see if they could convert some of this traffic into donors. Instead of just placing a one-time donation ask, we wanted to see if that was the best option for giving on the page and for this campaign. To test into this, we developed a slide up feature that would appear on the page after a few seconds. The control presented the one-time donation ask and the control presented the recurring ask. The gift handle for the one-time ask was $60, and the suggested gift handle for the recurring ask was $5/month ($60 for the year). Would giving a one-time gift of $60 appeal to more people, or would giving that same amount, but through a monthly gift at $5/month appeal to more people and result in more overall revenue for the organization?
experiments.keyLearnings:
While we had less initial transactions with the recurring gift ask, the overall revenue impact was significantly more than the one-time gift ask. When annualized, the recurring gift ask increased overall revenue by 75%! What is also interesting is that the average gift for the recurring gift ask was $19 when the suggested gift ask on the article pages was $5. The reason for this is that when people got to the donation page, the gift array for the recurring gift ask started at $15. We were initially concerned that we'd see the majority of the recurring gifts come in at the $5 handle, but to our surprise, only 20% of all recurring gifts were at that $5 amount. Having the donation page gift arrays start at $15 and not $5 was a critical move in ensuring we didn't get a high volume of low gift recurring donors. The $5 recurring gift was a great initial "hook" for the donor, but we learned that we can actually get donors to commit to a higher gift amount after they've made the initial decision to make a gift.
Even though there was a 34% decrease in the number of people making a gift between the one-time feature and the recurring gift feature, the recurring gift ask proves to be the best option for the organization since the annualized revenue is significantly more.

				gift).
				This experiment produced the following results:
0% increase in traffic
				× 624.2% increase in conversion rate
				× 75.8% decrease in average gift
									75.1% increase in revenue

experiments.title: How additional cultivation impacts online giving
experiments.researchQuestion: Will an increase in cultivation emails have any impact on a donor’s likelihood to give?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/12/2019 - 10/12/2019
During a data analysis performed for ADF at the beginning of the calendar year, we discovered a handful of trends related to the email file that were concerning:
3 out of 4 ADF subscribers had not opened an email within the last six months
New and existing donors were twice as likely to disengage as non-donor subscribers
ADF was sending twice as many solicitation emails as it was cultivation and stewardship-focused emails. Simply put: the subscribers were being asked twice as much as they were given back to.
While the digital fundraising program had been growing, these trends illustrated a potential problem in the near future if they were not corrected. The historical success of ADF’s online fundraising was closely correlated to the size and engagement of the email file.
Given the ambitious fundraising goals, we couldn’t reduce the number of solicitations. So, instead, we decided to test out an additional cultivation series that would increase the number of emails sent to the file. The additional emails would focus on educating and feeding back into the various segments. There would be no additional solicitations.
At a tactical level, this email series consisted of simply-designed emails sent each Friday by one of the authors on the blog (Sarah K). They would include a link to a blog post or a video and we emphasized getting replies to the email.
To validate the impact of this series, we split the email file in half, paying special attention to ensure that there was equal representation of active, lapsed and non-donors and that the average revenue donor was similar between the splits.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running for a full six months, we observed a statistically significant 13.1% increase in the likelihood for a donor to give a gift online. Combined with the 25% increase in average gift, this resulted in a 41.5% increase in online revenue from the donors.
If this cultivation series were rolled out to the entire file and run for an entire year, the projected impact would be an additional $262K raised online without sending any additional solicitations. We also observed a 43% increase in online engagement for all segments.
While the emphasis of this experiment was to measure the impact of online giving and engagement, we also observed several other trends:
The impact of the cultivation did not fade over time. We observed the same statistically significant increase in likelihood to give and revenue when we compared the first three months of giving to the second three months. The additional cultivation did not significantly impact a donor's likelihood to give across all channels but we did see an increase in the amount they gave by 30% regardless of channel (with a 99% level of confidence.) It should be noted that this lift did not reach the required sample size so this should be considered a directional lift.  The additional cultivation did not increase the likelihood of a non-donor to make their first gift. However, it did increase their average revenue per donor by 50% in the 6 month period.
* For all of the statistics above, we excluded both gifts above $10K and all recurring gifts. When we included these additional classifications of gifts, we observed a 14% increase in revenue for a total of $89.7K additional revenue for the treatment segment.

									41.5% increase in revenue

experiments.title: How going direct to a petition impacted email registration rates
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a simplified funnel, which removes the first step (a quiz) from the funnel improve email registration rates?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/24/2019 - 05/22/2019
Americans for Prosperity launched a quiz that led to a petition, which led to an instant donation page. We found that although the quiz was acquiring emails, that people were dropping off at the point when we asked them to sign the petition, which meant that visitors to the instant donation page were really low. In an attempt to boost both email registration and instant donation pageviews, we wanted to experiment removing the quiz from the process and directly promoting the petition as the first step.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 99.9% level of confidence, we were able to lift email acquisition rates by cutting the quiz from the process and directly promoting the petition by +127.7%.
Subsequently, we did see directional increases in both donor conversions (+151%, LoC: 54%) and revenue (+151%, Loc: 77%), but neither statistic validated through the experiment, since so few gifts ultimately were secured through the offer.
Although the original thought was that the quiz would build momentum towards petition signatures, which could crescendo into generating instant donors and dollars, in practice we found that a more simplified two-step approach was confirmed to produce more emails, and directionally more donors and dollars.

experiments.title: How the purchase conversion pixel impacts donor acquisition
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: What is the impact of Facebook’s purchase conversion on conversion and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/13/2019 - 03/13/2019
The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate launched a Facebook campaign to promote an offer called Lenten Prayers. Historically, the campaigns have been optimized for email conversion using a custom conversion in Facebook. In recent months, Facebook released a new Purchase pixel conversion that allows you to optimize the campaign off of people who donate. We tested this new approach against the name conversion optimization.
experiments.keyLearnings: By using Facebook's purchase conversion, we were able to significantly impact instant donor conversion. In fact, this conversion event increased instant donor conversion by 284.6%! The results of this experiment dramatically changed the method in which we view and execute paid acquisition campaigns moving forward. The purchase conversion allows Facebook to pull in additional purchase data to optimize the campaign instead of relying on a singular conversion metric that we tell it to do.

experiments.title: How ordering the steps of the initial step of a donation process affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How ordering the steps of the initial step of a donation process affect conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/17/2020 - 09/25/2020
CaringBridge’s tribute widget generated the majority of their donations by asking users to make a gift, then making the first step of that gift be a message to go to their friend along with their gift. This widget, in the CaringBridge site, sent tons of traffic to the donation page—but the first step was a simple comment box. The team wondered if their familiarity bias might be keeping them from understanding if users really knew how the widget worked, and what they were supposed to do. Was it clear enough? They created a treatment that numbered the steps: Step 1: Leave a message for KellyStep 2 (on the donation button): Submit your message and complete your donation to CaringBridge.
They hypothesized that breaking these steps down and ordering them would increase donor conversion through this funnel.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment, with the ordered steps, increased revenue by 21.9%. This revealed a hidden secret: it may not have been clear to donors exactly what they were supposed to do with the tribute widget, regardless of how familiar it was to the CaringBridge team. This presents a host of opportunities to look at various elements of the CaringBridge site and perform more optimization experiments specifically centered around increasing clarity on "how this works".
Of particular notice in this experiment:
Mobile devices had a much higher than average lift at 28%New visitors had a 45% increase in conversion rate

experiments.title: How personalization and brand reduction affect conversion on a post-survey donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: How will personalization and brand reduction affect conversion on a post-survey donation page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/14/2020 - 10/17/2020
Hillsdale College had run an experiment during the pre-launch of their Introduction to Western Philosophy course that tested two variables independently. First, they tested using personalization in the headline of the “instant” donation page to increase the buy-in of the registrant and get them reading the page. Secondly, they noticed that the donation page had many mentions of Hillsdale College, which seemed redundant and seemed to get the brand “in the way” of the relationship that the copy was attempting to develop with the donor. They tested a separate treatment that did NOT include the personalization but did reduce the mentions of the brand, substituting pronouns where appropriate. These two treatments both appeared to lift revenue, 19% and 13% respectively. They wanted to re-test this, and combine the elements into one treatment. They chose their Electoral College Survey, developed the treatment, and launched a test.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment, which combined the personalization and the reduced brand mention, increased revenue by 13.4%. This reinforces the learning from the initial experiment, and shows that a similar lift in revenue holds for a treatment on a different offer, especially when combining the treatments.
This experiment results suggests that:
Personalization has a statistically significant impact on conversion. Reducing the mentions of the brand on the donation page has a statistically significant impact on conversion. Both elements combined contribute to a lift in average gift and revenue per visitor.
