									109.5% increase in revenue

experiments.title: How focusing on the product-level value proposition affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Which value proposition is most appealing to visitors from the Daily Signal?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/17/2015 - 08/05/2015
The Heritage Foundation operates a political news organization called the Daily Signal. Part of the site has links back to the Heritage Foundation properties include a donation link found at the bottom of the page.
Since the donors coming from the site may not be as familiar with The Heritage Foundation, we wanted to discover what value proposition would be most appealing to the donors. We decided to create two treatments, one that targeted the primary value proposition of giving to The Heritage Foundation and the other that focused on a product-level value proposition of giving to the Daily Signal.
experiments.keyLearnings: By focusing the messaging on the value of the Daily Signal, we were able to better align with the visitors’ motivations. We were also able to keep visual congruence by changing the header of the page to reflect the branding of the Daily Signal. All of these adjustments led to a lift in revenue by 172.8%.

experiments.title: How changing the landing page design affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Which landing page design produces the most donor conversions and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/09/2015 - 06/23/2015
The Heritage Foundation had been using the same landing page design for their search engine marketing (SEM) donor campaigns for the past three years. During those three years, we had run multiple experiments on the main organizational donation page which had gradually evolved into something that looked completely different.
We were in the process of moving the SEM page to the same domain as the main donation form and realized that it would be a good time to validate that the old design was still effective for the SEM audience segment.  We created an experiment by duplicating the page content but displaying it on the two different page designs.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The new design treatment caused an 85.1% drop in revenue by decreasing conversion rate by 67.6% and decreasing average gift by 54.1%. The simplified design used in the treatment was developed for a page with minimal copy. When we added the long value proposition text that had been developed for this specific landing page, it became difficult to read.
Landing pages must be designed so that they are easily scannable by the visitor. In this situation, the sub-headlines didn’t stand out enough and the lack of page border made the page seem longer than it was.  All of these elements caused enough friction in the mind of the donor to have a negative impact on both conversion rate and average gift.

experiments.title: How adding incentive and removing donation form friction affects conversion

rows.name: Form with incentive and no dividers

experiments.researchQuestion: Does a lower initial donation ask in the gift array and added incentive improve the overall visitor conversion and revenue of the main donation page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/27/2015 - 05/27/2015
On Hillsdale College’s main donation form, we had done previous experiments to lift conversion rate 22% by modifying the format of the donation form and adding messaging around what we believed is both unique and appealing about Hillsdale College to potential donors. In an effort to continue optimizing this high traffic page, we shifted our focus to reducing friction and adding incentive to the gift.
We reduced friction by adding a lower starting donation amount to the gift array with the belief that a larger ask may make the donors feel a smaller gift would not be as impactful.  We also removed dividers between each section of the form to help move visitors down the page.  For incentive, we added the existing offer to receive a brick in the College’s Liberty Walk for a gift of $500 or more.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The new donation form treatment was able to produce a 32.5% increase in conversion and a lift to average gift of 13.8%. We noticed was that, despite adding the incentive for a $500+ gift, we didn’t see any donors give at or above that level so the incentive did not seem to have much impact on the average gift.

Our initial concern was that adding the $25 option to the gift array would lower the average gift enough to negate any increases in conversion. This proved to be false.  The lower gift was able to increase the conversion rate and it did not seem to make donors give less.

experiments.title: How adding membership level indicators affects membership renewal
experiments.researchQuestion: Does adding language on the donation form that informs donors what member level they qualify for and then telling them the amount needed to move to the next level increase the average revenue per visitor compared to the standard donation form?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/07/2015 - 01/08/2015
The Heritage Foundation has 12 different levels of membership that come with incremental benefits as the donors give me. The standard donation form uses member language but does not detail what level their gift qualifies them for. During their annual membership renewal campaign, we wanted to find a way to let donors know their membership level based upon their giving amount while finding ways to upgrade them.
We devised a donation form that would display what level their current gift qualifies them form and what it would take to upgrade to the next level.  We made sure to include what benefits would come with the next level in membership.  Before rolling it out, we wanted to validate that it would improve the results of the campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The membership language treatment was able to lift the average gift of the donor by 18.6% but it negatively affected the conversion rate by 34.5%. This negative effect on conversion was enough to adversely effect the overall revenue of the form.
Upon digging deeper into the data, we discovered that the language would lift the average gift for donors above $50 but would cause lower level donors, those below $25, to not give at all. We may test membership language for future campaigns but limit the language so that only the larger donors see the language.

									22.3% decrease in revenue

experiments.title: How replacing 3rd party credibility via social validation affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Showing recent donors will add to the credibility of the previously stated value proposition and will foster a herd mentality within the donor’s mind. This will increase conversion rate compared to other 3rd party credibility indicators
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/16/2014 - 12/26/2014
As part of most campaign landing pages, Heritage has included 3rd party credibility indicators (such as external quotes and media appearances) as a way to reduce anxiety. However, we thought it might be possible to make it more relevant if the 3rd party credibility came from actual donors instead of well known sources. As such, we developed an embeddable widget that would scroll through recent donors.
experiments.keyLearnings: Seeing recent donors did not carry as much weight as quotes and appearances in well known sources. In addition to using the 3rd party credibility indicators near the donation form, we will also include these facts in the copy and throughout the page design where appropriate.

experiments.title: How testing the call-to-action on thank you page affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Which call-to-action will produce the greatest amount of revenue in the long term?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/19/2014 - 12/08/2014
With the number of donors each month, the thank you page on the primary Heritage donation form offered an opportunity for testing. In an effort to further capitalize on the donor’s motivation, we created two follow-on calls-to-action to either become a recurring monthly donor or to give a gift to their sister organization, Heritage Action.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The follow-on call-to-action of giving to Heritage Action produced significantly more gifts and additional revenue beyond what would be provided by the monthly donors. The revenue given in the Heritage Action donation even out performed the recurring donor revenue when the recurring donor gifts were multiplied to account for the recurring nature of the gift. The donors saw Heritage Action as another way to make their gift go further which carried more value in their mind.
This follow-on call-to-action will be rolled out to other donation forms maintained by Heritage and it will be utilized in future campaigns for join fundraising opportunities.-

experiments.title: How shortening an email affects clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: By placing the content on the landing page and using the email to tease and get the click, can we drive additional traffic to the page which will increase the potential number of donors?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/10/2014 - 12/12/2014
Shorter copy with a soft call-to-action drove 481.4% more visitors to the site. However, both treatments had identical response rates which suggests the need to optimize future long-copy landing pages.
experiments.keyLearnings: Nonprofits always ask us: how long should our emails be? There’s no absolute right answer for every organization — but it’s something that every nonprofit should be testing. Since the goal of the email is to get the click, DTS decided to test long copy (with a donation-focused call to action in the email) against an shorter email (that made the reader click to find out more).

experiments.title: How an engagement device affects clickthrough rate versus a direct ask
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a survey ask instead of a direct donation ask increase clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/01/2016 - 08/29/2016
Hillsdale College wanted to send a digital follow-up to a direct mail appeal to a segment of their donors who had both a mailing address and an email address on file. These “chase” appeals had generated a significant amount of revenue in the past, but they wanted to try a new approach.
Previously, these emails just mirrored the direct mail package and made a direct ask for a donation. However, the direct mail package for their August appeal included a survey. They hypothesized that they could get better engagement by asking the donors to take a survey (that led to a donation) rather than putting a direct ask in the email. Ultimately, they hypothesized that this increase in clickthrough rate would result in more people reaching the donation page, and higher donor conversion. But first, they needed to get more clicks.
They recreated the survey online, split their email file, and send one segment a direct ask and the other a link to take a survey.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The survey, as expected, delivered a 693.1% increase in clickthrough rate. A soft ask almost always delivers more clicks than a direct donation ask, because the motivations are entirely different.
However, more clicks doesn’t always mean more donations, since people aren’t specifically clicking to make a donation. That warranted another test to see how the survey affected donor conversion.
experiments.fluxMetrics:
The Flux Metrics analyze the three primary metrics that affect revenue (traffic, conversion rate, and average
				gift).

experiments.title: How an engagement device affects donor conversion versus a direct ask
experiments.researchQuestion: Will an engagement device (in this case, a survey) increase donor response and attract more donations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/16/2016 - 08/29/2016
Hillsdale College wanted to send a digital follow-up to a direct mail appeal to a segment of their donors who had both a mailing address and an email address on file. These “chase” appeals had generated a significant amount of revenue in the past, but they wanted to try a new approach.
Previously, these emails just mirrored the direct mail package and made a direct ask for a donation. However, the direct mail package for their August appeal included a survey. They hypothesized that they could get better engagement by asking the donors to take a survey (that led to a donation) rather than putting a direct ask in the email.
They recreated the survey online, split their email file, and send one segment a direct ask and the other a link to take a survey.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The survey produced a 126% lift in donations by attracting more recipients to start the donation process.
The survey email generated a 688% increase in clickthrough rate, although the Hillsdale team knew from experience that the survey ask was a far different motivation than the donor ask. However, this massive increase in clicks from the email got more people to start the process. Even after people fell out of the “funnel” as they went through the survey, the increase in traffic was significant enough to produce more than twice as many donations.
This inspired future guidance for chase emails. Although a survey every month would not be a sustainable strategy, using different engagement devices to get more people beginning the donation process proved to be a successful tactic.
experiments.fluxMetrics:
The Flux Metrics analyze the three primary metrics that affect revenue (traffic, conversion rate, and average
				gift).
				This experiment produced the following results:
0% increase in traffic
				× 126.1% increase in conversion rate
				× 0% increase in average gift

experiments.title: How a radical donation page redesign affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a radical redesign that removes friction and strengthens and emphasizes the value proposition increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/22/2017 - 08/30/2017
Harvest Ministries had a donation page that produced above-average conversion rates (at nearly 20%). However, they viewed this as an 80% failure rate, and wanted to run an experiment to optimize for donor conversion.
Their control donation page kept the same template as their website (which is common for ministries and nonprofits). This template included navigation that gave many opportunities to leave the page and take other actions. The page copy also started with a premium ask, with little value proposition language to hook the visitor.
The donation form started with radio buttons, which presented possible usability friction for the visitor. The prospective donor was then presented with the choice to make a single gift or a recurring gift, the details of which were located on another page. This introduced possible decision friction into the funnel. Next was a designation. Designations can be helpful for the organization, but research has showed that they present another decision for the user and can bring the donation process to a halt.
Next, the visitor was asked to claim their premium, and fill out the typical information and payment fields, with a question about where they engage with Harvest in the middle.
The treatment removed all the excess navigation from the page and began with a simple, clear value proposition: “Your gift helps reach the lost with the gospel of Jesus Christ”. It then unpacked the value proposition, providing evidentials to each claim of value and connecting the gift to different forms of impact. Then, it repositioned the premium as a thank-you in response to the gift, rather than the reason for the gift.
The gift array was reworked into larger buttons that were easier to click and see which amount was selected. The information and payment fields were moved to the second step of a two-step screen.
Harvest launched an A/B test to discover which donation page flow resulted in more donations and revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment page generated a 44.8% lift in donations — a big feat for a page that was already attracting highly motivated donors. This showed that some combination of all the elements of friction on the page were distracting would-be donors. It also proved that focusing on the clarity of the value proposition (rather than the incentive alone) can increase motivation to give.
This prompted a series of tests to remove more friction from the giving process and further clarify the value proposition.
experiments.fluxMetrics:
The Flux Metrics analyze the three primary metrics that affect revenue (traffic, conversion rate, and average
				gift).
				This experiment produced the following results:
0% increase in traffic
				× 44.8% increase in conversion rate
				× 0% increase in average gift

experiments.title: The impact on one-click giving for dual ask gifts
experiments.researchQuestion: How will simplifying the giving process impact revenue and conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/16/2015 - 12/27/2015
The Heritage Foundation has a unique process where, after every online gift, the donor is immediately prompted to make a second gift to their sister organization, Heritage Action for America. All the donor has to do for this second gift is enter their donation amount since we already have their address and credit card information from the initial form. This secondary ask generally has a 5% to 8% conversion rate for donors making that second gift.
We had the opportunity to test a new feature in their giving system that allowed for one-click giving. Existing donors would only have to enter their zip code and credit card number to make a donation.  We decided to test this functionality out in their 2015 calendar year end campaign and monitor the impact on all aspects of the donation process.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Interestingly enough, the one-click giving process did not have any statistically significant impact on the initial donor conversion. Our hypothesis is that the decreased friction that came from the simplified form was balanced out but the confusion the process caused. While it was a simpler process; it was different than what the donors were use to.
However, while the immediate conversion was not impacted, the follow on donation opportunity saw a 90.4% decrease in conversion with the one-click giving. Since the donors did not have to enter their address or credit card for the first form, they would have to enter it for the second form (due to system limitations). This added significant friction to the second step in the process that was enough to significantly impact the gifts made to Heritage Action.
experiments.fluxMetrics:
The Flux Metrics analyze the three primary metrics that affect revenue (traffic, conversion rate, and average
				gift).
				This experiment produced the following results:
0% increase in traffic
				× 90.4% decrease in conversion rate
				× 17.4% decrease in average gift
									92.1% decrease in revenue

experiments.title: How a dynamic goal thermometer on a high urgency donation page affects revenue and conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that replacing a single, static $500,000 campaign thermometer with a dynamic thermometer that resets in $5,000 increments will achieve an increase in revenue and conversion rates because smaller, frequently achievable goals make progress feel more attainable, reinforce donor impact, and create a stronger sense of momentum and urgency during the year-end giving period.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/18/2025
This experiment took place during The Heritage Foundation’s annual calendar year-end fundraising campaign, one of their most important giving periods of the year. The campaign had a total goal of $500,000, and the primary donation experience included a progress thermometer meant to signal momentum and urgency to donors as they considered giving.
Because year-end traffic is high and donors are especially motivated by deadlines and progress cues, this was an ideal moment to test whether an alternative framing of the goal and progress indicator could increase motivation and perceived impact.
The test was run directly on the campaign donation page.
Control: The standard donation page displayed a traditional progress thermometer showing the total amount raised toward the fixed $500,000 campaign goal.
Treatment: The donation page used a dynamic thermometer that “reset” once a smaller goal was reached, increasing by $5,000 increments each time the goal was met. This created the perception of frequent wins and ongoing momentum rather than a single, distant finish line.
Both versions were otherwise consistent, ensuring the thermometer behavior was the primary variable being tested.
The motivation for this experiment came from a behavioral insight: large, distant goals can feel abstract and discouraging, especially late in a campaign when progress may slow. In contrast, smaller, rapidly achievable goals can increase donor confidence, urgency, and the sense that their gift meaningfully contributes to immediate success.
We wanted to test whether breaking a large year-end goal into visible, recurring milestones would:
Make progress feel more attainable
Increase perceived donor impact
Sustain momentum throughout the campaign rather than only near the final deadline
This experiment was designed to evaluate whether a dynamic, milestone-based progress indicator could outperform a traditional static thermometer during a high-stakes year-end fundraising push.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The most significant takeaway from this experiment was that the dynamic, milestone-based thermometer meaningfully increased revenue, even without a corresponding increase in transactions. The treatment generated a 30% lift in revenue, with an 89.7% level of confidence, indicating a strong and practically meaningful impact on total dollars raised.
At the same time, transactions decreased by 10.8%, though this result came with only 48% confidence, suggesting the decline is not statistically reliable and may be due to natural variance rather than a true negative effect. Taken together, these results indicate that the revenue lift was driven primarily by higher average gift amounts, not by more donors completing gifts.
This suggests that the dynamic thermometer helped donors feel more confident making larger contributions by reinforcing momentum and the sense that their gift could meaningfully “complete” a near-term goal.
Moving forward, this experiment reinforces the value of breaking large fundraising goals into visible, attainable milestones, especially during high-pressure moments like year-end campaigns. Rather than relying solely on a single, long-term progress bar, future donation experiences can leverage smaller, frequently achieved goals to sustain momentum and encourage generosity.
Specifically, we will:
Continue testing dynamic or segmented goal framing in high-volume fundraising momentsPair milestone-based thermometers with strategic ask array testing to better understand how goal framing influences gift sizeBe cautious about interpreting transaction volume changes when confidence is low, focusing instead on revenue-per-visitor and average gift as primary success metrics
Overall, this test demonstrates that optimizing how progress is framed can materially impact fundraising outcomes—even when surface-level conversion metrics appear flat or mixed.

experiments.title: How a sliding bar on a campaign donation page allowing donors to adjust their impact influences conversion rate and revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that allowing donors to select the level of their impact dynamically will increase donor conversion rate by increasing the perceived clarity and appeal of the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/09/2024
IMB is running a direct ask offer where people can give to fund Bibles, but we wanted to increase the tangibility and clarity of the donor’s impact on the campaign’s donation page. We hypothesized that allowing the donors to drag a slider that dynamically changes the number of people reached would increase the clarity of the offer when compared with the current experience.
We used VWO to create a graphical, interactive slider on the page that dynamically changed the gift amount and the corresponding number of people the gift would reach. We split traffic 50/50 and all campaign sources were directed to the test.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the dynamically changing slider for donors to select the level of their impact did not increase donor conversion rate as hypothesized. In fact, the treatment group experienced a 35.8% decrease in donations compared to the control group.

While the results are approaching validity, with a statistical level of confidence of 93.8%, further testing would be necessary to confirm these findings.

In future experimentation, it is important to carefully consider the impact of any changes made to the donation page. It may be beneficial to conduct more qualitative research or user testing before implementing a new feature to ensure it resonates with donors and effectively communicates the impact of their contribution. Additionally, testing smaller changes or variations of the dynamically changing slider could help pinpoint what specifically led to the decrease in donations in this experiment.

experiments.title: How a simplified campaign Challenge Grant goal impacts overall revenue and average gift
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that simplifying the campaign challenge grant goal for Human Coalition housefile will achieve an increase in donor conversion and revenue because it eliminates potential confusion and instead focuses on highlighting the impact of the challenge grant.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/17/2024
For Human Coalition’s Fiscal Year End campaign, readers were presented with two goals: the overall goal of $703,875 as well as the challenge grant goal of $352,000. In reality, these two goals represent the same number. $703,875 is the total of what would be raised if the challenge grant goal was met. We hypothesized that presenting the user with both could cause unnecessary confusion and friction. We believe that by simplifying this goal in the treatment, to only highlight the challenge grant as the main priority will help improve donor conversion and revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that simplifying the campaign challenge grant goal for Human Coalition by focusing solely on the $352,000 goal led to a significant increase in donor conversion and revenue. By eliminating potential confusion caused by presenting two goals, the treatment version saw a 124.8% increase in revenue for all traffic.
Our hypothesis is that by simplifying the ask, and focusing the reader's attention on the doubled impact of the challenge grant, users were more likely to make a larger gift knowing.
In future experimentation, it is important to continue testing this simplified approach to determine its consistent impact on donor behavior. Further testing with larger sample sizes and variations in messaging could help strengthen the validity of these findings. Additionally, applying this learning to other campaigns within Human Coalition and potentially extending it to other similar nonprofit organizations could help optimize fundraising efforts and improve overall outcomes.

experiments.title: How autonomy in an email impacts revenue and donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that utilizing an autonomy technique for email subscribers will achieve higher revenue due to an increase in gifts and average gift because the donor is making the decision themselves.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/08/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas.
During June and December, PTV conducts matching campaigns to increase incentive for donors and prospective donors.
For the final email in the June campaign, we decided to test autonomy to see if that approach would encourage more gifts and higher gifts from donors
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment on utilizing autonomy in email campaigns for Pathway to Victory's matching campaigns are:

The treatment group, which received emails with an autonomy technique, experienced a 34.0% increase in revenue for all traffic compared to the control group. This includes a 16% in average gift and 15% increase in donor conversion.

Two gifts were removed from this experiment as they were viewed as outliers. A $1,200 gift from the control and $5,000 from the treatment.

experiments.title: How reducing the length of copy and increasing urgency impacted revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by shortening copy and increasing urgency for email subscribers will achieve a higher conversion rate and revenue because the ask is stronger and less copy to digest.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/05/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas.
During June and December, PTV conducts matching challenges in order to increase donor conversion.
For the next to last email during their June match campaign we decided to test shortening the email and increasing urgency to see if we could get more donations and revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that shortening the email copy and increasing urgency can lead to a significant increase in revenue. The treatment group saw a 38.6% increase in revenue for all traffic, which is a promising result.

In future experimentation, we should continue to test the effectiveness of shorter copy and increased urgency in driving conversions. Further testing with larger sample sizes and different variations of the email content could help confirm the initial findings and determine the best approach for maximizing donations and revenue during matching challenges. Additionally, we should strive to achieve a higher level of statistical confidence to ensure the validity of the results before making any significant changes to our email strategy.

experiments.title: How including a gift handle with the corresponding impact described in a fundraising email affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including gift handles with the corresponding impact described for potential donors to Eagle Lake will achieve more revenue because we’re providing clear, tangible examples of how different donation amounts will be used .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/22/2024
The Navigators are running their bi-annual Eagle Lake Campaign to raise money for this kids’ camp that operates every summer. In the kickoff email of this campaign, they wanted to test how including gift handles in the email would affect donor conversion rate. Each gift amount described what that amount would provide for Eagle Lake.
This experiment idea came from their direct mail piece which included “tickets” for people to purchase. Each ticket was for a different amount (same as the email) and described what each level of ticket would provide for the kids at Eagle Lake.
To keep the language consistent between the email and the donation page, the treatment donation page also had the gift handles in the body copy.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that including gift handles with the corresponding impact described for potential donors to Eagle Lake can significantly increase revenue. The treatment group saw a 40.2% increase in revenue for all traffic, which indicates that providing clear, tangible examples of how different donation amounts will be used can motivate donors to give more.
However, it's important to note that the statistical level of confidence was only 82.0%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 85%. This means that while the results show a positive impact, they are not completely reliable and should not be used for future decision making.
In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the inclusion of gift handles and impact descriptions in donation appeals to see if the results can be replicated and validated. Additionally, it may be helpful to investigate other factors that could influence donor behavior and revenue generation to further optimize fundraising efforts for Eagle Lake.

experiments.title: Homepage “Regular” Wiki vs Autonomy Wiki v2
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a decision point to make an impact for people who land on the homepage during a HUC will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the donor has complete autonomy to click “yes” and participate or “no” and close out of the ask because the donor has complete autonomy to click “yes” and participate or “no” and close out of the ask.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/15/2024 - 03/21/2024
For our first test in this series we wondered if adding a question and a simple decision to make an impact increase donor conversion on the homepage campaign wiki banner? By adding a level of autonomy to the donor experience and giving them “control” to say “Yes, I’d like to make an impact” or “No, I’m not interested today,” would we invite more participation in the campaign and insight additional donor conversions over our “regular” campaign messaging with a gift array? You can see the results of the first test here.
For this test, the second in the series, we wanted to see if we created the autonomy treatment that’s more closely aligned with the control, will more donors say “yes” to help give families the resources they need?
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:
1. The treatment, which involved a decision point for donors to make an impact, resulted in a 64.6% increase in Revenue for All Traffic.
2. The statistical level of confidence for this increase was 84.5%, which is below the desired threshold of 95% for validity.
3. While the results suggest that the treatment was more effective in driving donor conversions, the lack of statistical significance means that these results should not be used for future decision making

experiments.title: How a high contrast donate button in the main navigation of a website affects conversion and revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a high contrast donate button to the main navigation for heritage.org visitors will achieve a lift in transactions and revenue because it helps website visitors visual interpret the webpages hiearchy of purpose and breaks down their decision process to decide to give a gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/05/2024 - 03/07/2024
After successfully testing the addition of a red donate button in the main navigation on the homepage of heritage.org we decided to run the test side wide to Heritage.org visitors in a 50/50 split. The test excluded the homepage from results. This experiment validated several times and ran for over a month, but for logistical reasons it was kept running. While there was lift among all website visitor types, (Email, Social, Search, Returning, and NEw Visitors, etc), the largest lift came from organic traffic to the home page, which created a 55% lift in transactions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. Adding a high contrast donate button to the main navigation for heritage.org visitors resulted in a significant increase in revenue for all traffic, specifically a 24.9% increase. This indicates that the addition of the donate button had a positive impact on user engagement and conversion rates.

2. The treatment also led to a 55% lift in transactions from organic traffic to the home page, showing that the donate button was particularly effective in capturing the attention of visitors coming through this channel.

3. Although the results showed a high level of statistical confidence (85.2%), further testing is recommended to ensure the validity of the findings. The statistical confidence level of 95% was not reached in this experiment.

4. It is important to note that the treatment resulted in a 4% increase in bounce rate. While this may be a concern, it could also indicate that the donate button led to more engaged visitors who explored the website further before exiting.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to conduct additional tests to validate the impact of the donate button on revenue and transaction rates. It may also be worth exploring different variations of the donate button, such as varying the placement or color, to determine the most effective design for encouraging donations.

Overall, the results of this experiment suggest that the addition of a prominent donate button to the main navigation can potentially improve user engagement and increase revenue for Heritage Foundation.

experiments.title: How a direct appeal in a dear reader affects conversion and revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a direct donation appeal on Dear Reader Ads for daily signal readers will achieve a decrease in traffic and revenue to the content offer page and subsequent donation page because traffic to Daily Signal is motivated more for information and research and less towards making an impact through giving.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/16/2024 - 02/22/2024
We tested a Founders’ Day donation request vs. an eBook Guide to the Constitution offer. Previous testing had shown that the Guide offer outperformed a direct ask during a special campaign, so we wanted to lay to rest whether we should run content offers or direct solicitations on TDS. We utilized “less than 1%” language that had been boosting results in other acquisition funnels. The campaign quickly validated in favor of the control 73% more clicks and 60 more transactions. We will use these results to inform future testing on TDS with Dear Reader Ads and no longer include direct appeals in campaign assets.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment show that adding a direct donation appeal on Dear Reader Ads for daily signal readers resulted in a significant decrease in transactions for all traffic, indicating that the control (eBook Guide to the Constitution offer) outperformed the treatment (direct donation appeal). The statistical level of confidence was at 88.4%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95% for validity, but it is approaching validity.

In future experimentation, it would be advisable to continue testing the impact of content offers versus direct donation appeals on Dear Reader Ads. Since the initial results indicate that the content offer performed better in this case, it might be beneficial to focus on providing valuable content to Daily Signal readers rather than making direct donation appeals. Further testing could help to confirm these findings and provide insights for optimizing donation strategies on the platform.

experiments.title: How an image of the premium impacted revenue for donors
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by including an image of the book for existing donors will achieve a higher revenue because their is a visual appeal of the new book.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/30/2023
Pathway to Victory is a broadcast ministry of First Dallas.
Each month PTV promotes a new premium for the month. For this month we decided to retest using an image of the book to see if it would increase revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that including an image of the book in the promotion resulted in a significant increase in revenue for all traffic. While the statistical level of confidence is not quite at the desired 95%, the results are approaching validity, indicating that the image likely had a positive impact on donor response.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the inclusion of images in promotions to further validate the results. This could involve testing different types of images, such as a picture of the author or a graphic related to the book's content, to see if they have a similar effect on donor response.

Additionally, it would be helpful to analyze other metrics, such as click-through rates and conversion rates, to gain a more comprehensive understanding of the impact of including images in promotions. This will further inform decision-making and provide a more accurate assessment of the effectiveness of different promotional strategies.

Overall, the results of this experiment suggest that including an image of the book in promotions can have a positive impact on donor response. By continuing to test and refine this strategy, the nonprofit can potentially increase revenue and attract more donors.

experiments.title: How reducing the number of options in a gift array impacts revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that updating the gift array to match the copy callouts for Eagle Lake supporters will achieve an increase in gifts because you eliminate the friction of multiple-choice and better highlight the $150 amount called out in the page copy.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/24/2023 - 05/12/2023
For Navigators’ Eagle Lake campaign, much of the language and copy focused on how a gift helps send kids to camp. The donation page called out the amount of $150 specifically, as what’s needed to send one child to camp this year. The Navigators hypothesized that by eliminating the number of choices in the gift array to either the $150 amount or $75 (while still keeping an option for other) would increase the average gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment resulted in a 4% increase in donor conversion rate with a level of confidence of 29%. This means that the reduced number of options in the gift array had virtually no impact in people's motivation to give or not. The treatment version of the page, which only had options for $75 and $150, resulted in a 19.0% directional decrease in revenue with a level of confidence of 86%. Our hypothesis is that because the control version that included $25 and $300, there were people who were inclined to give the larger amount when presented the option.

In future experimentation, it may be beneficial to test the impact of different copy callouts and messaging on the donation page or how the gift array is presented from a design standpoint (having a preselected amount).

experiments.title: How increasing the ask amount on an instant donation page affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by increasing the “ask amount” for online course enrollees will achieve an increase in average gift and revenue for this page because there will be a larger average gift amount.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/19/2023 - 05/08/2023
This theological seminary client offered a new biblical resource, the Revelation Online Course. The control donation page for the Revelation Online Course had a $10 donation ask in the copy. The treatment page had the original control donation page copy but also included a larger gift amount ask of a $50 or more donation. This treatment page experiment was set up to show to 50% of viewers as an A/B test. We wanted to test if asking for a larger gift amount in the treatment donation page could increase the average gift and revenue for this online course.
We have noticed in past experiments that including specific donation ask copy can produce a lift in conversion rate. We thought that this donation page copy for this new biblical resource would be a great place to ask specifically for a larger donation of $50 or more.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that adding a specific ask amount of $50 or more to the donation page copy for the Revelation Online Course, did not lead to an increase in average gift and revenue. In fact, the treatment page saw a 56.1% decrease in conversion rate. While the statistical sample size is approaching validity, the level of confidence was 88% which did not reach above 95% to validate this experiment. Further testing is necessary to confirm the effectiveness of this approach. In future experimentation, it may be beneficial to explore alternative ways of encouraging larger donations, such as providing more details on the course they will get if they donate $50 or more.

experiments.title: How a lower ask amount affects revenue on a Wikipedia-style article appeal during a high-urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a lower recurring donation ask on a Wikipedia-style in-article appeal banner will achieve greater revenue by offering a lower commitment, thus increasing donations enough to offset the lower ask amount.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/16/2023 - 03/24/2023
During a high-urgency campaign focused on recurring donor acquisition, we wanted to know if lowering the ask amount on a Wikipedia-style in-article appeal would increase recurring donor rates by offering a lower commitment given that donors would be giving on a monthly basis.
This test followed on the heels of a previous experiment (during the same campaign), where we learned that the Wikipedia-style appeal tactic outperformed both a banner ad and a slide-in appeal when presented on article pages being viewed by prospective donors.
We set this up as an A/B test with roughly 50% of traffic seeing the $30 ask and roughly 50% of traffic seeing the $15 ask in the hopes that donor volume would outweigh the lower ask and provide a net increase in revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for 5 days, we were unable to validate our hypothesis that a lower ask amount would lift donations enough to overcome the lower ask and produce a net increase in revenue over an assumed 11-month period.
The treatment was only able to produce one additional recurring gift and the 48% decrease in revenue (81.4% level of confidence) did not justify continuing to run this experiment. Based on these results, we elected to run the $30 ask for the remainder of the campaign since it appeared that the decreased ask amount did little to increase the volume of donors while cutting overall revenue nearly in half.

experiments.title: How showing a reminder pop up to users that abandoned the donation process affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that showing a pop up reminder to complete a donation for people that have closed out a donation page, without making a donation will achieve a higher amount of revenue than allowing users to remember on their own.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/18/2022 - 09/30/2022
Focus on the Family wanted to see if showing a pop-up with an opportunity to complete a donation (from a previously clicked on amount), would cause users to complete their transaction…instead of abandoning their donation all-together. This experiment was based on abandoned cart functionality often seen in the e-commerce space, but seldom used in the NPO space. Their current donation platform does not natively allow for this functionality, so we built a test using the user’s last click on a donation amount (on an abandoned or closed-out donation page) to launch a pop up with that amount, asking the user to complete their transaction with a simple nudge.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment ran for 41 days. While it did not reach statistical significance (only an 87% Level of Confidence), we did have some very interesting learnings. Since we were testing for revenue lift, we wanted to be sure the data was not skewed by abnormally large donation amounts from either the control or the treatment. FOTF often gets large donations that can skew a revenue experiment if we do not normalize the data. After we ran the experiment, we created a custom Google Analytics segment that only counted donations that were less than $1k in the sample size. After doing so, we noticed that the revenue for the treatment was significantly higher - an 85.2% lift in revenue. This took the average gift of this new sample group from $75.54 to $131.86. It is to be noted that prior to removing the gifts over $1k, the control had a sample size much closer to the treatment. Did this weigh into the final results? Are the motivations different for people that come to the main site vs coming from an article page or elsewhere? WHile this test provided some exciting potential lifts, it is still unclear if motivations based on entry point could skew this data. The next step in the process will be to run the same experiment, but split into two different ones, and test if the revenue lift is better or worse when directly visiting via the main page or visiting instead through a secondary page.

experiments.title: How removing organizational language in an email appeal impacts revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing organizational language in an email appeal for prospective donors will achieve more revenue .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/09/2022
For Giving Tuesday, the IMB sent out email appeals to their housefile. In one of the emails we wanted to test how removing organizational language would impact either donor conversion rate or overall revenue. We hypothesized by replacing “the IMB” with words like “us” or “our,” the prospective donor would feel like they’re giving to people instead of a organization or corporate entity.
experiments.keyLearnings:
When pulling the results, the donor conversion rate was relatively the same. We saw a 16% increase in donor conversion rate but with only a level of confidence of 52%. We also saw little no impact in click-thru rate for these emails. However when looking at revenue data, we saw a 51% directional lift in revenue with a level of confidence of 87.8%. While this is not a statistically valid result, it can be surmised that there are some donors who may have been motivated to give a larger gift when reading the treatment copy. The treatment copy speaks to the donor as if they're joining "us" to accomplish something as opposed to giving to someone–making this much more communal in nature.
It's recommended we retest this concept and also include the donation page copy in the experiment so that the donation page copy also has the organizational language removed in the treatment experience.

experiments.title: How a “complete your gift” popup during a Fiscal Year End Campaign affects donor revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a “complete your gift” popup targeting visitors who choose a gift amount but abandon the donation page will increase donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/24/2022 - 09/04/2022
FamilyLife is a Christian ministry serving families worldwide. For their august Fiscal Year-End campaign, they were seeing a decrease in donor revenue and we wanted to see if we could turn that around in the final days of the campaign. We decided to test a popup what would show if a visitor visited any donation page on their site and chose a gift amount but then abandoned the page before competing their donation. This popup would then fire when they went anywhere else on the site and offer them the prompt to complete their X gift or indicate they weren’t ready to do so. If they chose to, it would reload the donation page so they could complete the transaction.
We split the abandoned donation page traffic 50/50 and ran the experiment for about 11 days.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We were only able to record an extremely marginal impact on donor conversion for the popup experience, however the impact on revenue was found in all primary channels to the website. While we were just under a 95% LOC for all traffic, we found a similar increase on all other channels including a 41% increase for direct traffic at a 97% LOC.
The popup reduced friction for these donors, causing them to give a higher amount when they revisited the donation page. Since this ran during the Fiscal Year End campaign, we want to relaunch it in September to measure impact without a high urgency campaign skewing results one way or another.
Other revenue impact measured included:
Social visitors had a 77.0% increase in revenue with a 91% level of confidence.Desktop visitors had a 20.7% increase in revenue with an 88% level of confidence.Mobile visitors had a 13.8% increase in revenue with an 88% level of confidence.New  visitors had a 16.6% increase in revenue with a 91% level of confidence.Returning  visitors had a 17.9% increase in revenue with an 86% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How adding a premium to an ebook instant donation page impacts conversion rates and revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a premium for ebook downloaders will achieve a change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/26/2022 - 08/08/2022
Hoover Institution has a quarterly publication called the Hoover Digest, which is a compilation of recent articles from Hoover scholars. A donor can receive a ‘subscription’ to all 4 publications by making a donation over $100. In this experiment, we tested whether adding this offer to the instant donation page for the Spring Digest ebook would impact donor conversion rates and average gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Providing a subscription for all 4 of the physical versions of the Hoover Digest as an enticement to increase ones' gift amount had a mixed effect. While the donor conversion rate not impacted at all (only a 0.9% difference), the average gift increased by 37%, with a 85% level of confidence. This shows that people are interested in giving more to receive the physical versions of the Digest.
Since this is a quarterly publication, we will continue testing this, as well as testing to see whether the same premium offer will work across other ebook offers for Hoover.

experiments.title: How asking for less impacted overall revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that asking for less than their previous highest gift for recent donors will achieve an increase in overall appeal revenue.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/15/2021 - 09/30/2021
For KCBI’s fall fundraising campaign, we gained access to a new tool (Pursuant’s GivingDNA) with the ability to predict donors who were likely to upgrade. To validate this modeled data, we decided to launch a two-prong experiment. We wanted to see if we could accelerate the upgrade process by asking for a gift above their highest previous gift. We also decided to see if we could increase conversion by asking for a donation less than their highest previous gift for the segment that was not identified as likely to upgrade.
We split the donor file in four ways to create control and treatment groups of the upgrade and non-upgrade identified segments for this experiment. We then created four distinct emails in an eight email campaign that would either include the standard ask or would ask them for the upgraded/downgraded amount. The upgrade/downgrade language would consist of logic that explained why we were asking for more or less. This same language was used in all four experiment emails.
The experiment information below is specific to the segment that was identified as not likely to upgrade.
In this campaign, we utilize a tactic known as the chart of standards. We segmented each segment by their highest previous contribution and then selected a limited number of donors for a specific ask amount we wanted for this campaign. You can see this breakdown here. This concept is based upon research done on a theory in social psychology known as construal level theory. Our previous research has found this to lift giving in high urgency campaign–this experiment was a continuation of this research effort.
At the end of the campaign, we analyzed the behavior of these distinct donors to understand how it both impacted their likelihood to upgrade and their likelihood to give.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a directional lift of 31% in revenue, we found that asking for a donation that was less than their highest previous gift amount increased the total revenue raised from the appeal. This came from a 24% increase in response rate combined with a higher average gift in the treatment group.

The concern with this approach was that we would suppress giving from donors that would have upgraded their gift amount. However, we found that the same number of people increased their giving despite the treatment group seeing a downgraded ask. This means the tactic didn't negatively impact the likelihood of a donor upgrading their highest previous gift.

This tactic needs to be further validated with additional experimentation but is exciting in its potential.

experiments.title: How a different value proposition affects email acquisition on a homepage ad
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a value proposition that conveys more urgency and focuses on the revenue at stake for homepage visitors will achieve more ebook downloads.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/08/2020 - 12/30/2020
In this experiment, we were promoting a new ebook focused on fundraising ideas that can help increase revenue during the last week of the year. We had two main ideas as to how to message the advertisement.
In version A, we used the homepage ad itself to illustrate how fundraisers can use a similar homepage ad to promote their year-end campaign.
In version B, the messaging emphasis focused on the urgency around the biggest giving week of the year being right around the corner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We didn't quite hit our desired 95% level of confidence, but the result was a strong indicator that it was more relevant to emphasize the urgency around the last week of the year – leading to a 50% increase in conversions. As noted below, it was especially effective for website visitors that already had some affinity towards us and our content.

Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Returning visitors had a 77.7% increase in all form submissions with an 89% level of confidence.
 	Email visitors had a 213.3% increase in all form submissions with a 96% level of confidence.
 	Paid Social visitors had a 66.7% increase in all form submissions with a 99% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How communal language impacts donor conversion on a hero banner
experiments.researchQuestion: Communal language on the hero image may increase donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/29/2021 - 02/27/2021
Care Net gets a healthy conversion rate on the hero image of their website. The call to action is to make a (monthly) gift, though they get a high volume of one time gifts from it as well. To see if we could increase the conversion from the banner, we wanted to test communal language on it.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running the experiment for a month, we weren't able to validate the test but we did see a 27% directional lift from the communal language. Additionally, this increased overall revenue by 33% at a 86% level of confidence. What is really interesting is that returning visitors had an even greater difference in conversion. The communal language increased donor conversion for returning visitors to the site by 49%! All of these results reinforce the idea that when we talk to the visitor as if they are a friend and even recognize and call-out the fact that we know and appreciate that they are fierce advocates for the cause, we increase their likelihood to give and ultimately convert. The conversion become less about the transactional nature of the gift and more about the impact and the donor.

An additional observation of the impact on another visitor segment in the experiment:
Direct Traffic visitors had a 70.9% increase in donations with an 86% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How communal language affects donor conversion rate during a matching gift campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that employing communal language for tribute donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/16/2020 - 12/20/2020
CaringBridge was running a matching gift campaign for two days as part of their holiday appeal. They had previously tested a sticky bar during a previous match that employed “communal” language (as opposed to transactional language) that increased both clickthrough rate and conversion rate during the match. They wanted to extend that language to their tribute donation pages, where people can give in honor of a specific journal author.
The original page positioned CaringBridge as the hero—it helped the author stay connected to family and friends. Then, it positioned the reader as the one who makes that happen. Instead, they decided to double down on language that addressed the prospective donor as the hero, removing CaringBridge entirely. First, they changed the headline to thank the donor for directly helping the author stay connected to their loved ones. Then, they reinforced that (and introduced CaringBridge) by adding exclusivity to the subheadline.
Finally, they applied the communal language theory to the call-to-action that mentioned the match. The original CTA offered a “dollar for dollar” match if the donor gave—which appeared to be transactional language. It also brought the brand “CaringBridge” back into the conversation. They removed the brand name from the CTA and provided a “pressure release valve” that put the power of whether to participate or not into the hands of the donor. Finally, they removed the “dollar for dollar” language and launched a test to see if their hypothesis was proven correct.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment with the communal language did not appear to have a significant impact overall. However, when the team dug into the device types, they found that desktop visitors—which made up the majority of donations—showed a 20.2% increase in conversion rate. On top of this increase in conversion rate, desktop visitors showed a 29.6% increase in revenue with a 98% level of confidence. Not only did more people give—they gave larger amounts!

This showed the potential impact of replacing transactional language with communal language. Next, the CaringBridge team wanted to re-test this language in a non-match scenario to see if it would have year-round impact.

experiments.title: How starting the gift array at a higher level impacted donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing the first gift array option for petition signers will achieve increase donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/29/2021 - 02/13/2021
The Canadian Taxpayer Federation was continuing their experimentation with their gift arrays on post-petition donation forms, and decided to experiment with a higher starting point on the gift array.
The control experience started with a $10 option and the treatment started with a $25 gift option.
Would increasing the amount on the first gift array button increase the donor conversion rate and raise revenue?
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment near fully validated but was turned off early because there was a near-valid decrease in donor conversion rate of -27.5% (LoC: 93.4%).

Some other observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Mobile visitors had a 54.3% decrease in donations with a 96% level of confidence.
 	Email visitors had a 28.0% decrease in donations with a 90% level of confidence.
The treatment decreased gifts by -83% and revenue by -85% at the $10 (or below) gift level.
 	The treatment increased gifts by +23% and revenue by 23% at the $25 gift level. And,
 	After removing a large outlier gift from the treatment, we saw that it decreased gifts by -30% and revenue by -40% at the $25+ gift level.

experiments.title: How more succinct copy affects total revenue on a homepage takeover
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that writing more succinct value proposition copy for homepage takeover visitors will achieve more sales and revenue.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/28/2020 - 12/31/2020
During the last week of the year, we ran a homepage takeover to promote our last week of the year sale: 50% off any course, membership, or NIO Summit ticket.
After creating the control, we wondered if shortening the copy to be more succinct (without removing any of the core reasons to purchase) could actually lead to more sales and revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings: Although on the surface it appears that the treatment saw a big lift in revenue, the number of transactions are the same and only a single transaction made the difference in revenue. Pairing that with a small sample size, we can't really say that shorter copy was better performing with any confidence.

experiments.title: How does a homepage takeover affect year end donations and revenue?
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: Sending direct homepage traffic a calendar year end giving page will increase year end donations.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/30/2020 - 12/31/2020
During their calendar year end campaign, on December 30th and 31st, we tested redirecting 50% of the Oblates direct homepage traffic to the calendar year end donation page. The hypothesis was that most people who come to a page on that day will be interested in giving, or more likely to give.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test saw an 11% increase in donations, which resulted in 45 more gifts. We recommend including this in calendar year end tactics in the coming year.

Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Mobile visitors had a 19.8% increase in donations with a 95% level of confidence.
 	Email visitors had a 16.8% increase in donations with a 99% level of confidence.
 	Returning visitors had a 18.8% increase in donations with a 96% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How breaking your fundraising goal down into a smaller ask impacts revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that setting a specific goal of donors and targeted gift amount for campaign prospects will achieve increase revenue.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/03/2021
During the Leadership Institute’s “2021 Kickoff” campaign, we wanted to experiment with asking donors to be one of a few donors to give a specific gift amount to see if it would increase the donor’s likelihood to give, or their generosity altogether.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With an 88.9% level of confidence, we observed a revenue increase of +52.1%.

This was driven by an increase in the donor conversion rate of +24% (LoC: 73%) and an average gift that was 22.6% higher than gifts made from the control segment.

Although this didn't fully validate, the results were directionally sound, which means that through the course of a longer campaign and more gifts, we could expect to see this hold true in 17.7 of 20 times that we ran this experiment.

When looking at what donors gave against what they were asked for, we noticed some interesting facts, which are:
40% of treatment gifts received gave more than what we asked for, representing 43% of the dollars raised in treatment experience.
 	38% of the treatment gifts received gave exactly what we asked for, representing 37.5% of the dollars raised in the treatment experience.
 	22% of the treatment gifts received gave less than what we asked for, representing 19.5% of the dollars raised in the treatment experience.
74% more donors decided to give "more than" their MRC when in the treatment experience.
 	These donors produced 86.5% more revenue when in the treatment experience than donors in the control experience.
 	3x as many donors decided to make their largest gift ever through the treatment experience. And,
 	This represented a 257% increase in revenue from the treatment segment who decided to give a gift to this campaign that was higher than their HPC value when compared to those in the control segment.
When a donor is left to their own decision on how much to give, they often choose to give less than they could — so we should tell them exactly what we want them to give in this sort of way.

experiments.title: How a longer term campaign flight date impacted revenue and donor conversion rate in a Facebook
experiments.researchQuestion: Would launching with a longer term (and more budget over that term) impact donor conversion or revenue when compared to the traditional 30-day campaign launch timeline (and budget) setting?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/11/2020
As a part of the COVID-19 (Coronavirus) response efforts, Americans For Prosperity launched a new open letter acquisition offer to allow concerned Americans the opportunity to add their name to an open letter to Congress to tell them NOT to use taxpayer money to bail out the states.
In launching the new offer, they wondered if launching the new campaign to promote this offer would be best if it used a 30-day campaign timeline (and 30-day budget) vs. a 6-month timeline (and 6-months worth of budget) in the campaign settings.
No other variable was tested within this campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Although we needed to turn this experiment off before it validated, the results were directionally accurate when we observed a +36% increase in donor conversion rate (LoC: 66%) and subsequently a 39.2% increase in revenue (LoC: 90%) when giving the campaign a longer period of time and budget setting.

Our net cost per donor also dropped by 35% for the 6-month campaign and ROI was up +40% when compared to the 1-month campaign type.

Key Takeaway:

Our hypothesis is that when showing the ad service platform (in this case Facebook) that we want to run a campaign, telling them that we're willing to advertise for 6-months (and subsequently, can spend 6-months worth of budget in total) on this new campaign tells the algorithm that they stand to make more money when showing your ad, compared to other advertisers that might also be bidding to serve ads to that same user.

If you think of digital ad campaigns as an auction (which is what they are) — and you, as a buyer, show up with a big briefcase full of money — the auction house knows you're a qualified buyer, and will provide you with preferential treatment. Just like with an auction, and as a buyer with a big briefcase full of money, you don't have to actually spend the money you came into the auction house with. So, if the campaign isn't performing well, you can always turn off your campaign(s) or ads.

Further experimentation may be required to validate this hypothesis, however, it might be advisable to simply run your campaigns for a longer period of time, with the total budget (under the "lifetime budget" setting) to ensure that you can garner better efficiency, better prioritization, and better return on investment for the same dollar spent by simply appearing bigger than you really are.

experiments.title: How the “fear of failure” at a campaign’s deadline impacted donor response rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe the fear of failure will increase donor response rates.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/08/2021
As a part of American’s for Prosperity’s year-end fundraising campaign, they had a final email appeal reminding the donor of an upcoming deadline (midnight) so as to inspire them to give.
We decided to experiment with a positive message (implying that as the deadline approached, that the reader’s gift right now could “put us over the top” of our goals) vs. a more negative tone that focused on the fear of loss (implying that “we, collectively, will not succeed” without the reader’s gift right now as the deadline is approaching).
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 93.9% level of confidence (just shy of a significant result), we observed an increase of +374% in the donor conversion rate when using a negative tone (eg a message that emphasizes the fear of not hitting our collective goals without the donor's gift right now).

Subsequently, we also observed an increase in revenue from the treatment (negative tone) message of +898% (LoC: 85.8%).

This revenue increase was achieved by a combination of a +52% increase in the average gift size, as well as the increased response rate—which multiplied together to drive the revenue lift seen within the study.

Overall, the samples are pretty light and we'd like to further experiment with this approach again in future studies, but the results seem to indicate that the "fear of loss" within the treatment message drove a higher response and better generosity.

experiments.title: How trustmarks on a donation page impacted donations and revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a Charity Navigator trust mark for donation page visitors will achieve increase in number of gifts and average gift size.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/05/2020 - 12/10/2020
In previous experiments, we have found that adding trust marks near the donation button on a donation page can lead to an increase in donations. However, we wanted to take this learning a step further and test how the presence of ANY trust marks and the presence of CERTAIN trust marks would impact revenue.
The control version of the donation page featured two of the organization’s standard trust marks, with the first treatment removing all trust marks and the second treatment containing the standard trust marks and the additional Charity Navigator trust mark.
The Charity Navigator trust mark showed the organization’s score/ranking of 75% with the “Give with Confidence” banner. The placement for all trust marks was directly beneath the donate button at the bottom of the page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While we did not reach a valid level of confidence for the experiment, what we observed through this experiment was the treatment with no trust marks and the treatment with the added Charity Navigator trust mark were both seeing an overall increase in conversion rate. However, when we analyze revenue and average gift performance for each version, the first treatment with no charity marks saw a slight decrease in revenue compared to the control while the second treatment with the additional Charity Navgiator trust mark was approaching a valid lift in revenue (58.3% with a 91% level of confidence). We also noted a few segments that were impacted directly, with an increase in conversion rate for both tablet and email visitors.

Notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment:
Revenue experienced a 58.3% increase with a 91% level of confidence.
Tablet visitors had a 33.3% increase in donations with a 92% level of confidence.
 	Email visitors had a 55.0% increase in donations with an 87% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How emotive language affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will emotive language cause more conversions, even without language proving credibility?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/29/2019
The Leadership Institute has a plan for conservatives to take back college campuses by placing at least one conservative student group on every college in America. They wanted to know if stats that back up their credibility would gain more views on a page than using emotive language without supporting credibility. They decided to test it.
experiments.keyLearnings: The results were 10 donations for the copy with emotive language versus 2 donations for the copy with language supporting credibility. Out of a sample size of 19,040, the relative difference in this conversion rate saw a 225.3% lift in revenue. The Leadership Institute should focus more on emotive language rather than numbers and stats.

experiments.title: How visitor motivation affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Which landing page treatment will lead to the most donations and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/11/2015 - 08/14/2015
The Media Research Center (MRC) was running a petition to the major news networks to increase their coverage of the Planned Parenthood scandal. After the initial signature, the visitors were sent to a thank you page and given the opportunity to make a gift.  The page was a two-column design in keeping with layout of the previous petition page.  The left side had value proposition language relating the media coverage to the Gosnell trail and the initial Planned Parenthood video embedded in the copy.
It was our hypothesis that we were assuming too much with the visitors. By placing the donation form at the top of the page, we were visually making an ask without first giving the visitors a reason to give. Additionally, the copy focused on what MRC had done in the past instead of what it was currently doing to combat the Planned Parenthood media outage.  As a result, we created a new treatment that utilized a single column design, removed the video from the page, and tweaked the copy to focus on Planned Parenthood instead of Goznell.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The single column landing page resulted in a 32% decrease in donor conversion. It is our hypothesis this decrease is less about the page layout and value proposition used and more about the motivation of the visitors. With a campaign topic as emotional as the Planned Parenthood scandal, the visitors were already highly motivated to give. By moving the donation form down on the page, we added additional friction to the process. The visitors did not need the additional reasons to give so when they didn’t see the donation form, the abandoned the process.
Most landing pages will see increased results when we place the value proposition copy before the donation form however, it is important to recognize the influence that external factors have on the donors’ motivation to give.

experiments.title: How asking active donors to be one of a few donors to give a specific amount impacted revenue during a matching gift campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: How does communicating a specific quantity of donors we’re asking to make a gift relevant to the active donor impact donor conversion or revenue?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/04/2020
As a part of The Heritage Foundation’s matching gift campaign (called “The Board Challenge”), we wanted to experiment with the ask amount and approach. Typically, the organization has used “make your best gift today” kind of language within their control copy.
In an attempt to set a smaller, more reasonable target, we wanted to experiment with driving donors to be “one of just X donors we need today to make a gift of Y.”
The idea behind this is to (a) set a relevant ask amount for the donors, but also (b) allow them to see that they can help achieve a “small quantity” goal at that amount, as well.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Although we did not validate an increase, we did see a directional increase of revenue by +12.6% (LoC: 83.9%).
Further, the donor conversion rate was relatively flat between the two segments (only showing a +2% increase, with a level of confidence of 29.9%). Additionally, we observed that 20.7% of the people in the treatment segment decided to give more than the amount that was asked for, while another 36% gave the exact amount that was asked of them.
Conclusion: Perhaps further experimentation with the ask amounts is advisable for active donors, but this directionally showed a lift in revenue.

experiments.title: How personalization and brand reduction affect donor conversion on a post-signup donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: How will personalization and brand reduction affect donor conversion on a post-signup donation page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/07/2020 - 09/05/2020
Hillsdale College was running a pre-registration campaign to promote their new course “Introduction to Western Philosophy”.
