They wanted to test two concepts on the donation page that followed the pre-registration. The first concept they wanted to test was personalization. Since the user signed up and gave their first name, they wanted to test a version of the donation page that congratulated the user by name for signing up. They were interested to see if this personalization would increase engagement on the page and humanize the “transaction”, resulting in an increased conversion rate. They also noticed that the approved copy had multiple repeated mentions of both Hillsdale and the full course name, “Introduction to Western Philosophy”. They hypothesized that removing these repeat mentions, replacing with “our” and “this course” would both “get the brand out of the way” and increase the readability of the page, resulting in increased conversion. Since they expected a large influx of traffic for this pre-registration period, they launched a three-way test to see if they could find a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Both treatments had positive increases on the control, but in different ways.
Treatment one, with the added personalization, actually had very little impact on conversion but resulted in a statistically valid 26% lift in revenue. This indicates that personalization may increase the force of the value proposition in the mind of the donor, inspiring them to give more generously.
Treatment two, which removed the brand and course mentions, produced two increases, neither statistically valid but both directionally interesting. First, it increased conversion rate by 10.6% with a 86.9% LoC. While this did not validate, it did show a trend that, with more traffic, might be proven favorable. It also produced a 19% lift in revenue, with a 91% LoC—again, not statistically significant, but revealing a learning to be re-tested in a future campaign.
Hillsdale's team decided to combine the two treatments in a future offer to see if the combination of the personalization (which had a valid lift to revenue) and the reduction of brand terminology (which had a near-valid lift to conversion) would work together to produce a valid lift overall.

experiments.title: The impact of using a donor’s name on a post-survey donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: Will seeing your name on a donation page after giving your name in a survey increase your liklihood to donate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/22/2020 - 07/20/2020
As we continued to test the impact of using smart content on donation pages with DepositFix, we decided to take our high performing “Abortion in America Survey” Facebook campaign and test smart content on the donation page. Mainly, if someone was visiting the page already in our database, they would see their name on the page. Since they just gave their name on the survey, we thought that the continuity might increase donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running the experiment for one month, we achieved a 35% increase in donor conversion. However, our revenue decreased by 11%. This was due to a smaller average gift on the variation (smart content) page. The control page received two donations that were $250 and multiple that were $100.
With the lower revenue, we may not move forward with smart content on these campaigns. Revenue and donor conversion are both important, but ultimately revenue is the prevailing concern.

experiments.title: How simplifying the reading level of an open letter impacted downstream revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Would simplifying the reading level of the open letter increase email signup, donor conversion or revenue?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/19/2020
As a part of the COVID-19 (Coronavirus) response efforts, American’s For Prosperity made available to concerned Americans an opportunity to add their name to an open letter to Congress as they planned a government response to provide citizens aid, as well as focused stimulus dollars to “restart the economy” again after the pandemic.
The open letter had become a wonderful engagement piece for the housefile, so they decided to open it up to prospective supporters to give them the opportunity to add their name alongside tens of thousands of other signers, as well.
Because the open letter was so popular, they decided to put a donation appeal behind it and test acquiring new donors, and did so very effectively.
But as time went on, the open letter signup (or email capture) rates were beginning to decline, so they wanted to experiment with increasing email signups.
The original letter was carefully crafted by government affair and policy experts and when graded in a readability tool, it was projected to be at a 15th grade reading level. If the 5th grade reading level they tried previously was determined to be “oversimplified,” they then thought to try a 12th grade reading level in an attempt to bring it closer to the original letter contents.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We observed that with a 92.7% level of confidence, that this “simplified readability” open letter decreased revenue by -32.5%.
This decrease in revenue was driven by two factors:
A directionally valid 24% decrease in donor conversion rate (LoC: 80%), and
A 13% lower average gift for the fewer number of donors that gave through the treatment experience.
It’s worth noting that we did observe a fully validated increase in email signup rate on the simplified open letter by +12.7% (LoC: 100%). This is particularly interesting, because although it did facilitate more email signups, that greater volume of people moving through to the donation page were NOT as motivated as those coming through the higher grade reading level in the control experience.
This experiment’s findings seem to validate the original hypothesis, which was that the increased reading speed works against the psychological approach and learnings gathered by adding the mini checkboxes—in that we found when we can slow someone down as they move through the funnel, that they become more likely to read and consume the content within the open letter. And when they do that, they are more likely to convert as a donor on the subsequent instant donation page because they fully understand and align with the contents designed to grow their motivation.

experiments.title: How a longer term campaign flight date impacted revenue and donor conversion rate in a Facebook
experiments.researchQuestion: Would launching with a longer term (and more budget over that term) impact donor conversion or revenue when compared to the traditional 30-day campaign launch timeline (and budget) setting?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/11/2020
As a part of the COVID-19 (Coronavirus) response efforts, Americans For Prosperity launched a new open letter acquisition offer to allow concerned Americans the opportunity to add their name to an open letter to Congress to tell them NOT to use taxpayer money to bail out the states.
In launching the new offer, they wondered if launching the new campaign to promote this offer would be best if it used a 30-day campaign timeline (and 30-day budget) vs. a 6-month timeline (and 6-months worth of budget) in the campaign settings.
No other variable was tested within this campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Although we needed to turn this experiment off before it validated, the results were directionally accurate when we observed a +36% increase in donor conversion rate (LoC: 66%) and subsequently a 39.2% increase in revenue (LoC: 90%) when giving the campaign a longer period of time and budget setting.

Our net cost per donor also dropped by 35% for the 6-month campaign and ROI was up +40% when compared to the 1-month campaign type.

Key Takeaway:

Our hypothesis is that when showing the ad service platform (in this case Facebook) that we want to run a campaign, telling them that we’re willing to advertise for 6-months (and subsequently, can spend 6-months worth of budget in total) on this new campaign tells the algorithm that they stand to make more money when showing your ad, compared to other advertisers that might also be bidding to serve ads to that same user.

If you think of digital ad campaigns as an auction (which is what they are) — and you, as a buyer, show up with a big briefcase full of money — the auction house knows you’re a qualified buyer, and will provide you with preferential treatment. Just like with an auction, and as a buyer with a big briefcase full of money, you don’t have to actually spend the money you came into the auction house with. So, if the campaign isn’t performing well, you can always turn off your campaign(s) or ads.

Further experimentation may be required to validate this hypothesis, however, it might be advisable to simply run your campaigns for a longer period of time, with the total budget (under the “lifetime budget” setting) to ensure that you can garner better efficiency, better prioritization, and better return on investment for the same dollar spent by simply appearing bigger than you really are.

experiments.title: How inviting donors to contribute to a matching gift affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: How will the opportunity to contribute to a matching gift affects conversion versus a traditional matching gift?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/25/2020
Leadership Institute was running a special “pre-Giving Tuesday” campaign as the initial part of their year-end campaign. The team wanted to test whether offering donors the opportunity to contribute to a matching gift would inspire more generosity, as opposed to a traditional matching gift as an incentive (as had traditionally been done). They developed two series of emails and split donors by previous giving amounts to run each through a three-email campaign leading up to Giving Tuesday to determine which approach resulted in more gifts and revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings: In terms of conversion, there was no statistical difference between the two approaches. Given the same size of housefile recipients, the incentive to "be the match" did not get more people to give. However, a deeper look at the data showed that the "be the match" effort had a large, if not statistically significant effort on average gift size. This approach may have unlocked larger gifts due to the elevated importance it placed on the donor. Since many donors, especially those at LI, are trained to understand that matching gift sizes are usually $10,000+, there may have been a motivation to give more to join a "larger giving group". However, it should be noted that with only 114 gifts between the two campaigns, the sample size is relatively small. This approach (which is somewhat limited in its application abilities) should be tested with a larger sample size and a higher expected number of total gifts.

experiments.title: How emphasizing a recurring gift impacted revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Will emphasizing the impact of a recurring increase the overall revenue per visitor?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/03/2017 - 06/06/2017
A past analysis of The Heritage Foundation donor file found that recurring donors provide the highest donor value to the organization and have the best chance of being retained into future years. Based upon this analysis, we decided to launch an experiment to increase the number of recurring givers.
We crafted value proposition copy around the reason to make a recurring gift and included near the recurring gift option on the donation form. We also emphasized making the gift via their Bank Account as these donations tend to have a longer lifetime.
experiments.keyLearnings: After a month of collecting data, we did not see any significant impact on the donor’s likelihood to make a recurring gift, select their Bank Account, or increase overall revenue to the organization. This is still a priority for the organization so we will go back to the drawing board to develop new hypotheses to increase someone’s likelihood to make a recurring gift.

experiments.title: How a book premium impacted acquisition offer revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Would a book (gift premium) work to increase either donor conversion rate or revenue (and subsequently ROI) for our acquisition offers?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/04/2019 - 01/31/2020
In an attempt to boost ROI from their acquisition efforts in expanding their digital program, The Fund for American Studies (TFAS) was looking for ways to increase their average gift, while ideally not impacting their donor conversion rate. They decided to test a gift premium tied to the $100 gift amount to see if they could improve results substantially and drive ROI to a higher level.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 89.7% level of confidence, we were able to achieve a +20.6% increase in revenue by using the book as a gift premium (tied to the $100 gift level) on our acquisition offers.
Although this experiment did not fully validate (at the 95% LoC that we would typically require to declare a winner), because of the preference to increase ROI, TFAS decided to rollout the treatment and take the increased revenue (and ROI).
There are a few more observations made throughout this experiment, notably the following:
We did also observe a -10% donor conversion rate (although that only achieved a 55% LoC, which means it was not validated).
The increase in revenue, albeit with fewer donors for the treatment was driven by the dramatic increase in average gift (+36%).
Specifically, the treatment experience produced nearly 25% of the overall gifts received at the $100+ gift level (to receive the premium), whereas the control experience only achieved 6.4% of the overall gifts received at that same gift level ($100+).
Overall, the overall net incremental cost the organization was an additional $3 to acquire these book premium donors at the $100+ gift level.
For all of these reasons, TFAS decided to take the much higher volume of $100+ donors through their acquisition efforts for the additional $3 net cost to acquire them.
It should be noted that if you are considering using a book premium, that the placement of the book (after the core value proposition) as well as the relevancy of the book to the core value proposition that is chosen is highly recommended.
in this case, a high-profile alumnus wrote the book used in this experiment. We believe that these two factors are critical for acquiring long-term supporters for the organization (as the offer is not the book, but the core value proposition, and that the book sits behind that core offer as an incentive to “give more” to receive it for the donor).
Because of this, future testing with this book serving as the control will be considered, whereas new and more relevant books will be considered as treatments to future acquisition offers.

experiments.title: How a pre-selected value on the gift array impacted donor conversion rate and revenue from acquisition forms
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding a “most popular” pre-selected value on the gift array impact donor conversion rate or revenue for acquisition forms?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/22/2020
Americans for Prosperity was looking into ways to increase their donor conversion rate and revenue from the investment they were making in acquiring donors through instant donation forms online. In reviewing the forms, we decided to try adding a “Most Popular” pre-selected option in the gift array to see how it would impact these key metrics.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This treatment improved revenue by 94.4% (with a 92.3% level of confidence). Additionally, the donor conversion rate was showing an increase of 29.5% (although the level of confidence achieved was only 66%). Although these results didn’t officially validate, the lift in revenue is certainly directional and the decision was made to roll this out to gain the apparent benefits of the treatment option.

This increase in revenue was largely driven by an increase in the average gift amount of +55%.

Additionally, the number of $50 or greater sized gifts acquired through the treatment option was doubled in the treatment.

When donors are considering giving a gift during the instant donation page in your acquisition efforts, one of the decisions they have to make is “how much should I give?” This “Most Popular” treatment appears to decrease the friction in the process of making a donation because the donors now have a “starting point” provided to them as to make a decision about “how much” to give.

experiments.title: How removing the email template impacts clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does a plain text email generate more clicks?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/03/2018
During Hurricane Florence, BGEA experimented with sending a plain text style email against their standard marketing email template to see if it generated more clicks.
experiments.keyLearnings:
In reviewing the two creatives that were involved in this experiment, we initially thought that the marketing email generated more clicks, but after further slicing the web analytics details, we identified that the marketing template had an additional clickable link to an article (which was hyperlinked on the header image) that the plain text version did not include.
Overall clicks were higher on the marketing template, but when you isolate the click traffic on the PRIMARY objective (which is calling the recipient to give a gift), we actually saw that the treatment generated 1,025 clicks against the control’s 526 clicks on the “Give Now” option (which represents a 94% increase in click activity).
Subsequently, the clickthrough traffic on that primary call to action (Give Now) saw a higher conversion rate (16.6%), as well as a 53.1% higher overall revenue than the control.
Further experimentation is required to validate this hypothesis, but initial results look promising.

experiments.title: How moving Lourdes water to a one page donation increased donations
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: We wanted to learn if a one step checkout process would help increase donations for Lourdes Water.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/20/2019 - 10/27/2019
We get a lot of traffic to make a donation for Lourdes Water. It’s our most popular item, so we wanted to try to find a way people could make a donation easier.
experiments.keyLearnings: It increased donations and conversion rate, but the revenue stayed about the same. For our longer checkout process we have an area that asks them to make an additional donation. We found that this is why there was less donations, but similar amount in revenue. We are going to move forward using the one step process for the Lourdes Water, but continue to test different ways to increase the average gifts.

experiments.title: How moving Lourdes water to a one page donation increased donations
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: We wanted to learn if a one step checkout process would help increase donations for Lourdes Water.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/20/2019 - 10/27/2019
We get a lot of traffic to make a donation for Lourdes Water. It’s our most popular item, so we wanted to try to find a way people could make a donation easier.
experiments.keyLearnings: It increased donations and conversion rate, but the revenue stayed about the same. For our longer checkout process we have an area that asks them to make an additional donation. We found that this is why there was less donations, but similar amount in revenue. We are going to move forward using the one step process for the Lourdes Water, but continue to test different ways to increase the average gifts.

experiments.title: The impact of making a monthly donor appeal in a homepage hero image (Hint: 1,392% increased revenue)
experiments.researchQuestion: Which would raise more money, a hero image making a one-time donation appeal, or one that would appeal for new monthly donors?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/28/2019
Every two weeks, we would change out our homepage hero image featuring a donation CTA as well as our Top Ad donation CTA. We would swap these out to match the language of the most current bi-monthly digital appeal. These hero images were almost always for a one-time donation page.  We hypothesized that, instead of making a one-time donation appeal, we should try acquiring new monthly donors through the hero image. We did not set up a strict AB test, but rather replaced the hero and top ad images with a new monthly appeal and tracked the results.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The Control had a higher donor conversion rate, but much fewer clicks. The variation attracted far more visitors to click and, since the appeal was for a monthly appeal, rather than a one-time appeal, resulted in significant more revenue. We ran the test for 72 days. Here are the results:
Control: 327 clicks, 13 donations, $981 raised.
Variation: 1057 clicks, 24 donations, $1,220 raised in monthly revenue, $14,640 in yearly revenue.
The variation had a 227% increase in clicks,  84% increase in donations, and a 1,392% increase in revenue! So, while fewer visitors to the donation page were prepared to become monthly donors, the significant increase in clicks and revenue more than made up for the lower conversion rate.

experiments.title: How including a small, tangible goal impacted overall donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Does adding a small, tangible goal help motivate donors to make a gift?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/20/2019 - 06/02/2019
Buckner International was running an appeal around providing scholarships to foster children in order to go to camp. In the past, we had observed increased results when we used small, tangible goals in our appeal but it had never been tested directly. We decided to run a head-to-head test for this campaign to understand the impact of using these small goals with the Buckner donor file.
For this campaign, we split the full house file in half and send three total emails to each segment. The control segment would not have a goal and the treatment segment would have a goal of sending 10 children to camp. We would then combine the giving across all three messages to validate the results.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 90% level of confidence, we observed a 120% increase in donor conversion by using a small, tangible goal. We generally shoot for a 95% level of confidence but, considering the difference in overall gifts (and revenue), we believe it is safe to say that the small goal had a positive impact on donor conversion.
In the past, when we have used large goals in the hundreds of thousands of dollars, they have not been very motivational without being tied to a match. Knowing that we can use a smaller goal to motivate donors will be helpful in future campaigns.

experiments.title: How a Daily Signal core fundraising message impacted donor conversion rate for the Dear Reader ads
experiments.researchQuestion: Does a Daily Signal support call to action acquire more donors (or generate more revenue) than the existing message to activate a membership?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/25/2019 - 03/31/2019
In an attempt to move away from the existing control message (focusing upon “partnering with President Trump” by “activating your Heritage membership”), we wanted to experiment with a more TDS-focused message about why The Daily Signal exists, and how we can only continue with the support of readers.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With 84.6 level of confidence, we substantiated a -29.5% decrease in donor conversion rate. We also saw a -14.8% decrease in revenue (70.2% level of confidence) alongside these results.
We did, however, see a +20% increase in “Dear Reader” ad clicks (with a 95% level of confidence).
We decided to stop this treatment due to the compounding impact of decreased revenue and donor conversion rates.
When digging into the data, we found some interesting pieces of information as it pertains to the type of visitor (new visitor vs. returning visitor).
Here are some observations for new visitors and comparative impact for the treatments within this experiment:
The control was 138% better at converting new visitors.
The control generated a 119% higher revenue/visitor value from new visitors.
The treatment was 36% more effective at converting returning visitors (topping a 31% donor conversion rate).
The treatment generated a 22% higher average gift from returning visitors.
The treatment generated a 67% higher revenue/visitor value from returning visitors.
It would appear that the control message is most effective at converting first time visitors, while the treatment message was most effective at converting returning visitors.
Our recommendation would be to look to roll both treatments out based upon the number of times a visitor has arrived at a page. Specifically, if the the reader has already converted through a “Dear Reader” ad in the past, it might be advisable to immediately swap the ads they see to a message more similar to the treatment.

experiments.title: How targeted digital advertising affected direct mail results
experiments.researchQuestion: Will showing ads to a targeted group of direct mail recipients result in increased revenue from the treatment group?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/26/2017 - 11/25/2017
The Canadian Taxpayers Federation wanted to test whether showing Facebook video ads to house file direct mail recipients, before and during the response period caused a lift in any key metric: conversion, donation size or total revenue. This test was based on the successful test executed by Hillsdale College.
The Taxpayers Federation created a control audience that was excluded from seeing any video ads, and a treatment audience that would see a rotation of three video ads (but no donation-centric or petition ads) for a one week period before the mail piece hit mailboxes and for three weeks after, the prime time in which a donor would respond. They chose videos that had been popular in the past, but were still relevant. They optimized the ads for reach—aiming to show ads to as large a percentage of the target group as possible. The goal of this test was to lift direct mail revenue—not to add an additional segment of online revenue or capture data through petitions.
Ads were turned off once each video had an average frequency (number of times shown to each person reached) greater than 4.1. However, some videos were displayed as many as 4.5 times.
They spent just shy of $700 (CDN) to show ads to the treatment audience and waited to see the results come in.
experiments.keyLearnings: It appears that with a 25.4% increase in dollars per visitor, at a 92.6% confidence level, that this was a success and should be expanded and replicated in the future. Overall, a $690 investment in Facebook ads, resulted in a nearly $10,000 increase in overall revenue.

experiments.title: How the reasons NOT to give affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: By giving reasons NOT to support DTS on Giving Tuesday, will we be able to drive incremental traffic to the donation page and which will lead to an increased number of donors?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/02/2014 - 12/04/2014
Giving Tuesday has become a powerful fundraising response to the consumerism of Black Friday and Cyber Monday. But with so many nonprofits are soliciting gifts on the same day, it becomes more difficult to stand out in the crowd. DTS tested a contrarian messaging approach that would both intrigue their supporters and increase traffic to the donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The test treatment was able to increase the click through rate by 528.3% but was not able to convert enough of those visitors to donors to be a statistically valid experiment.  With that said, the results would certainly indicate that this tactic may work in the future as long as we then focus our efforts on optimizing the landing page for the varying motivations of the visitors.

This same tactic of driving an increased number of visitors to the page will be employed in the future but more effort needs to be placed on developing a page that fully addresses the reason for the visit. Our hypothesis is that we did not spend enough time explaining the reasons NOT to give before leading into reasons TO give.

experiments.title: How active language in the call to action increases donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Does active language on a donate button increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/26/2017 - 06/14/2017
Live Action wanted to increase donations on their main donation page. They tested the copy used on the “Donate” button in the navigation of liveaction.org. After finding that the copy “Defend the Preborn” had a 60% increase in clicks over the original “Donate” copy and the other treatment, “Support Live Action”, they tested which copy converted more donors.
experiments.keyLearnings: The control was “Defend the Preborn” and treatment #1 was “Support Live Action”. “Support Live Action” had a 46.5% increase in donations over “Defend the Preborn.” So while “Defend the Preborn” was able to get more people to the donation page initially, more people gave a donation using the text “Support Live Action”. What this tells us is that even though people were more motivated to click on “Defend the Preborn”, the lack of clarity in that copy to where they would be taken resulted in less people converting to what the primary objective was on the page – to give a gift. By using more active language with greater clarity, we were able to increase traffic to the donation page and increase donations and revenue.

experiments.title: How reducing form functionality affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Which donation page treatment will lead to the highest number of gifts and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/23/2015 - 08/05/2015
Jews for Jesus had been running a series of optimization experiments on their primary donation form in an effort to improve the overall revenue generated by the page. Our first attempt resulted in no significant improvement when we removed elements of friction from the form and added language to the top of the page highlighting the organization’s unique value proposition.
We decided to attempt another treatment that would modify the value proposition of the page while leaving in place the changes to the form. With this new treatment, we wanted to utilize a personal letter from David Brickner, the organization’s CEO. Analysis of the email program had shown that he was popular with the constituency so we hypothesized the a shorter appeal, signed by him, could lift the average gift on the page enough to impact revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running the experiment for a month, we saw that the new treatment decreased the conversion rate on the page by 13.8%. The language that was used have already proven successful in a previous appeal so we analyzed what else could have been impacting conversion. Upon further reflection, we discovered that, in an effort to reduce the number of decisions made on the form, we may have inadvertently make it more difficult to complete.
One of the features of Jews for Jesus’s donation system is the ability for previous donors to log in to complete their gift without entering any additional information. We removed this feature to make room for quotes from credible sources and to remove the number of decisions a donor has to make. However, with this being the primary location existing donors come to make their gift, this caused additional friction for that key segment. This cause the number of gifts made to the form to decrease.
Future experiments will add this functionality back to the form and we will focus our efforts on purely refining the value proposition.

experiments.title: How the tone of the email can affect the donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Which messaging tone in the email will produce the greatest number of donors and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/19/2015 - 08/24/2015
When the Planned Parenthood videos were first starting to be released, the Media Research Center launched a petition with the goal of getting the major media networks to give the story the level of attention it deserved. By the time the 7th video was released, this petition had acquired more than 53,000 signatures. With each subsequent video, we wanted to update these signers on the progress of the petition and give them a way to contribute further with a “Quick Donate” option.
Previous emails had all used a “negative” tone by focusing on the continued lack of media coverage and what was needed to overcome the problem. We had a hypothesis that if we led with a positive tone by acknowledging the signers’ past action and the resulting progress, we could inspire them to take the next step and give a gift.  We decided to split the email file and validate this hypothesis.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The more negative email was able to produce 11% more opens but both emails had the same number of people click to the landing page to give a gift. However, those clicking from the positive email were not as incentivized to give a gift which led to a 46% decrease in donor conversion. Those visitors that had been inspired by the work left to do (the “negative” tone) and the urgency of the appeal were more highly motivated to give a gift once they made it to the page.
It is critical to remember, in an email appeal, the tone of the entire campaign can be impacted by every step of the process.

experiments.title: How the value communicated in a link can affect donations
experiments.researchQuestion: Does communicating the value of the gift in the link increase the subsequent donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/25/2015 - 08/31/2015
Media Research Center was running a petition campaign to force the major news networks to spend more time covering the Planned Parenthood undercover videos. We had learned in previous tests that the signers of the petition were highly motivated individuals that did not need a lot of value proposition copy to be convinced to make a gift. As a result, we utilized a “quick donate” strategy in subsequent emails that let the donors select their donation amount in the email and skip directly to the donation page.
While this strategy was proving successful, we wanted to find a way to boost the average gift of the donors since the average gift was below what is normally seen. We had a hypothesis that adding value to the individuals links would inspire larger gifts. As a result, we created a new treatment that applied value proposition copy to the links as a method of communicating greater value to the donor.
experiments.keyLearnings: While this test did not reach the 95% level of confidence necessary to be considered statistically significant, it was quickly approaching a valid result. While additional testing will be necessary to be certain, the increase in donations and revenue would indicate that the value proposition language boosted the overall campaign results.

experiments.title: Main WEB Donation Form (Short vs Long)
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a shortened form increase donations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/22/2016 - 03/31/2016
We are looking for ways to optimize our web donation form. This particular form is the source of over 30% of online revenue so it has high impact on donations online.
experiments.keyLearnings:
In this case, the shortened form did not increase donations. We learned a few things from this:
Not enough value force
There was too much friction by having to click donate again
We are redesigning another donation form that will focus on adding more value force and decreasing friction.

experiments.title: How a countdown clock affects the value proposition and revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Which countdown clock would increase donor conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/29/2015 - 01/20/2016
As part of their year-end campaign, Online for Life wanted to find a way to boost the conversion rate on their donation page.
In a previous test, we had learned that the addition of the “Every 26 seconds” clock on the donation page had increased donor conversion rate. Now, we wanted to test it against a treatment with a timer that counted down to the end of the year.
Our hypothesis was that people would be more motivated to give based on the urgency of the year-end. We ran a split A/B test. The control donation page had the”Every 26 seconds” clock and the treatment was the donation page with the year-end countdown clock.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The year-end countdown clock was able to increase the urgency of the ask and it also reinforced the value proposition of the donation page. This lead to the increase in conversion rate by 41.6%, but a decrease in average gift by 4.7%. However, overall, the addition of the year-end clock with messaging that supported it resulted in a 35% increase in revenue.
In the future, the countdown clock with financially focused messaging will be deployed towards the end of large year-end campaigns to increase revenue in the final days of giving.

experiments.title: How the layout of a donation form affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Regular buttons will increase the conversion rate given their increased ease of use both for reading and selecting.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/16/2014 - 09/23/2014
The out-of-the-box defaults for Online for Life’s donation system results in giving arrays that consist of radio buttons. Given the amount of text that accompanies each giving option on the form, these radio buttons tend to clutter the form and make it hard to operate on a mobile phone.  We wanted to test a new way of laying out the same information.
experiments.keyLearnings: The new buttons resulted in a 59.9% increase in conversion rate and were also able to increase the average gift by 56.5%. This proved how the new method for displaying the donation options made it easier for donors to understand.  As a result, the gift had more value to the donor resulting in a net increase of 150.2% revenue.

experiments.title: How Facebook ads impacted a campaign’s non-member conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding a 3rd channel of touches increase donor conversion rate for activating non-members for the first time?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/12/2019
During The Heritage Foundation’s Kickoff Campaign, we wanted to run Facebook ads designed to remind members to activate their membership to see if it would boost response rates by adding a 3rd channel (digital ads) along side regularly scheduled email and direct mail messages.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 99.9% level of confidence, the non-member segment that received Facebook ads in the 3rd channel of communication saw an online response rate increase of 20.3%.
Other metrics impacted are as follows:
Average gift: +13.1% increaseTotal Revenue: +36.09% increase
During the campaign, the total cost to run these ads to non-members generated a 2.03 ROI against the increase in revenue generated from the "with ads" segment. Simply put, 100% of the ad expense to increase responses from this audience was covered and generated an equal amount to the budget spent in additional revenue free and clear of any expense.
Final Conclusion:Where possible, adding a 3rd channel of communication to heed the campaign's call to action for non-members should be considered.

experiments.title: How a long-form, donor-centered appeal decreased donor conversion rates on the primary donate page
experiments.researchQuestion: Does a donor centered message help improve donor conversion rate (or revenue) on the primary donation form for Heritage?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/08/2019 - 03/13/2019
We wanted to test a donor-centered message on the primary donation page, as every treatment historically has been focused on an organizationally centered series of value propositions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 94.7% level of confidence, we validated that the treatment decreased the donor conversion rate by -37.8%.
Although the donor conversion rate decreased in the treatment segment, because the average gift in the treatment jumped pretty substantially (+61.3%) compared to the control, the revenue difference between the segments was only -0.6% (with a 50.86% LoC).
Our hypothesis is that the copy length led to the decrease in donor conversion rate, but we were encouraged by the increase in average gift from the treatment, so we will now develop and launch a short-form version of a donor-centered message to experiment against the short-form control message.

experiments.title: RD Size Test – October 2014 – University Appeal II
experiments.researchQuestion: Will the reduced copy positively effect our total income?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/22/2019
What Was Tested
We reduced the size of the copy from 2 pages down to 1/3 a page on a buckslip RD front & back.

experiments.keyLearnings: The average gift and total revenue was increased, however response rate and ROI were down. I am not sure if the costs were loaded in correctly, it seems like the costs should be lower than the CVP but they are not. This may be worth re-testing.

experiments.title: How emphasizing matching gifts during the serial ask impacted revenue and donor conversion.
experiments.researchQuestion:
There were actually two tethered research questions associated with this experiment, which were:
Will additional social proof language to reinforce matching your gift to HAFA increase revenue?
Will the higher gift ask amount positively or negatively impact the donor conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/24/2019 - 02/28/2019
During the calendar year end campaign for Heritage, we installed an exit popup which used social proof language along with a symbolic gift ask to people looking to leave the page (on desktop computers only) to try to increase donor conversion rate and revenue increases. We wanted to see if we could increase revenue while maintaining the donor conversion rate when using language to reinforce matching your gift amount to Heritage Action to the same amount the donor just selected to give to the Heritage Foundation.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Although the revenue lift did not fully validate we noticed two things, which were:
With 80.8% level of confidence, we saw that the matching gift language increased revenue by 20.8%.
With 100% level of confidence, we also saw that it decreased the instant donation conversion rate by -23.3%.
Although the revenue lift wasn’t validated, it’s enough to be directionally accurate. Our assumption was that this would decrease the donor conversion rate relatively to the lift in revenue, and it appears that our hypothesis was indeed true.
There are two additional noteworthy observations from this analysis, though:
In reviewing the control transactions, the symbolic gift amount of $17.76 represented 87 of the 449 transactions in the “control” segment of the experiment. If those gifts were removed, the control segment was about even with the number of transactions that were in the “treatment” segment (where the treatment would only have seen -3.62% difference).
Finally, when looking at the percentage of gifts that were matched between organizations across the two segments, only 70.8% of the gifts within the “control” segment were matched between the two organizations, but the “treatment” segment (which focused on emphasizing a matching gift between both organizations) boasted a 90.3% matching gift rate.
Relatively speaking, that would be an increase of +27.4% increase in matching gift rates for the treatment segment — which was the driving force between the increase in revenue for that segment.

experiments.title: How a “how did you hear about us” field affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How does the presence of a “how did you hear about us” attribution field affect conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/25/2012 - 12/18/2018
Compassion International had long included a required dropdown field in their checkout process that asked the user what prompted their decision to sponsor a child today. This data was used to help guide their high level budgeting process, and thus had some amount value, but they were curious to see how it affected conversion. They created two test variants—one that made the field optional, and one that eliminated the field entirely. They launched a multivariate test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: While none of the treatments gained full statistical validity, the test delivered two important directional learnings. First, there is no increase in conversion by removing the attribution field, which would imply that the data gained is more valuable to have than the revenue gained by removing the field. Second, making the field optional actually decreased conversion (and was approaching validity). One hypothesis as to why is that making the field optional now gave TWO decisions to the user: the first being “should I complete this field?” and the second being “what answer should I give?” This shows how seemingly small tweaks can compound decision friction. The results of this test indicate that this field should stay right where it is, as long as the data is useful to Compassion.

experiments.title: How changing the value proposition on a donation page can impact overall revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Which value proposition would acquire more donations and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/05/2017 - 06/20/2017
The treatment value proposition that was focused on the spiritual impact of a person’s gift increased overall revenue by 50%! There were not only more people who were motivated to give, but these people were motivated to give more. People were willing to invest in a kingdom-focused mission more generously.
While people cared about the event being successful and effective, they responded more when they knew their investment would have a larger impact – an eternal one.
This experiment didn’t validate, but it was leaning towards being valid and was rolled out for the rest of the campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings: Harvest Ministries had a high-urgency campaign leading up to their large-scale evangelism event, Harvest America. On the donation page for the campaign, the value proposition and call-to-action was centered on making the event a success. It had more of a logistics angle to it. Because the event was an evangelism outreach, we hypothesized that people might be more motivated to give if the value proposition was focused on the spiritual impact. We developed a treatment and split the traffic coming to the donation page.

experiments.title: How the kind of offer in an appeal affects donor conversion.
experiments.researchQuestion: Would the Tell Someone course offer receive more donations than the Tell Someone book offer?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/15/2016 - 08/22/2016
Normally, for Harvest’s mid-month appeals, they offer a resource (normally a book or DVD) to people if they give a gift to the ministry.
For Harvest’s August mid-month appeal they ran a test to see if they could acquire more donations through offering a book (the Tell Someone book) or through a free online course (the Tell Someone course). For the book offer, people were taken directly to a donation page where they could get the book and give their donation. For the course offer, people had to first sign up for the course and then be asked for a donation. Using 40% of the email file, they A/B split tested this.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While the results didn’t validate, the initial data pointed to validity and the treatment (the Tell Someone course) had an increase in opens, clicks, donations (25%) and revenue. Using this data, they then sent the remaining 60% of the email file the Tell Someone course offer.
Even though the test didn’t validate, deciding to send the remaining file the Tell Someone course offer was not only the best decision from what we were seeing with initial donations and revenue, but the long-term benefit and value of these donors is so much greater with the course than it would be with a more transactional offer like the book. The course gives people the opportunity to engage with Harvest on an ongoing basis at a deeper level. And at the same time, it allowed them to instantly support the ministry. By offering people something out of the ordinary that had a higher perceived value, people were much more motivated to give through the course offer than the book.

experiments.title: How increasing clarity in email copy affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will copy with a clearer call-to-action increase donor conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/01/2016 - 05/02/2016
Harvest Ministries was sending a mid-month update on their fundraising appeal for the month. The offer was a book by Joni Earackson Tada. The control email highly incentivized the click by asking the reader to “find out why” the sender (Greg Laurie) admires Joni, the author of the book. While this might drive a lot of clicks, we’ve seen this approach drive much lower conversion rates, as those who click through are not as motivated to accept the offer.
We devised a treatment that moved some of that copy into the email, incentivizing the click by giving them a timely call-to-action to find out how to get their own copy of the book before Mother’s Day.
This treatment produced a 58.7% decrease in clickthrough rate, but we wanted to measure conversion on the following donation page to see if those that clicked had a higher motivation to complete the intended transaction.
experiments.keyLearnings: The clearer call-to-action in the email did produce a 55.1% lift in donor conversion. However, because the email drove much less traffic, the control won in terms of overall revenue.

experiments.title: How the friction added with website navigation affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does adding the standard website header and navigation to the landing page ease the concerns of visitors about the cost of the course while not affecting or increasing conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/02/2015 - 03/17/2015
Hillsdale College offers a free, online training series named Constitution 101.  After initially enrolling, participants are sent to a contextualized donation opportunity where we ask for a gift to help support the efforts of Hillsdale.
There had been complaints recently that the contextualized donation page gave the impression that the course was not free.  We wanted to alleviate those concerns while not reducing the revenue or conversion rate of the page.
experiments.keyLearnings: The addition of navigation decreased conversion rate by 17%. Whenever we offer donors the opportunity to explore more areas of the website, we are increasing the likelihood that they will.  While we were trying to decrease anxiety, we inadvertently increased the friction associated with making the gift.

experiments.title: How increasing the clarity of the value proposition affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a more direct copy approach that increases clarity improve donor conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/01/2015 - 12/02/2015
Harvest Ministries was planning to send fundraising emails on Giving Tuesday. They had crafted an email to the housefile that utilized Greg Laurie’s traditional storytelling style, but wanted to see if a more direct approach that educated their donors about Giving Tuesday would increase results. They tested a new subject line, but also new email copy that took a more direct approach to see if increased clarity improved donor conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings: This showed that our donors respond financially when we are straightforward and direct. This 60% lift in revenue was a product of a subject line test that slightly increased open rate and a copy test that increased both clickthrough rate and donor motivation, as evidenced by this experiment.

experiments.title: How the power of match language affects conversion

rows.name: "Double My Impact" Call to Action Copy
Action Oriented Call to Action Copy

experiments.researchQuestion: We hypothesized that because of the urgent message and the clear impact of the campaign, more donors would respond to a call-to-action that showed the impact of their money.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/03/2013 - 06/06/2013
Americans For Prosperity ran a one-day fundraising campaign that had a matching gift it — a generous donor had offered to double every gift. These are common in fundraising, and are usually a boost to any campaign — but we wondered how much the match actually incentivized the gift. Would more people give if they knew their gift was being matched, or would more people give to support the outcome they wanted?
We devised a test to see which treatment would convert more visitors to donors: match language or impact language?
experiments.keyLearnings: The new call-to-action decreased donor conversion 26.6%, showing that “doubling the gift” is a powerful motivational incentive. This led to a focused effort to secure more matching gifts for online campaigns and continuously promoting the match throughout the fundraising initiative.

experiments.title: How removing an exit-intent pop-up on a donation page impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will removing the exit-intent pop-up impact conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/02/2018 - 01/26/2018
On Focus on the Family’s primary donation page, they use an exit-intent pop-up that appears on the page as you are about to leave. During their year-end giving campaign, we tested removing this feature. We found that by removing the exit-intent pop-up, we were able to increase donor conversion because the audience was highly motivated to give. Understanding these learnings, we also wanted to test this approach outside of year-end when motivations were more neutral. During the month of January, we tested removing the exit-intent pop-up from the primary donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings: After four weeks of running this experiment and analyzing the data, we found that having an exit-intent pop-up on the primary donation page doesn't significantly impact conversion - positively or negatively outside of high-urgency campaigns. The conversion rate throughout the month fluctuated week by week telling us that there is no real difference in conversion with an exit-intent pop-up.

experiments.title: How removing elements of friction and strengthening the value proposition impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Which donation page would increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/07/2017 - 09/26/2017
The primary donation page for FamilyLife used a large image at the top of it with their traditional value proposition copy on it. They recently developed their value proposition and wanted to test a radical redesign of their primary donation page that tested this copy and removed other elements of the page.
experiments.keyLearnings: The radical redesign increased donor conversion by 14.3%. By removing elements of friction on the page and strengthening their value proposition, we were able to not only increase the number of people giving a gift, but we were able to also motivate them to give more generously resulting in a 94.4% increase (77% valid) in revenue as well because the average gift increased.

experiments.title: How suggested gift amounts affects donations
experiments.researchQuestion: Does suggesting gift amounts based upon previous giving increase the revenue and conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/16/2015 - 06/22/2015
As part of their fiscal year end campaign, Dallas Theological Seminary wanted to find a way to lower the number of decisions donors had to make when giving their gift. The idea was the suggest a gift amount based upon their previous giving history with the hypothesis that this would lower the friction of the giving form.
To test this hypothesis, we set up an experiment to split traffic between their standard blank gift amount field and a form that would populate three different buttons based upon their last gift amount (this was passed in via the querystring).
experiments.keyLearnings:
By suggesting a gift amount, the overall donation page conversion dropped by 54.9%. This was a surprising result given the success of this type of experiment with other research partners.
This outcome taught us something significant about DTS donors. The average gift for their online donors is significantly higher than the most other nonprofits.  As a result, the suggested gift amounts were far larger than would normally be seen on a donation page.  It is our hypothesis that these larger gift options scared away potential donors to the campaign by presuming too much.  We get better results when we let the donor determine their own level of generousity.

experiments.title: How the location of a success indicator affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Which design treatment will produce the highest conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/23/2015 - 07/01/2015
The donation page is the most critical step in online fundraising. Any improvement in the conversion rate on the page, even minor, will lead to increased revenue. During Dallas Theological Seminary’s fiscal year end campaign, we wanted to see if the placement of the campaign progress meter would affect the conversion rate on the form.
Our goal was to gain insight into the donor’s thought sequence but determining where an element that shows process towards a goal should be placed, right at the top of the form or after the impact of a donation has been stated?
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment was able to statistically validate but given the short timeframe and the relatively low sample size, we would want to run this experiment again to be certain of these results.  The increased motivation of donors towards the end of a fiscal year end campaign may have impacted the overall performance of the test.

experiments.title: How the wrong value proposition can affect conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Which donation page treatment will collect the highest number of donors and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/10/2015 - 06/22/2015
The primary donation form for Dallas Theological Seminary (DTS) had remained consistent for the last several years. It was a well-designed form but it made the assumption that those donors that make it to the page are already convinced to give their gift. This was evident from the lack of any copy or significant calls to action at the top of the form.
In an effort to increase the perceived value of the donation to potential donors, we decided to create a new treatment. This new page would utilize the same donation form but would add copy to the top that discussed one of the unique value propositions of DTS. It also included quotes from well known figures in the Christian community supporting the work of DTS.
experiments.keyLearnings: The new donation form treatment resulted in a 28.1% decrease in donor conversion. This would indicate that the value proposition communicated on the page did not appeal to the potential donors. Additionally, by adding the wrong copy and pushing the donation form further down on the page, we introduced friction into the donation process. These two factors combined to reduce the donors and revenue to the page.

experiments.title: How selling the value proposition in the email affects response rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Adding additional copy around the value proposition of Heritage will increase the visitors to the page and the overall number of donors once they get there.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/31/2014 - 01/01/2015
The end of the year deadline serves as a way to increase the urgency around the year end campaign. As the clock ticks closer to midnight on the 31st, there is increased urgency to give.  With that increased urgency, we wondered if it was still necessary to sell the value of Heritage to the reader in the email or would a shorter email get them there faster. So we decided to test that.
experiments.keyLearnings: While the test did not validate, it would indicate that the additional value proposition is not necessary so close to the deadline.

experiments.title: How increasing the clarity of the value proposition affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Which of the three value propositions, backed by evidentials, will increase both average gift and conversion rate the greatest amount?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/30/2014 - 07/20/2014
Despite many experiments, the control version of the main Heritage Foundation donation form had consistently maintained the highest conversion rate. In an effort to influence both conversion rate and average gift, we developed and tested multiple value propositions and backed them with evidentials.
experiments.keyLearnings: The research aspect of Heritage's efforts had the biggest impact on conversion rate. The main donation form will use this language moving forward and this aspect of Heritage's mission will be emphasized in future campaigns when communicating with donors.

experiments.title: How a campaign progress meter affects the average donation amount
experiments.researchQuestion: How does the use of a progress meter impact overall giving?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/30/2015 - 07/01/2015
During their fiscal year end campaign, Hillsdale College had set a target revenue total as a way of establishing the need. Historically, we had used a progress meter found at the top of the page as a way of communicating both the goal and the progress toward it. As the end of the campaign approached, we wanted to determine if these progress meters had the impact on revenue that we assumed they did. Knowing this would help us with future campaigns.
experiments.keyLearnings: The test was not able to reach a statistically valid level before the end of the campaign but, it was quickly approaching validity. With this in mind, we would hypothesize that the progress meter did have an impact on the overall revenue of the campaign. There did not seem to be much of an impact to donor conversion but those that did give, gave at a higher amount.

experiments.title: How a testing into a new landing page affects donations
experiments.researchQuestion: Which donation landing page will produce the greatest amount of donors and dollars?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/01/2015 - 06/08/2015
Hillsdale College had recently launched a new microsite to handle all of their online giving. The new microsite had a variation of their standard design but made it easier to create contextualized donation forms and took into account certain principles we learned from previous experiments such as removing banners and navigation, adding value proposition language, and an easier to use donation form.
One of their more traffic donation forms was a general donation form for their online classes.  Each class has its own donation form but, to handle general website traffic, we wanted one that could be applicable to all courses.  Before making the switch to the new microsite, we wanted to validate that the new page would achieve the lift in conversion rate and revenue that we were expecting.
experiments.keyLearnings: The learnings achieved through previous experiments held true with this new landing page and help achieve a 35.8% increase in donation conversion rate. The experiment was a good example of leveraging the learnings from multiple experiments to craft a new, more effective landing page.

experiments.title: How clarifying the value proposition on a donation page affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Which value proposition is most appealing to donors to increase the donor conversion and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/19/2015 - 06/05/2015
For Hillsdale College, the Constitution 101 online course has proven to be a great source of new email addresses and new donors for their file.  In particular, the landing page that is shown after someone signs up has the highest volume of traffic compared to their other donation offers. As a result, this page has been the focus of multiple testing iterations over the past several months.
The first big lift to conversion rate came from modifying the value proposition displayed on the original page. After that initial experiment, we tried experimenting with different tactical elements of the page. None of these experiments were able to boost results.
Since the value proposition was proven to be the best way to boost results so we wanted to try refining this value proposition even further. We created a new copy treatment that removed the organizational goals and instead explored the reasoning for the course in greater depth.
experiments.keyLearnings: By removing the organizational goal of having 50 million people take the class and instead emphasizing why it is so critical that the class be offered for free, we were able to boost conversion rate by 22.6%. This is a good example of examining the message through the eyes of the donor to focus in on what is most important to them.  The “50 million people” goal wasn’t necessarily bad but this test proves it was the deeper value of the course that they valued more.

experiments.title: How strengthening the value proposition through a radical redesign affects donations
experiments.researchQuestion: Which page layout and value proposition best presents the value proposition and minimizes friction and anxiety to optimize donor conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/12/2015 - 02/23/2015
Hillsdale College’s popular Constitution 101 online course is supported by donations from individuals, many of whom donate after taking the course. Immediately after signing up for the course (or any of their other courses), registrants are given the opportunity to donate.  The original donation page contained multiple links to other parts of the site, concentrated in two different navigational areas. It also gave them an immediate opt-out opportunity — by clicking the link (in the second paragraph of copy) “I cannot make a donation at this time”. A single line of copy asked the user to support the course, making the assumption that the visitor already understood the value of Hillsdale College. Additionally, the page focused on a premium book, the Constitution Reader, and used most of the available language to sell the value proposition of that item.
In order to improve donor conversion on this page, Hillsdale wanted to test a simplified design that expanded and highlighted the value proposition of Hillsdale College rather than the premium associated with their gift.
The new treatment page took away all of the navigation menus — creating a “cul-de-sac”, where the visitor either continues through the page or closes the window. The value proposition language focuses on the goal of the course and the intended outcome — educating 50 million Americans on Constitutional founding principles. The Constitution Reader offer is reduced to a secondary, value-added ask.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The two donation forms resulted in nearly identical revenue — but the new treatment led to a 33.3% increase in conversion rate. Since the primary goal of the page is the acquisition of new donors, the increased conversion rate made the new treatment the winner.
The discrepancy in average gifts between the pages did lead to some interesting observations.  After further examination of the individual transactions, we learned that the new treatment was getting lower average gifts than the control. This led us to believe that the three options in the new treatment’s gift array may be too much of a stretch for most people.  As a result, they are using the “Other amount” option.  This is an example of “unsupervised thinking” which is leading to the visitors entering lower gift amounts than we would prefer.
As a result, Hillsdale’s next experiment will be a test of the gift array to see if lowering the initial ask can lead to an overall increased average gift.

experiments.title: How live chat on a donation page affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: How does live chat affect donation conversion rate and average gift amount?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/05/2015 - 02/04/2015
The Heritage Foundation tested a Live Chat feature on their donation page as a way to boost engagement with the potential donors. The audience was split between existing members and nonmembers in order to monitor how it affected each audience.  The results below are for nonmembers only.
experiments.keyLearnings: The segment targeted at existing donors showed no significant difference between the segment of visitors seeing live chat and those not seeing it.  For new donors, the live chat dropped the conversion rate slightly but was able to increase the average gift by 21.6%.  While it is not statistically significant, this test directionally indicates that live chat can increase the average gift.  Further testing would be necessary to validate this hypothesis.

experiments.title: How message tone of an email affects response rate
experiments.researchQuestion: How does messaging tone affect donation response rate in an email appeal?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/21/2015 - 01/22/2015
As  part of their new monthly email appeal strategy, Hillsdale College wanted to learn which messaging tone produced the best response rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment utilized two distinct emails and two distinct landing pages. In the experiment, the softer tone email generated 257% increase in click through rate. This increase in traffic illustrates a concept that we have observed in other experiments.  By reducing the “sharpness” of the ask in the email, we can increase the volume of traffic to the landing page, however the visitors will be less motivated causing the on-page conversion rate to be lower than the Control.   However, if the increae in traffic is significant enough, the net result will be more revenue.
The softer tone treatment delivered a response rate 85% higher than the original control with a 40% higher average gift. This experiment is a great example of the importance of analyzing the entire conversion pathway, from email delivery to successful gift, when determining a winner.

experiments.title: Can gift array preselection increase revenue for paid social content offers with persuasion-heavy messaging?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believed that testing lower and higher gift arrays with the second gift amount preselected for content offer audiences would achieve higher revenue per visitor without materially reducing average gift value because anchoring donors to a mid-tier option could balance conversion volume and gift size.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/19/2025 - 12/01/2025
The Hoover Institution is a public policy research organization that advances scholarship on individual, economic, and political freedom, relying on donor support to sustain its work. As part of its fundraising program growth goals, Hoover is testing donation form configurations that can increase revenue per visitor while maintaining healthy conversion rates across acquisition channels.
This experiment is part of a broader Preselect/Array testing program evaluating how gift array size and preselection influence conversion and revenue outcomes. The test combined two gift arrays ($ 25, $50, and $100, and $50/100/250) with and without preselecting the second gift amount, resulting in four variants. This read focuses on the “Trickle Down” content offer, which typically runs on paid social and attracts a more prospect-heavy audience.
experiments.keyLearnings:
For the “Trickle Down” offer, results were directional and not statistically valid, and performance patterns diverged from aggregate findings.

📊 Performance metrics
Total samples: 1,085
 	Total conversions: 50
 	Overall conversion rate: 4.6%
 	Overall average gift: $45.30
 	Control ($50/100/250, no select):
 	Conversion rate: 4.3%
 	Average gift: $39.58
 	$25/50/100, no select:
 	Conversion rate: 4.7%
 	Average gift: $52.31
 	Revenue per visitor: $2.44
 	$50/100/250, select 100:
 	Conversion rate: 5.4% (+25.5% vs. control)
 	Average gift: $56.33
 	Revenue per visitor: $3.06 (highest variant)
 	$25/50/100, select 50:
 	Conversion rate: 4.0%
 	Average gift: $26.50
Offer and channel context
Traffic is driven primarily by paid social
 	Sample size is small relative to required validity
 	Contrary to aggregate trends, preselecting the higher gift array performed best on both conversion rate and revenue per visitor
 	This deviation may be influenced by the nature of the offer or donation page copy, which could be priming donors toward higher gift amounts
Statistical validity
Results did not reach statistical significance
 	Confidence levels were below the 95% threshold required for validation

experiments.title: How does a tabbed form on acquisition donation pages impact donor conversion rate and revenue?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a tabbed form for users on Time of Grace’s acquisition donation pages will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate and revenue because it helps remove unnecessary friction and choose how much they’d like to give after clarifying how often they’d like to give.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/10/2025 - 07/16/2025
We’ve seen a tabbed donation form perform well for Time of Grace on their main donation page. We hypothesized that applying the same experience to their acquisition donation pages, receiving Facebook traffic for book premiums, would result in a similar increase. We believe that a tabbed form removes unnecessary friction, making it clear the type of gift a donor is making, which by extension can better clarify how much they may like to give and how often.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:The tabbed donation form on Time of Grace's acquisition donation pages resulted in a significant increase in transactions, with a 26.0% increase in Transactions for All Traffic. However, a larger takeaway is that revenue experienced a substantial 64.7% increase with a 100% level of confidence. This is a very promising result that shows the potential impact of the tabbed donation form on donor contributions.

In future experimentation, it is important to continue testing the tabbed donation form on acquisition donation pages to further validate the positive impact on donor conversion rates and revenue. It may be beneficial to run the experiment for a longer duration to ensure a higher level of statistical confidence in the results. Additionally, incorporating learnings from this experiment, such as the design elements and user experience of the tabbed form, into future tests can help optimize the donation process and improve overall performance.

experiments.title: How using donor autonomy language during a year-end campaign impacts email revenue.
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using donor autonomy language for active donors will achieve a change in email donation conversions..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/13/2024 - 12/31/2024
Avila is an organization that aims to draw Christians worldwide into deeper union with Christ through mystagogically oriented spiritual education and formation.
This year during their year-end campaign, Avila was promoting their largest-ever match, with a triple match as well. We wanted to see whether using donor autonomy language–allowing the donor to make a choice to give–throughout the entire campaign would make a difference. We ran this copy test on their active donor audience, through a total of 8 emails.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that using donor autonomy language for active donors may have a positive impact on donation conversions, as evidenced by the 4.3% increase in donations for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 21.9%, which is below the threshold of 85% for results to be considered valid.

In future experimentation, it is essential to ensure a larger sample size to improve the statistical significance of the results. Additionally, it would be beneficial to explore other factors that may influence donation conversions among active donors, such as the timing of the campaign, the messaging used, or the specific calls to action.

Overall, while the initial findings suggest a potential benefit of using donor autonomy language, further research is needed to confirm these results and make informed decisions for future campaigns.

experiments.title: How campaign-related copy on the Tribute donation page impacts revenue during a holiday appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that campaign-related copy for users on the Tribute donation page will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because the value prop is expanded upon, greater urgency is tied to the campaign, and the donor has a sense of how their gift fits into the December goals.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/17/2024 - 12/18/2024
During CaringBridge’s holiday appeal, the Tribute Donation page largely remains the same. Aside from brief mentions of the match, the copy is stripped down, short, and doesn’t tie into the larger goals of the organization – instead focusing more on a donor’s gift to the individual. We believed that by using the campaign donation page on Tribute, tying to the larger December goals and reinforcing the match more, it would increase average gift and donor conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that implementing campaign-related copy on the Tribute donation page led to a 3.6% increase in revenue for all traffic as well as a slight bump in donations overall. While this result is promising, the statistical level of confidence was only 70.7%, below the 85% threshold for validity.

However, it is an encouraging first step to continue testing on the Tribute donation page, which historically, has been treated very separate from the rest of CaringBridge's campaign and goals – because users are there for a specific user. To have this broadened and more general appeal copy not result in a decrease, but also perhaps have a slight positive increase, could open up door for continued testing.

experiments.title: How campaign-related copy on the Tribute donation page impacts revenue during a holiday appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that campaign-related copy for users on the Tribute donation page will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because the value prop is expanded upon, greater urgency is tied to the campaign, and the donor has a sense of how their gift fits into the December goals.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/17/2024 - 12/18/2024
During CaringBridge’s holiday appeal, the Tribute Donation page largely remains the same. Aside from brief mentions of the match, the copy is stripped down, short, and doesn’t tie into the larger goals of the organization – instead focusing more on a donor’s gift to the individual. We believed that by using the campaign donation page on Tribute, tying to the larger December goals and reinforcing the match more, it would increase average gift and donor conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
