The key learning from this experiment is that implementing campaign-related copy on the Tribute donation page led to a 3.6% increase in revenue for all traffic as well as a slight bump in donations overall. While this result is promising, the statistical level of confidence was only 70.7%, below the 85% threshold for validity.

However, it is an encouraging first step to continue testing on the Tribute donation page, which historically, has been treated very separate from the rest of CaringBridge's campaign and goals – because users are there for a specific user. To have this broadened and more general appeal copy not result in a decrease, but also perhaps have a slight positive increase, could open up door for continued testing.

experiments.title: How does customizing a gift array based on a donor’s giving history impact revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that customizing the gift array for donors will achieve a higher revenue per donor because the amount is specific to their giving history.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/27/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas.
For PTV’s year-end efforts, we decided to test the gift array on our donation page.
The control gift array for everyone is $25, $50, $100, $200
We used the donor’s HPC (highest previous contribution) or largest gift as the base for the treatment. Then we multiplied the amount by 1.5, 2, 2.5, 3.
To execute this experiment
This organization uses HubSpot as its email marketing platform and Anedot as a donation platform, but this experiment is possible with other platforms.
First, we created new calculated contact properties within HubSpot, which took the largest gift amount and multiplied it by 1.5, 2, 2.5, and 3. We also had the contact property round up to the nearest dollar so the donor would not see random amounts with cents.
Second, we reviewed Anedot’s documentation on how to pass the custom amounts.
This test can still be conducted for organizations that do not have these platforms.
Use a spreadsheet to calculate the custom amounts and upload them to your email marketing platform as new fields. Then consult your donation platform documentation to see if/how to pass custom amounts.
If your donation platform does not allow custom amounts, you could break this test into buckets based on HPC. For instance, $1-50, $51-100, $101-150, $151-200, etc, or in whatever buckets best fit your organization. Then, you can AB test by sending half of the bucket to the control and half to a different donation page with a gift array that best fits that bucket’s range.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that customizing the gift array for donors based on their giving history can potentially lead to an increase in revenue per donor. In this particular case, the treatment with customized gift amounts based on the donor's highest previous contribution saw a 40.6% increase in revenue for all traffic.
However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence was only 79.5%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 85%. This means that the results of the experiment cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.
We will be conducting further analysis on this test to see how much more individuals gave based on the gift array.

experiments.title: How a dynamic impact callout on the tributes donation array impacts conversion rate and revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a dynamic impact array for users on the tribute donation page will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate and revenue because it encourages interaction with the array and clearly outlines the impact of a donor’s gift amount.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/02/2024 - 12/03/2024
For CaringBridge’s Giving Tuesday match, we hypothesized that a dynamic impact array that automatically updated impact language based on the user’s gift selection would increase revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the dynamic impact array tested on the tribute donation page led to a 3.5% increase in Transactions for All Traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 31.7%, which is below the standard threshold of 85%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.
In future experimentation, it is essential to ensure that the sample size is large enough to generate statistically significant results. Additionally, it is crucial to consider running the experiment for a longer duration to capture more diverse data and account for any external factors that may impact the outcome.
While the initial results of the dynamic impact array seem promising, it is important to validate these findings with a more rigorous experiment before implementing any changes based on this data. Further testing with a larger sample size and a longer duration will provide more reliable insights into the impact of the dynamic impact array on donor conversion rate and revenue.

experiments.title: How using donor autonomous language on an instant donation page affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Using donor autonomous language on an instant donation page after an individual signs up for a free online course will increase the donation amount because people won’t feel marketed to, but realize they have the power to make the decision.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/04/2024 - 10/28/2024
Dallas Theological Seminary wants to reach more people worldwide with biblical-truth and teach people how to study the Bible. To this aim, they offer free online courses. All their courses are funded by generous donors. After a person signs up for the Revelation course they are re-directed to an instant donation page. This page thanks them for signing up and then asks then to donate.
In an attempt to increase the donor amount on this page, they wanted to test whether donor autonomous language would lower the barrier to donating and in turn increase the amount of donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that using donor autonomous language on an instant donation page after an individual signs up for a free online course can potentially increase the donation amount. While the treatment did see a 6.3% increase in Revenue for All Traffic, the statistical level of confidence was only 64.5%, which is below the standard threshold of 95%.
In future experimentation, it is important to consider the statistical significance of the results before drawing conclusions. It may be beneficial to repeat the experiment with a larger sample size or make adjustments to the donation page to further test the impact of donor autonomous language. Additionally, it could be useful to explore other factors that may influence donation amounts, such as the design of the donation page or the timing of the donation request. By continuing to test and analyze different variables, Dallas Theological Seminary can gather more reliable data to inform their decision-making process and optimize their donation strategy.

experiments.title: How adding an icon to the donation page showing the number of gifts made so far impacts revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a badge icon to the Tribute tab for users on a site profile will achieve an increase in gifts and revenue to the tribute widget because it makes this tab more visible, highlights its importance, and drives more users to the widget..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/09/2024 - 09/13/2024
On CaringBridge profiles, the Tribute widget is a significant source of donations and revenue. A tribute donation is a gift to CaringBridge that a user makes in tribute to the profile they are visiting. This widget appears in a few different locations on the site, but historically one of the highest-performing locations has been on the Tribute page itself. This tab shows a list of tributes that have been made on the user’s profile, it includes copy “Join the X donors who have made a donation in honor of [name].
Since migration to CB2, CaringBridge has seen low performance on this widget. We hypothesized that by driving more traffic to the tab, it would increase donor conversion rate and revenue. On the old site, this Tributes tab was it’s own designated page and the button to navigate to it included a badge icon with how many tributes were on the profile. We created two treatments, one that’s a static badge icon and another where the same badge icon animates and counts up to the total number of tributes.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a badge icon to the Tribute tab on site profiles significantly increased Revenue - Tribute from the widget by 106.7%. Additionally, Tribute Tab clicks experienced a substantial increase of 9,5881%. These results were statistically significant with confidence levels of 95.6% and 100% respectively.

Moving forward, it is clear that showcasing the importance of the Tribute tab by making it more visible and engaging users with a badge icon can lead to a significant boost in donations and revenue. This insight should be taken into consideration for future experimentation and site optimizations. By continuing to test and iterate on features that drive engagement with the Tribute widget, CaringBridge can further enhance user experience and ultimately increase donor conversion rates.

experiments.title: How does calling out the CTA at the top of an email impact revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by providing the CTA at the very top of an email for email subscribers will achieve a higher gifts and revenue because it highlights what is most important without much reading.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/22/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas
Each month, PTV promotes a different book in an effort to encourage donors to give as an incentive
For July, we released a brand new kids book by Dr. Jeffress.
We received an email from another company that used a text box at the top of the email that plainly and briefly gave the CTA in case the reader did not have time to read the entire email.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that providing the call-to-action (CTA) at the very top of an email did not yield the desired results. In fact, the treatment group, which received the CTA at the top of the email, saw a 24.8% decrease in revenue compared to the control group.

It is important to note that the statistical level of confidence was below the 85% threshold, which means the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision-making. This indicates that the decrease in revenue may not be solely attributed to the placement of the CTA at the top of the email.

In future experimentation, it is important to continue testing different strategies to optimize the placement of the CTA in emails. It may be beneficial to test variations in the messaging, design, or timing of the CTA to determine what resonates best with donors and drives the desired actions. Additionally, considering other factors that could have impacted the results, such as the content of the email or the timing of the campaign, could provide further insights for future experiments.

experiments.title: How a higher contrast donation button impacts clicks and overall giving
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a higher contrast button on the donate button for homepage visitors will achieve an increase in clicks and overall giving because the higher contrast button would attract the visitors attention quicker therefore reducing friction on the page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/03/2024 - 05/29/2024
In an effort to push more traffic to IMB’s Home Donation Page, we tested using a higher contrast button to attract visitor’s attention.
This type of test has resulted in a lift in donor conversion metrics for other clients, so we were excited to see how it performed.
Our goal was that by using a higher contrast button color, more visitors would click the button to be directed to the donation page, therefore, increasing giving.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using a higher contrast button on the donate button for homepage visitors resulted in a 4.3% increase in transactions for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 27.3%, which means that these results are not considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to achieve a higher level of statistical confidence. Additionally, it may be beneficial to run the experiment for a longer period of time to gather more data and potentially increase the validity of the results.

Overall, while the initial results show promise, it is essential to rely on statistically significant data to make informed decisions about implementing changes to the website.

experiments.title: How reducing the size of the Express Checkout button on donation pages impacted revenue and donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing the size of the Express Checkout button for visitors to our video series acquisition donation pages will achieve an increase in donor conversion because the smaller button will provide less of a competition for the donor’s attention as they’re making their gift, and this means that the friction will be reduced.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/11/2024 - 06/26/2024
The team at Focus on the Family (FOTF) has been testing various elements of their donation pages to determine which factors could improve donor conversion and revenue. One element that we wanted to test was the Express Checkout button that appears in the donation form below the gift array. Users who click this button can choose to donate through Google Pay and the idea is that this makes the donation process easier and thus increases donor conversion. However, we wondered if this button could serve as a distraction during the donation process and actually negatively affect donor conversion.
FOTF first tested removing the Express Checkout button on their main donation page and the results were very flat. They then tested whether reducing the Express Checkout button’s size would drive a different outcome. This test found that the smaller button size led to a 21.3% increase in transactions, with a 95.7% level of confidence.
The purpose of this experiment is to see if that lift in transactions will occur when we test the smaller button on FOTF’s video series acquisition donation pages.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results of this test were completely flat; we didn't see any significant changes in revenue or transactions between the control and treatment. This means that, even though we didn't see any statistically-significant improvement on these metrics, the smaller button didn't hurt our donor conversion or average gift.

Because the smaller button saw a positive lift on Focus on the Family's other donation pages -- and did not harm their acquisition donation page conversion -- it's recommended that the smaller button treatment be rolled out on all of their donation pages.

experiments.title: How clarity around the problem and expanded value prop on main donate impacts revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increase clarity around the problem and expanding value prop copy to specify how RTB is the solution for users on the donation page will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because users will not only have better clarity around the problem RTB solves but also the impact a gift makes towards solving it.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/22/2024 - 04/26/2024
For Reasons to Believe’s main donation page, we believe there was an opportunity to leverage the value prop language we’d seen perform well on acquisition donation page and in campaigns. We hypothesized that adding more clarity around what the problem is and better illustrating how a gift to RTB helps solve it would improve conversion rate. A few specific callouts are the more action-oriented headline versus “Support Reasons to Believe. The inclusion of details about what types of questions RTB answers, whose writing the resources, and finally what’s really at stake when these questions go unanswered.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that increasing clarity around the problem and expanding the value proposition copy to specify how Reasons to Believe addresses the users' needs can significantly impact donor conversion rates and revenue generated. While donor conversion rate remained similar between control and treatment, the latter did show a substantial 103.7% increase in revenue for all traffic, the statistical level of confidence was only at 84.5%, below the accepted threshold of 85% for validity.

Moving forward, it is important to continue testing variations of the value proposition copy and messaging to determine the most effective way to communicate the impact of donations to Reasons to Believe. Additionally, conducting experiments with larger sample sizes or running the test for a longer period may help increase the statistical confidence level of the results. Overall, the results of this experiment suggest that focusing on clearly articulating the problem and how RTB provides a solution can have a positive impact on donor conversions.

experiments.title: How adding autonomy to a “Wiki” banner impacts revenue V.1
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a decision point to make an impact for people who land on the homepage during a HUC will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the donor has complete autonomy to click “yes” and participate or “no” and close out of the ask.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/07/2024 - 03/13/2024
Would adding a question and a simple decision to make an impact increase donor conversion on the homepage campaign wiki banner? By adding a level of autonomy to the donor experience and giving them “control” to say “Yes, I’d like to make an impact” or “No, I’m not interested today,” would we invite more participation in the campaign and insight additional donor conversions over our “regular” campaign messaging with a gift array?
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that adding a question and a simple decision to make an impact on the homepage campaign wiki banner resulted in a 21.5% increase in revenue for all traffic. This suggests that giving donors the autonomy to choose whether they want to participate or not can positively impact donor conversion rates. Further investigation will need to be done in order to see if the increase in revenue is actually related to the experiment or if there is a large gift that needs to be discluded.

experiments.title: How adding a side column ad in TDS articles affects transactions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a digital content offer in the sidebar of TDS article pages for TDS readers will achieve an increase in transactions and emails acquired because they are receiving relevant content that brings more value to their visit.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/26/2024 - 03/05/2024
We added an ad for our Founders Almanac eBook offer in the side column of TDS articles. Our hypothesis was that adding an additional digital offer alongside our Guide to the Constitution Dear Reader ads would boost conversions for email sign ups and donations. There was a lift in email sign-ups but a non-validated decrease in transactions and revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:

1. The addition of a digital content offer in the sidebar of TDS article pages led to a decrease in revenue for all traffic by 18.4%, which was not statistically significant at a 52.1% confidence level. This means that there was not enough evidence to conclude that the digital offer had a negative impact on revenue.

2. However, there was a significant increase in Almanac Button Clicks by 1% and Submit Founders' Almanac Form submissions by 5441%. These results suggest that TDS readers were engaging more with the digital content offer.

3. The results of this experiment should not be used for future decision-making due to the low level of statistical confidence (below 85%).

In future experimentation, it is important to conduct tests with a larger sample size and for a longer duration to ensure the results are statistically valid. Additionally, it would be beneficial to analyze user behavior and feedback to understand the impact of digital content offers on user experience and engagement. It is also crucial to continue testing different types of offers and placements to determine the most effective strategy for increasing email sign-ups and donations without negatively impacting revenue.

experiments.title: Does mentioning secure in a direct appeal email impact revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding the word “secure” for subscribers will achieve a higher conversion and revenue impact because of the additional perceived security.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/31/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas.
Each month PTV promotes a different premium to its housefile in an effort to acquire new donors and retain existing donors.
For this email, we decided to test adding the word “secure” when asking the subscriber to make their gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding the word "secure" when asking subscribers to make their gift can potentially have a positive impact on conversion and revenue. Although the results of this experiment are not statistically valid, they do show a significant increase in revenue for the treatment group.

Revenue impact, treatment saw an increase of 63%. 

Donation impact, treatment saw a increase of 33%.

The treatment version also saw more larger gifts made from the link within the PS (7 gifts vs 3 gifts)

Based on these findings, it would be recommended to further test the use of the word "secure" in future experiments. This could involve testing it with different segments of the audience, using it in different parts of the email, or testing variations of the word itself.

By conducting further experiments and gathering more data, it will be possible to determine with more certainty whether adding the word "secure" consistently leads to increased conversion and revenue. This will help inform future email campaigns and potentially increase the effectiveness of fundraising efforts.

experiments.title: How adding an image of the book impacted revenue for nondonors
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by including an image of the book for nondonors will achieve a higher revenue impact because of the visual appeal of the book.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/30/2023
Pathway to Victory is a broadcast ministry of First Dallas.
Each month PTV promotes a new premium for the month. For this month we decided to retest using an image of the book to see if it would increase revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that including an image of the book for non-donors did not achieve a higher revenue impact. In fact, the treatment group saw a slight decrease in revenue compared to the control group. However, since the statistical level of confidence is below 85%, these results cannot be considered valid.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to achieve a higher level of statistical confidence. Additionally, it may be helpful to further explore the reasons behind the decrease in revenue for the treatment group. This could involve conducting qualitative research or gathering feedback from participants to better understand their preferences and reactions to the image of the book.

Overall, the learnings from this experiment suggest that including an image of the book may not be the most effective strategy for increasing revenue. It would be beneficial to consider other potential variables or elements to test in future experiments in order to optimize revenue impact.

experiments.title: How a brief, to the point email impacted revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by shortening the email to the important parts for emails subscribers will achieve a higher conversion rate because there is increased urgency and less copy.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/18/2023
PTV is the global media ministry of First Baptist Dallas.
Each month PTV promotes a book written by Dr. Robert Jeffress. For August, Dr Jeffress release a new book that was a available for only a couple weeks. In an effort to see if we could drive results in the final days, we tested a shorter, straight to the point email vs the standard descriptive version.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the shorter, straight to the point email was more effective in driving conversions and generating revenue compared to the standard descriptive email. Despite the statistical level of confidence being below the threshold of 85%, the treatment still showed a significant 31.1% increase in revenue for all traffic.

In future experimentation, it would be advisable to continue exploring the use of shorter and more concise emails for promotional purposes. This experiment suggests that there is potential in leveraging increased urgency and reducing the amount of copy to drive better results.

However, it is important to note that the results of this particular test should not be used for future decision making due to the low statistical level of confidence. It would be beneficial to run further experiments to validate these findings and ensure their replicability before implementing them as a standard practice.

Additionally, it would be useful to analyze any qualitative feedback or customer insights to better understand the reasons behind the improved performance of the shorter email. This can inform future experiments and help refine the email strategy further.

experiments.title: How a value-driven headline on an acquisition offer landing page impacts email conversion, donor conversion, and revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that incorporating reader-focused value proposition language for visitors to the Cherish video series landing page will achieve an increase in email conversions because the reader will have a better understanding of what the video series is and how it will help them in their own life. because the reader will have a better understanding of what the video series is and how it will help them in their own life.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/16/2023 - 06/10/2023
Focus on the Family is a Christian ministry that serves families worldwide. Every year, hundreds of thousands of people sign up for their free video series’ to learn more about strengthening marriages, families, and faith.
One of their most effective series is Cherish Your Spouse. In reviewing the landing page for this offer, we felt that the headline lacked a strong value for the visitor. We hypothesized that if we changed the headline to clarify the impact of the series on subscribers, it would increase the appeal of the offer and signups on the page, ultimately leading to more donations and revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running this for three weeks, we found that there was no statistically significant impact on email signups from the new headline. Downstream donor conversion actually trended down for the treatment page but we were not able to validate that decrease either. Future tests will need to change more than the headline to have a higher likelihood of resulting in a statistically valid result. Our next test will be to change the body copy to emphasize what subscribers will experience as a result of watching these videos.

experiments.title: How increasing the median amount of a one-time array affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing the gift array amounts  for lamplighter donors will achieve an increase in overall giving without affecting recurring donors and overall donor conversions  because  the increased donation amount doesn’t affect their decision to give a recurring gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/31/2023 - 02/23/2023
Potential donors for Prison Fellowship’s Lamplighters are presented with a tabbed donation form and given the option to give a one time gift or a recurring monthly gift.  The control donation form provides the gift arrays options of $15 (one prisoner)/$60 (4 prisoners)/$165 for an entire class.
We hypothesized that by increasing the median gift array option presented that there might be the potential to maintain the donor conversion rate and raise revenue without affecting recurring donors.   The tested gift array options for the treatment started at $30 to help 2 prisoners/$90 (6 prisoners)/$165 for an entire class.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that increasing the gift array options presented to potential donors of Prison Fellowship's Lamplighters program led to a decrease in overall number of donors; however there was essentially no effect on recurring donors in the control vs. the treatment.  As expected there was increased revenue for the treatment which presented the higher gift array including a large gift of $4,971.  When removing the large gift, the treatment received a 13% increase in revenue.
When separating out the one time and recurring donors and eliminating the large gift, the test validated for all donors and one time donors, but did not validate for recurring donors.  There was essentially no effect on recurring donors.  There were 14 recurring donors in the control and 12 in the treatment; the average gift for the recurring donors in the treatment was slightly higher - $37 vs. $33 in the control.
When focusing on one time donors we see that treatment resulted in 21% fewer donors with 20% more revenue.  Interestingly both the Control and the Treatment garnered 4 gifts of $165.  The Treatment did have 2 - $200 gifts which that plus 3 gifts in the $90 range resulted in the slight lift in revenue for the treatment's one time donors.
The higher conversion rate resulting in 25% more donors  indicates the control as the winner even considering the lower revenue that was generated.

experiments.title: How communicating the amount of a donation goes to the organization’s mission impacts overall revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: How will including the percentage of a donor’s gift that goes directly towards the organization’s mission impact donor conversion and overall revenue?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/26/2021
Focus on the Family was sending an email appeal and recently adjusted the percentage of a donor’s gift amount that goes directly to ministry versus overhead cost for their organization. Historically, they had never communicated that to their donors. With the new higher percentage of the donor’s gift going directly to their organization, they wanted to test adding this message to the body copy of the donation page to see if they might be able to increase donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings: While there was no valid lift in donor conversion, we did see a directional increase in overall revenue of 33%! By communicating to people that we are being good stewards of their money and that their gift now goes even further towards ministry impact, donors were even more generous and gave larger gifts than when we didn't communicate that to them. This tells us that this principle is important to donors, but that it doesn't impact the number of donors who convert, but instead it does impact the generosity of their gift.

experiments.title: How “rally together” messaging impacted donations for a recurring giving program
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that “rally together” messaging for potential donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because donors will want to unite against the threats to Texas independence and be a part of the efforts to combat those threats.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/11/2023 - 02/17/2023
A public policy client has struggled to get traction on their recurring giving donation page. The page is producing a 0.29 instant donor conversion rate heading into this experiment. Assessing the current donation page copy, we wondered if it is too general and does not do a good enough job of inciting emotion among potential donors. We also wondered if page visitors would convert at a higher rate if we apply relevant language to the current public policy landscape in Texas and the threats to the people of the state.
We’ll develop a donation page treatment that uses “rally together” language, highlighting specific threats to Texas and its citizens and making it clear that these threats won’t seize unless we come together as one to fight for better policy and safeguard our personal liberties. The copy will reference current threats and conditions that are close to home and close to the heart of conservative Texans.
We expect this messaging to increase motivation and urgency to join the Come and Take It Society as a monthly donor and make a continuous effort to fight for independence in Texas.
experiments.keyLearnings: Neither the control nor the treatment reached significance. The treatment led to 5 donations to 4 for the control. Revenue was a tick higher for the treatment compared to the control, but the increase in revenue only had a 67.1% level of confidence. It seems the "rally together" messaging did not motivate potential donors to convert to donors in the way that we hypothesized it would. The messaging could be worth testing through a regular offer in which someone can make either a one-time or monthly donation. However, for the recurring ask, the messaging did not do enough to make potential donors give recurring gifts at a rate in which we could trust rolling out the treatment.

experiments.title: How adding a middle giving option to the gift array impacted donation revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a third giving option as an “in-between” amount for monthly and one-time giving will achieve an increase in donor revenue.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/16/2022 - 01/25/2023
The PFI Lamplighters program gives people the opportunity to support prisoners in ways that help them become closer to God. The current one-time and monthly giving options for Lamplighters are $15 to support one prisoner and $165 to support an entire class. We hypothesized that adding a middle amount as a giving option would reduce giving friction, giving donors an additional option that might nestle in nicely between an amount that is too low or not impactful enough and an amount that is too high.
To test this hypothesis, we added a $60 giving option to support four prisoners. We tested this concept already but only for monthly giving. The test resulted in a valid lift in revenue for the treatment with an average gift twice the amount of the control. The average gift for the treatment was $61.52, implying that the added $60 giving option was a common selection among donors.
Let’s see how the concept performed when testing it for monthly AND one-time giving…
experiments.keyLearnings:
The impact on revenue was minimal this time, with the treatment experiencing a 0.71% decrease in revenue but with only a 51% level of confidence. The average gift for the treatment was $39.08 and $46.95 for the control. You'll recall that when we ran the experiment only for monthly giving, the average gift was just over $60.
This tells us that adding the $60 giving option to the one-time giving gift array did not influence as much giving at that giving level as when we added it as an option only for monthly giving.
However, we saw a much greater impact in overall donations. The treatment experienced a 21% lift in total donations at an 89.3% level of confidence. Interestingly enough, the experiment specifically for monthly giving resulted in a slight decrease in donations but a significant increase in revenue.
Having three giving options "low, "middle", and "high" amounts likely reduced donation friction as potential donors did not feel the pressure of giving too little in terms of impact and too much in terms of money. They had a middle option to provide comfort in choice (a happy medium), helping to reduce anxiety. This led to more donations.
Even if a donor chose to give less than $60, we believe their desire to give period was still stronger because they didn't feel as if their gift was "small" when comparing it to the next level up, which was $60. The psychological effect may be the opposite for a gift array that had only $15 and $165 options. In this case, potential donors may choose not to give at all because a $15 gift in their minds would be too small or insignificant when compared to the other amount of $165. Ultimately, $60 provides a nice reference point for potential donors.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Mobile visitors had a 43.4% increase in revenue with a 95% level of confidence – which contributed the bulk of the experiment traffic.

experiments.title: How showing an abandoned donation pop-up to users based on entry point affects revenue – homepage version
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that visitors entering the site on the main page will be prompted to complete their donation when presented a pop-up reminder and we will generate more revenue than not showing a pop-up.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/11/2022
Focus on the Family wanted to see if showing a pop-up with an opportunity to complete a donation (from a previously clicked on amount), would change based on the entry point of a visitor. The thought was that user intentions might be different for visitors coming directly to the home page vs visitors entering from other pages.
Prior to this experiment, we first tested if showing a pop-up to visitors (regardless of entry point) would increase overall completed transactions. We built this initial test with a single pop up that was shown to all visitors that visited a donation page, clicked on an amount to give, but abandoned the process after that. The pop-up would show to that visitor as they navigated around on the FOTF site.
For this test, we split the test into two separate tests. One test focussed on visitors to all non-homepages. The pop up was shown only to folks that came through anywhere but the main page, hit the donate button, clicked an amount…then abandoned the process.
This particular test focused on visitors to the main home page. The pop up behaved exactly the same, except that it only showed to people that visited through the homepage page first. The idea was that people coming through main page would be more familiar and thus more likely to complete a transaction, but they may need more reminders if they happened to go through the donation pathway…and possibly the abandoned donation pop up would serve as that gentle nudge and cause a completed transaction, and thus more revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
By showing a pop-up reminder to complete the transaction, our theory was that those that came to the main page to make a donation, but abandoned for some reason, would potentially be swayed by this gentle nudge. Just like the test on the non-homepage version, When we pulled transaction data, we found quite a few gifts that skewed the results of both the control and the treatment. We decided to remove any donation of $2k or more from both the treatment and the control in order to remove any outliers that might be skewing the results. When we did this, we found the data to be inconclusive with an 11% decrease in the treatment, but with only a 53% Level of Confidence. There is no real positive or negative effect on the results. It is also important to note that experiments for FOTF that run outside of high-urgency time frames behave much differently than during campaign periods.

The key takeaway is that when running transaction experiments that involve revenue, we cannot take the results on face value. We must dig into the numbers deeper, look for any anomalies that can skew results, and then evaluate for lift. In this case, while we did not see a real difference either way (after removing outlier data), we also did not see a potentially harmful outcome. While we cannot recommend that this current variation become the new control, we can recommend to keep testing different variations of the same experiment. Next up is a reduced ask (60% of the originally clicked donation amount), but only on the non-homepage version.

experiments.title: How changing the landing page design affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Which landing page design produces the most donor conversions and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/09/2015 - 06/23/2015
The Heritage Foundation had been using the same landing page design for their search engine marketing (SEM) donor campaigns for the past three years. During those three years, we had run multiple experiments on the main organizational donation page which had gradually evolved into something that looked completely different.
We were in the process of moving the SEM page to the same domain as the main donation form and realized that it would be a good time to validate that the old design was still effective for the SEM audience segment.  We created an experiment by duplicating the page content but displaying it on the two different page designs.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The new design treatment caused an 85.1% drop in revenue by decreasing conversion rate by 67.6% and decreasing average gift by 54.1%. 

The simplified design used in the treatment was developed for a page with minimal copy. When we added the long value proposition text that had been developed for this specific landing page, it became difficult to read.

Landing pages must be designed so that they are easily scannable by the visitor. In this situation, the sub-headlines didn't stand out enough and the lack of page border made the page seem longer than it was.  All of these elements caused enough friction in the mind of the donor to have a negative impact on both conversion rate and average gift.

experiments.title: How increasing the suggested ask amount for a premium offer during year-end impacted revenue
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the suggested ask for donors will achieve a change in revenue.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/20/2021 - 01/04/2022
The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate serve poor and needy people in the United States and more than 70 countries around the world. One of their most popular offers during their end-of-year campaign is to donate a specific amount and have 4 advent candles lit for your intentions at the Shrine of Our Lady of the Snows.
Since year-end is where the majority of online revenue comes in for the Oblates, we wanted to test whether increase the ask for the Advent offer from $50 to $75 would make an impact on revenue from the campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results of this experiment were interesting. Overall, there was a decline in revenue per visitor of 9.5% with a 73% level of confidence. That means that, despite a higher average gift on the $75 experiment version, total revenue was less with the higher ask.

However, the results also varied by the kind of donor that landed on the page. New visitors had a decrease of 33% in revenue with a 96% level of confidence; our hypothesis was that the $75 donation was just too high for new donors.

The story for returning visitors was different, however, with a 28.6% increase in revenue and a 89% level of confidence. Those that knew the work of the Oblates, and were interested in giving again to have advent candles lit for their intentions, were willing to donate at the higher ask.

This is an experiment we will continue to refine in future campaigns.

experiments.title: How adding a $5 minimum gift requirement impacts donor conversion rates
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a minimum donation amount for donors will achieve a change in conversion rates..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/20/2020 - 04/17/2020
The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate serve poor and needy people in the United States and more than 70 countries around the world. One of their most popular digital acquisition offers is the Our Lady of Lourdes digital novena.
Traditionally, there was a premium associated with this offer in an effort to get ebook sign-ups to donate, with an offer of a vial of water from the sacred Lourdes grotto in France as a thank for a donor’s gift. For this experiment, we added a $5 minimum donation to the premium ask to see whether it would have an impact on donor conversion rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Adding in the $5 minimum gift, with language that made it clear the minimum was to cover shipping and handling costs, had a slight increase of 8% in terms of revenue per visitor, with a 72% level of confidence. Moreover, there was a 28% increase in overall donation conversion rates with a 98% level of confidence.

Our hypothesis is that by positioning the minimum gift requirement as a way to make sure the donor's donation is maximized (instead of spent on shipping and handling) led donors to be more generous with their gifts.

This kind of language merits continued testing throughout premium acquisition offers for the Oblates.

experiments.title: How adding value proposition language to a premium landing page impacted donation conversion rates for Pathway to Victory
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding value prop langauge for premium-resource driven donors will achieve a change in conversion rates. .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/13/2022 - 06/03/2022
Pathway to Victory is a ministry who has built a fundraising program around the offer of premiums—books, pamphlets, brochures, etc. Their featured offer in May was Invincible: Conquering the Mountains That Separate You from the Blessed Life.
For this experiment, we wanted to test whether adding in language surrounding PTV’s value proposition results from a recent survey would make an impact on donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
For this specific offer, adding in value proposition language to the landing page for the book led to a decrease in both conversion rates and revenue per donor of 14.2% and 8.3% respectively. Despite an increase in average gift, the overall result was a decline in revenue for the experiment variant page.
Our hypothesis is that the majority of traffic to these book offer pages is driven directly from TV and radio programs promoting the book; those that reach that page want to purchase the book and don't have that much of an interest in value proposition.
However, it will be interesting to keep track of the 'value prop' donors long term, as our hypothesis is that by buying into the value prop of WHY they should donate to PTV instead of another ministry, they will become better long-term donors, and not just those looking to buy one book and that is the extent of their support.

experiments.title: How continuity-based messaging affects recurring donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing continuity-based messaging for people interested in becoming a recurring donor will achieve increased donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/08/2020 - 12/10/2020
CaringBridge had seen experiments where adding a banner with the value proposition “Support 365 days a year” had increased recurring gift conversion on donation pages. This type of language helped the donor understand the continuity of their impact, and helped make the value proposition for a monthly recurring donation by reminding the donor that their impact went far beyond that initial gift.
Since CaringBridge had a recurring-specific funnel triggered by a popup for engaged visitors, they decided to launch this experiment. They added a banner at the top of the monthly page to express this “continuity-based” message to the visitor, and split the traffic to see how it affected conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment with the banner had a directional increase of 16% in converting page visitors to recurring donors. However, this sample size was too small due to the overall low conversion rate, and it did not reach statistical validity.

experiments.title: How adding donor cultivation stories on top of a campaign appeal schedule of emails impacted revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that sending donor stories for year-end campaign recipients will achieve increase revenue.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/10/2020 - 01/02/2021
The Leadership Institute decided to experiment with adding donor stories on top of their regularly scheduled calendar year-end campaign email appeal schedule to experiment with whether or not it inspired more people to give at all or increased generosity from those that saw the donor story emails.
To administer the test, we evenly split the file based upon (a) donor vs. non-donor statuses, (b) the donor’s highest previous contribution tier, (c) how long the person was on the email file, and finally (d) whether or not they were currently considered “engaged” or “disengaged” from the email program (where “engaged” was whether or not they had opened any of the last 10 emails that we had sent to them before splitting the email file evenly for this experiment).
The donor stories were sent from a “secondary sender” for the organization—someone who was planned to be used in two of the year-end campaign messages.
Finally, to ensure that the samples within the study are accurate, we looked at unique individual emails for both segments that received any year-end email (including the “donor story” emails for the treatment segment)
experiments.keyLearnings:
With only a 53.7% level of confidence, we observed that the treatment segment did not increase revenue in any significant way.

Although this treatment segment saw a decrease in the donor conversion rate of -23.2% (with a valid level of confidence of 99.4%) however, we don't believe that the additional donor story emails drove the decrease in donor conversion rate.

We did notice, however, that there was a +27.8% average gift value for those donors that did decide to give from the treatment segment over that of those in the control segment (who did not receive the donor stories).

Further experimentation should be considered with this approach.

experiments.title: How adding donor cultivation stories on top of a campaign appeal schedule of emails impacted revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that sending donor stories for year-end campaign recipients will achieve increase revenue.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/10/2020 - 01/02/2021
The Leadership Institute decided to experiment with adding donor stories on top of their regularly scheduled calendar year-end campaign email appeal schedule to experiment with whether or not it inspired more people to give at all or increased generosity from those that saw the donor story emails.
To administer the test, we evenly split the file based upon (a) donor vs. non-donor statuses, (b) the donor’s highest previous contribution tier, (c) how long the person was on the email file, and finally (d) whether or not they were currently considered “engaged” or “disengaged” from the email program (where “engaged” was whether or not they had opened any of the last 10 emails that we had sent to them before splitting the email file evenly for this experiment).
The donor stories were sent from a “secondary sender” for the organization—someone who was planned to be used in two of the year-end campaign messages.
Finally, to ensure that the samples within the study are accurate, we looked at unique individual emails for both segments that received any year-end email (including the “donor story” emails for the treatment segment)
experiments.keyLearnings:
With only a 53.7% level of confidence, we observed that the treatment segment did not increase revenue in any significant way.

Although this treatment segment saw a decrease in the donor conversion rate of -23.2% (with a valid level of confidence of 99.4%) however, we don't believe that the additional donor story emails drove the decrease in donor conversion rate.

We did notice, however, that there was a +27.8% average gift value for those donors that did decide to give from the treatment segment over that of those in the control segment (who did not receive the donor stories).

Further experimentation should be considered with this approach.

experiments.title: FY21 – CYE Notecard Image Test
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the image of the notecard for all donors will achieve higher net revenue.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/08/2021
The test was run during December 2020 in the notecard, which landed in-homes around 12/15. The election was a prominent topic in the news at the time, most receiving it just after the electoral college cast their votes. We wanted to see if changing the image on the outer envelope made a difference. Three versions were tested:
1) The control was a stark, wintery image of SCOTUS
2) The first test was an image of a family with young kids walking in a snowy forest
3) The second test was an image of a wintery barn with an American flag
experiments.keyLearnings: First, image selection matters. Second, the image of SCOTUS, which has been our control, was the better route to go. This is likely because our donors care deeply about our work at the Supreme Court and it felt most like ADF.

experiments.title: How positioning what happens if a donor doesn’t give rather than what happens if they do impact donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that positioning the “fear of loss” (eg what won’t happen if the donor doesn’t give right now) will impact donor conversion rate when soliciting donors within the context of a campaign.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/04/2021 - 02/10/2021
As a part of The Fund for American Studies’ recent Founder’s Day campaign, they were asking high capacity donors to contribute to building a matching gift fund that would be used to challenge other donors to join the movement to rally right now and do more to help more students and teachers in 2021.
We wanted to experiment with what would motivate donors to take action now: either (a) the hope of what could be achieved through their gift right now, or (b) what won’t happen without their gift right now.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We achieved neither the minimum number of samples we needed nor did we observe a wide enough variance between the two treatments to reliably validate that one variant over the other.

We knew that validating this experiment might be too difficult, considering we were running it against such a small universe of names (of very few high capacity donors on the email file).

For the sake of thoroughness, we decided to check the statistics related to a revenue lift study based upon these results — and we only observed a +6.5% increase in revenue for the treatment, but with only a level of confidence (LoC) of only 56%. Finally, the number of samples achieved on a revenue lift study was nowhere near where we'd need it to be, and it only achieved a "coin flip" likelihood to improve results by only a few percentage points.

All of this is to say: Further experimentation should be considered moving forward but on a much wider universe of available prospects by which we can potentially validate a lift for one variant over another.

experiments.title: How communicating what the benefits are to the donor impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding the donor benefits impact conversion on a monthly donation page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/10/2020 - 12/07/2020
Care Net has a stand alone monthly gift acquisition donation page. In an effort to increase the number of recurring gifts and the overall revenue, they tested adding copy to the page that communicated tangible benefits for the donor.
experiments.keyLearnings: The experiment was only able to run for a short time before traffic to the page was no longer being pushed to it. But during the week the experiment ran, there was no negative impact on monthly donor conversion and we even saw promising data that there could be an increase in overall revenue. While we will want to test this again, these initial data points tells us that the donor benefit language could have a significant impact on overall revenue; people like and want to know what they will receive by becoming a monthly donor and increases motivation to give and give more generously.

experiments.title: How does a cye campaign specific donation page affect revenue?
experiments.researchQuestion: Calendar year end specific language on a donation page will increase revenue.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/23/2020 - 12/31/2020
During their calendar year end campaign, the Bill of Rights Institute wanted to see if calendar year end specific language would increase donations from the direct traffic to their site. We ran a 50/50 split between their usual main donate page and their calendar year end campaign page.
experiments.keyLearnings: We need to continue to test as the sample size was not large enough to reach validation. We will test this again during the next calendar year end campaign. We will also test longer value proposition in the body copy of their main donation page to see how much that increases donations and revenue.

experiments.title: How the sender of cultivation emails during a campaign performed when soliciting for gifts
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the sender of cultivation emails during a campaign for campaign recipients will achieve increase revenue.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/06/2021
The Leadership Institute decided to experiment with adding donor stories on top of their regularly scheduled calendar year-end campaign email appeal schedule to experiment with whether or not it inspired more people to give at all or increased generosity from those that saw the donor story emails.
As the second part of this year-end experiment, we were interested to learn whether the second sender (who sent the donor stories) would increase the donor conversion rate and/or revenue when soliciting gifts from the treatment segment (that saw the donor stories) over that of the control segment (that did not).
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 61.1% level of confidence, we observed a -12.3% decrease in revenue.

Similarly, we only observed with a 48.7% level of confidence a -13.6% decrease in donor conversion rate, as well.

Ultimately, we were unable to validate either metric through this experiment and further experimentation may be required.

experiments.title: Does the signer on a Dear Reader impact donation rate?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that having a specific signer on a dear reader for blog pages will achieve higher donation rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/21/2020 - 12/31/2020
During KCBI’s calendar year end campaign, we added Dear Reader notes at the end of their blog posts asking for a year end donation. Originally the design was to have the note signed from “The KCBI Team”. But since the majority of their blogs are by one radio personality, we wondered if having her be the signer instead would increase donations. So we tested it.
experiments.keyLearnings: There was not a large enough sample size to validate this experiment or even show a directional lift. However, we did find that Dear Readers are a valuable source of revenue and that further experimentation is needed.

experiments.title: How increasing the force of the value proposition and removing elements of friction impact donor conversion
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: Would strengthening the value proposition on the primary donation page increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/14/2019 - 03/29/2019
The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate’s recently changed the kind of donation platform they were on and saw an increase in donor conversion just by improving the donor experience with the new platform. Now, they wanted to test the messaging to see if they could increase donor motivation even more. To do this, they added a headline that reinforced the value proposition of the page, strengthened the messaging, removed elements of copy that didn’t enhance the value proposition and added a clear call-to-action.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running the experiment for more than two months, we saw no difference in donor conversion. This can be attributed to the overall low donor conversion on the page. We propose testing other elements on the page that might increase overall revenue rather than increase donor conversion. The next experiment we will run in an effort to do this is testing around the suggested gift array.

experiments.title: How personalization on a donation page impacted donations and revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Will personalization on an instant donation page after a content offer increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/08/2020 - 10/02/2020
As part of a giving day campaign, DTS was offering a content offer and wanted to test using the donor’s first name in the headline to see if this would drive conversion rate on the instant donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings: While we did not reach validity with this experiment, when evaluating the results and drilling down even further by each segment, we noticed that while overall conversion rate decreased on the page, we saw an 89.4% lift in average gift—specifically with the returning visitors segment—with an 88% level of confidence. Across all traffic we saw a 40.8% lift in average gift with an 82% level of confidence. These findings in the directional lift in average gift will be used for future testing and will be useful for developing what language we present on donation pages to returning visitors moving forward.

experiments.title: How a “monthly-only” form affects recurring gift conversion during a recurring gift campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: How will a “monthly-only” form affect conversion during a recurring gift campaign?
experiments.summary:
Ended On:
Hillsdale College was running a campaign to increase membership to their recurring gift club, the Liberty and Learning Society. Their call center had noted that many people in the past who made recurring gifts did so believing that they were one-time gifts, and called to cancel. Therefore, they suggested that the form be a “tabbed” donation form that they defaulted to a monthly gift, but still allowed one-time gifts. The team wanted to test this against a “monthly-only” form to see if the lack of a one-time option moved more people towards one-time gifts. Or, put another way, they wanted to see if having a one-time option acted as a bailout mechanism that gave people an “out” to give a short-term gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment, with the monthly-only option, had no effect on the amount of recurring gifts submitted through the form. This indicated that, at least in the near term, there was no confusion about usage of the tabbed form. As an additional bonus, many people who did not make recurring gifts did make one-time gifts as well, leading to another source of revenue for the campaign, though it was not aligned with the intent of the campaign.
The caveat to this test comes in that it will take some time to see if the tabbed form generated as many downstream donor cancellation calls as the monthly form, though that data can be tied back to the original source form.

experiments.title: How adding an “About Us” section to the landing page impacted donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Would the addition of an “about us” section to tell the organization’s history and beliefs to the landing page impact donor conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/17/2020 - 10/03/2020
As a part of promoting a new content offer, The Fund for American Studies wondered whether telling more about their organization’s history would help boost email signups, but most importantly, whether or not it would increase the donor conversion rate after someone had signed up to request the resource they were offering.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With only a 61% level of confidence, we observed a -23.7% decrease in donor conversion rate. This was subsequently driving a similar decrease in revenue, as well.
We did observe roughly a directional increase of +8.5% in email signup rates, but we decided to turn this off and focus experimentation elsewhere.
Further experimentation may be required, as we did notice an uptick in revenue from returning visitors with the treatment, but decreases in new visitors. This may be driven by new visitors largely being motivated by the offer, instead of the organization. Perhaps adding this description to the donation page may be a better place within the funnel for experimentation next.

experiments.title: Mug Signer Test Revenue (Low-Dollar Donor)
How asking lapsed donors to be one of a few donors to give a specific amount impacted donor conversion rate during a matching gift campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: How does communicating a specific quantity of donors we’re asking to make a gift relevant to the lapsed donor impact donor conversion or revenue?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/04/2020
As a part of The Heritage Foundation’s matching gift campaign (called “The Board Challenge”), we wanted to experiment with the ask amount and approach. Typically, the organization has used “make your best gift today” kind of language within their control copy.
In an attempt to set a smaller, more reasonable target, we wanted to experiment with driving lapsed donors to be “one of just X donors we need today to make a gift of Y.”
The idea behind this is to (a) set a relevant ask amount for the donors, but also (b) allow them to see that they can help achieve a “small quantity” goal at that amount, as well.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We did not validate an increase, we did see we did observe a +10.8% increase in lapsed donor conversion rate — albeit at only a 58% level of confidence.
Further, the revenue was relatively flat between the two segments (only showing a +7.2% increase, with a level of confidence of 60.9%). It was promising, however, that we also observed that 29% of the people in the treatment segment decided to give more than the amount that was asked for, while another 41% gave the exact amount that was asked of them.
Conclusion: Perhaps further experimentation with the ask amounts is advisable for lapsed donors, but this directionally showed a lift in revenue.

experiments.title: How reinforcing the value proposition on a specific gift handle can impact overall revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Will emphasizing the higher gift handle of $50 increase average gift or donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/22/2020 - 08/13/2020
Billy Graham Evangelistic Association wanted to find a way to increase the average gift for the donors that finished one of their pledges. It was hypothesized that one potential way to do that would be to increase the perceived impact of some of the higher dollar value gift amounts. To do that, they highlighted the $50 gift button which would have been a ~20% increase to average gift. This same gift handle was previously mentioned on the page prior to the ask.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for a month and a half, we did not observe a statistically significant change in average gift or conversion rate. One hypothesis behind this is that the value of the $50 was mentioned in the copy right before the form. As a result, this visual emphasis didn’t have the same level of impact it may have otherwise had.

experiments.title: How does donor centered copy affect revenue?
experiments.researchQuestion: Donation page copy that speaks more directly to the donor rather than about the organization will increase donor
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/07/2020
BRI was offering a new eBook called Equal & Inalienable Rights. The donation page copy was very centered on the mission and impact of the organization. We wanted to test an approach that spoke more directly to the reader and drew them into the mission by focusing more on the benefits to them and to their impact. So we redirected 50% of their traffic to a new donation page with more donor centered copy centered around what they were receiving and why they should give. Donation page copy that speaks more directly to the donor rather than about the organization will increase donor

experiments.title: How a progressive form field impacts webinar conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding a progressive form field to ask for approximate annual revenue impact conversions?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/29/2020
Internally, we’re wanting to find ways to get more data on the size of the organizations that subscribe to our emails. But we didn’t want to sacrifice conversion rate from our webinars for the sake of getting a little more data.
We wondered if we could add a field asking for their organization’s approximate annual revenue. We had previously tested into asking if they work for a non-profit vs for-profit. So we figured the least invasive way to ask for revenue was as a “progressive form field.” This means that the field only showed up if they checked that they worked for a nonprofit.
*This was the 2nd of an identical test to see if greater volume would impact significance.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We saw a marginal shift in conversion rate – a drop of 3.9%. But this drop didn’t come anywhere close to reaching statistical significance.

This was the 2nd of an identical test after seeing an “invalid” result the first time. We thought that more volume could give a stronger indication of impact. The combined results of this experiment yield the same result…a 3.6% decrease, but with an invalid result.

If we determine that the benefit of getting this user-reported revenue data more valuable than the slight risk of small decrease in conversion, we’ll roll this treatment out.

experiments.title: How removing a video affects email appeal revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Will removing the video from the landing page (and thumbnail from the email) increase donation revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/27/2017 - 12/04/2017
We saw a number of different factors and potential learnings at play this test.
First off, eliminating the softer CTA (“Watch Video”) in the email reduced the open-to-click rate by 34.9% in the non-video treatment.  However, there wasn’t a corresponding reduction in donations—likely suggesting that the larger audience clicking the control was less motivated to give and more motivated to watch the video.
At first glance when reviewing overall giving, we saw a 2.1% increase in conversions for the non-video treatment . . . but at a 28.8% decrease in revenue. Neither met our 95% confidence threshold, although the revenue decrease had a confidence of 90.9%.  For this reason, we scrutinized the data more closely to ensure that we didn’t miss a learning.
After reviewing the individual transactions from both variants, we found that the gift amounts had significant outliers due to $,1,000+ donations.  Knowing when to filter data is a difficult challenge of testing and optimization—but since we have repeatedly seen that higher-value gifts are less likely to be motivated by an individual element of a broad-base ask, we typically exclude these from revenue validation.  After making this adjustment, we see the smaller difference noted in the results table.
Here are a few thoughts on this result, and why it requires additional testing to validate:
Response sizes are too smallDue to a low conversion rate among both target segments, there isn’t enough data to back up the normal distribution of gift amount due to its existing variability.  This is especially true of a non-donor segment that was tested with similar results, where conversion rate was just 0.007%.  Further testing, including during higher response windows, may improve the actionability of results.
The video wasn’t the only thing testedWhen removing the video thumbnail from the email, it was replaced with a different image, which was also rotated.  The additional call to action button was also removed instead of being modified. Copy was added in that area, but from a different testimonial changing the value being conveyed.On the landing page, the same additional testimonial copy was used, but was added much later in the copy compared to the video.  When testing video as a medium, the core value proposition of the video should be represented in text—if not, you’re also testing between different value proposition or the removal of some of the value proposition presented completely.
There were significantly more outliers in the control segmentWhile excluding $1,000+ gifts from both segments made a difference, it’s difficult to tell what the normal distribution of gifts is and whether segmentation played a factor in the results.  Typically the only ways to answer these questions are to measure historical distributions to be able identify abnormal ones, or to have a larger number of results to help balance any small number of outliers.
Due to the significant impact we’ve seen when replacing video on appeal pages, we would recommend further testing to ensure a clear and applicable learning for this audience.
experiments.keyLearnings: The team at Focus on the Family had seen experiments from other NextAfter partners suggesting that video on donation forms could actually be harming conversion.  With an appeal featuring video set to go out, they decided to test into this effect to see whether their audience would respond the same.  This particular test was against a segment of existing donors, although a non-donor segment was also tested.

experiments.title: How a progressive form field impacts webinar conversion rate

rows.name: Progressive Form Field Asking for Organization Size

experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding a progressive form field to ask for approximate annual revenue impact conversions?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/04/2020 - 06/16/2020
Internally, we’re wanting to find ways to get more data on the size of the organizations that subscribe to our emails. But we didn’t want to sacrifice conversion rate from our webinars for the sake of getting a little more data.
We wondered if we could add a field asking for their organization’s approximate annual revenue. We had previously tested into asking if they work for a non-profit vs for-profit. So we figured the least invasive way to ask for revenue was as a “progressive form field.” This means that the field only showed up if they checked that they worked for a nonprofit.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We saw a marginal shift in conversion rate – a drop of 3%. But this drop didn’t come anywhere close to reaching a significant level of confidence. It’s possible that running this for longer and getting more traffic to the page could prove that this decrease is in fact real – but the odds are low that this makes a significant impact over time.
We will continue to test the placement of this field, but knowing that the likelihood of a negative impact is very low. And the upside is that we get better segmentation and targeting for future outreach efforts.

experiments.title: The impact of using a campaign graphic in an email appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a “plain” email without the Giving Tuesday Now! graphic convert more donors than one with it?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/05/2020 - 05/08/2020
For the international Giving Tuesday Now! campaign, we decided to test the impact of using the campaign’s graphic in our digital appeal for the event. We wanted to see if adding it would increase or decrease donor response
experiments.keyLearnings:
We found that the version without the graphic had a 23% higher CTR on the donation link than the one with the graphic. However, those who clicked on the link in the email with the graphic were 45% more likely to convert to donors, meaning that our overall donor and revenue conversion was 41% higher for the variation with the graphic than our normal “plain” template.
We want to do further tests as our sample size was too small for confidence, however it seems that there was the potential for a directional lift in these results.

experiments.title: How a more personal tone in a direct mail letter can impact donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a more personal approach to Direct Mail impact donor conversion and overall revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/03/2020 - 04/16/2020
Back to the Bible has shifted the tone and approach to their digital communication dramatically in the past year. The emails they send to people are very conversational, intentional, and personal. Their email file health has seen a dramatic improvement since this change was put in place. The focus of the emails has been to care for people and their spiritual life. Because of this responses digitally, we were curious how this approach might affect their Direct Mail strategy. To test this, we wrote two different Direct Mail letters. One letter was more traditional in nature – the call-to-action focused on the premium offer and included a P.S. at the end of the letter. The treatment was more conversational, soft, and personal in nature – more similar to the approach we’ve taken in their digital communication. We split their Direct Mail file in half and made sure there was an equal number of donors and nondonors for each variable.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After six weeks of the Direct Mail piece being in homes, we pulled the results of the experiment and found that there was no significant difference in donor conversion. We did see more overall revenue from the treatment, but this is mostly attributed to a handful of major gifts that came in from that variable. We will continue to monitor the results of this campaign over the next few weeks, but we'd also like to test this approach again and take an even more radical approach between the two letters. As you can see in the copy, the main body of the text was the same between the two versions.
Next, we'd like to test taking the digital appeal and just repurpose it for Direct Mail.

experiments.title: How relevant, non-specific “crisis” language affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How will relevant, non-specific “crisis” language affects conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/19/2020 - 04/01/2020
When COVID-19 became a worldwide pandemic, the importance of CaringBridge became greater. As people were sheltering-in-place at their homes, the digital connections CaringBridge helped facilitate were more significant—which also increased the urgency to support CaringBridge as a platform.
