They wanted to find the right way to integrate this into their fundraising message, hoping that it would increase conversion rate. They created messaging that didn’t address COVID-19 specifically, as not to be exploitative, but addressed the larger sentiment. Then, they tested this in their highest revenue placement, the tribute widget.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After seeing an initial increase in conversion (that got as high as 15% in the first week), the data normalized at no significant increase in response. This could be because the copy was NOT specific to COVID-19, but could also reflect the fact that prospective donors did not share the sentiment that CaringBridge becomes more valuable in crisis times. There are two possible paths to take here:
Continue to test non-time-sensitive copy on the tribute widgetBe more explicit in the COVID-19 messaging to see if response can be increased with more specificity.

experiments.title: How using a smaller gift array impacted donor conversion on acquisition forms
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a smaller gift array increase donor conversion rate on acquisition forms?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/31/2020 - 02/28/2020
In an attempt to increase donor conversion rate, Americans for Prosperity experimented with whether or not they were asking too much of 1st time donors. So, they wanted to test a smaller gift array to see if it would boost donor conversion rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Although the experiment didn’t validate (achieving only a 56% level of confidence), we noticed an 18% decrease in donor conversion with this experience.
We also noticed that revenue decreased proportionately to the decrease in the donor conversion rate, showing a 19% decrease, which was largely driven by less volume in the treatment experience, because the average gift was only about 2% lower for the treatment.
From here, we recommend further experimentation with variable gift arrays, but note that it may not have that much of a difference in terms of acquiring new donors.

experiments.title: How inviting donors to contribute to a matching gift affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How will the opportunity to contribute to a matching gift affects conversion versus a traditional matching gift?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/25/2020
Leadership Institute was running a special “pre-Giving Tuesday” campaign as the initial part of their year-end campaign. The team wanted to test whether offering donors the opportunity to contribute to a matching gift would inspire more generosity, as opposed to a traditional matching gift as an incentive (as had traditionally been done). They developed two series of emails and split donors by previous giving amounts to run each through a three-email campaign leading up to Giving Tuesday to determine which approach resulted in more gifts and revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings: In terms of conversion, there was no statistical difference between the two approaches. Given the same size of housefile recipients, the incentive to “be the match” did not get more people to give.

experiments.title: How lowering the donation ask for the Daily Prayer Book impacts transactions and revenue
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will lowering the ask amount to $10 increase donations and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/14/2019 - 11/15/2019
We have a page that offers a Daily Prayer book for $25. We thought that this could be a little to high.
experiments.keyLearnings: There was no difference in the amount of donations when adding a lower amount. The control had an Avg. Order Value of $24.09 with $2,072 Revenue. The treatment had an Avg. Order Value of $20.91 with $1,798 Revenue.

experiments.title: How the kind of offer impacts the kind of donor acquired
experiments.researchQuestion: Which offer would be the best acquisition opportunity on article pages?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/19/2019 - 08/01/2019
Through a series of tests, Focus on the Family discovered that segmented GOAA offers – based off of what people are consuming on the Focus website – results in the best conversion from article pages. That said, we were curious if the GOAA offer was the best opportunity to present on the article pages. To test this, we proposed testing the free video series content offer that also had a donation ask at the end of the name acquisition funnel against the GOAA offers.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment only ran for ten days and we saw no difference in donor conversion between the two offers. As we dug into the data a bit more, we learned a couple of valuable insights…
Our primary metric is donations and revenue, but what is not communicated in these results is that the video series also allowed us to acquire 472 emails addresses in addition to the donations and revenue. These emails also provide an opportunity for downstream donor conversion which isn’t measured from this experiment.
While donor conversion was not impacted by the kind of offer, revenue was. The average gift on the video series offer was significantly higher than the GOAA offer. In fact, revenue increased by over 600% with the video series offer. What this tells us is that we are able to acquire donors that have a higher value to the organization through the video series, and we are able to acquire “purchasers” through the GOAA offers. People viewed the GOAA offers as transactional, whereas the video series as an investment in the ministry and lives of other families like theirs.
The video series is the best method to acquire donors for the organization on the article pages. They provide the opportunity to not only acquire high value donors for the organization, but capture emails as well that could convert to donor downstream.

experiments.title: How removing one of the payment plans can impact member conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Can we increase member conversion by removing one of the payment plans?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/14/2019
Adventures in Odyssey offer people three different payment/membership plans. They wanted to simplify their membership process and hypothesized that if they removed one of them, they may be able to increase memberships, and ultimately overall revenue. To test into this, we removed the 6 month payment option during the second step of the membership process. We kept the 1 month and 12 month options for the treatment and split the traffic in half to test our approach
experiments.keyLearnings: After running this for a month, we found that there was no significant difference in conversion. We had hypothesized that we might be able to minimize user decision friction by removing one of the options, but we learned that removing the six month option had no impact on conversion. We’d like to test into this more and see if we can eliminate decision friction in another way by emphasizing the most popular option.

experiments.title: How a “most popular” pre-selected value in the gift array did not hurt conversion or revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Would a visual treatment using that increases the visibility of the pre-selected value increase the average gift from instant donors?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/04/2019 - 11/04/2019
In an attempt to increase the average gift for The Fund for American Studies’ acquisition campaign, we decided to experiment with a “most popular” treatment on the $50 amount in the gift array to see if it would increase the average gift amount from instant donors.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Although we did not see a validated increase in either donor conversion rate or revenue, we observed a directional lift in both metrics.
The key learning with this experiment was that after running for a month, this treatment DID NOT show, at any point, a decrease in either one of these metrics, which was enough for us to consider that the lifts we achieved were directional, and that there would not be risk in rolling this treatment out as the “new control” version of the donation page.

experiments.title: Impact of changing the design of a banner ad and directing it to a monthly donation page

rows.name: Dear Reader to pledge to monthly gift

experiments.researchQuestion:
We wanted to learn two things:
Would changing the design of the ad so it was conversational and matched the look/feel of the site decrease visual friction for visitors
Would directing people to a monthly donation page increase donor revenue on the ads.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/17/2019
For several years, we have been running banner ads on a popular pro-life website to try to drive traffic to become donors. Our most successful ads have been ads for our pledges, the most recent being one against infanticide. We wondered if making our ad match the look and feel of the site, rather than have its own design, would decrease friction for visitors. Additionally, we wanted to see how well conversational language would work in the ad. Our final desire was to see how well directing people to a monthly donation page vs a one-time donation would impact the revenue generated from the ads.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We ran the test for a month. Both ads had great engagement, however the ad on the sidebar got far more clicks than the dear reader variation. We also, as expected, got far more donations on the sidebar ad as it led to a one-time donation page. We brought in 19 donations and raised $633 on that ad. However, through the dear reader variation ad, we brought in 4 new monthly donors and raised $195.70 in their first months gifts. This will result in $2380 over the first year of their giving, 369.7% higher revenue than the control ad. 

Moreover, while the sidebar ad had far more clicks than our variation, it had a lower conversion rate to donors, even with it using a one-time donation page. .27% of clicks on the ad became one-time donors while .31% of clicks on the dear reader ad became monthly donors. While we didn’t have enough samples to validate this portion of the test, it is encouraging to see that we got higher donor conversion on the dear reader variation.

Our next test will be to change the sidebar ad so it drives traffic from a pledge to a monthly donation page. This will let us see which ad brings in more monthly donors.

experiments.title: Dear Reader footer ad vs a mid-page pledge ad (500% increase)
experiments.researchQuestion: Which ad will bring in more monthly donors, the dear reader footer ad or the pledge ad?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/13/2019
In a prior test, we found that we could significantly increase the revenue from our Life News ads by creating a footer ad that looked like part of the site, using “dear reader” language on the ad, and linking it to a pledge and then to a monthly donation page. (the results of that test are here: https://app.winstonknows.com/experiment/2423) The “dear reader” ad had higher conversion and higher revenue than the pledge ad, but the pledge got more clicks. We wondered if we would bring in more monthly donors on the pledge due to its higher volume of clicks if we changed it so the pledge ad linked to the same monthly donor campaign as the dear reader one.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While the dear reader maintained a higher click to donation rate (16.67%), the pledge ad had 627% more traffic. This caused us to bring in 500% more monthly donors on the pledge ad than the “dear reader” footer ad. We also had a 76% higher email acquisition rate on traffic to the pledge ad vs the dear reader.
We aren’t sure how much of these results is due to the higher placement on the page of the pledge ad. We will likely test a “dear reader” version of this mid-page placement in the future.

experiments.title: How a radical donation page treatment impacts acquisition revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a radical redesign of the donation page increase overall revenue to the acquisition campaign?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/01/2019 - 08/03/2019
After running for nearly six months, we had not been able to achieve a statistically significant lift to the donation page used with Buckner’s 7 Scripture offer. After multiple iterative approaches, we decided to test a radical redesign of the page. We combined multiple factors based upon positive results seen with similar organizations.
The down side to this approach is that it makes it difficult to isolate exactly what helped move the needle if a lift is achieved. However, the overall goal was to lift revenue and if that could be achieved then knowing the exact reason for the lift became less important.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While we did not reach a statistical level of significance, we did see an increased average gift from the treatment version. Initial results saw the treatment also have a higher conversion rate but this balanced out after a few weeks of running. This is a good reminder of why it is so critical to ensure there is both a significant amount of time and total conversions before making a call on any experiment.
Our current hypothesis for the increase in average gift is that the treatment makes a targeted ask of $50 while the control version asks for $25. However, given all of the other factors that changed, we will need to isolate this factor to prove it out in the future.

experiments.title: How the experience on a donation confirmation page impacts subsequent gifts and overall revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Can we increase the number of subsequent gifts and overall revenue by improving the experience on the donation confirmation page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/11/2019 - 05/15/2019
A lot of focus gets put on the optimization and improvement of the actual donation process and little attention is put on the experience after a person makes a gift. We hypothesized that improving this experience and making the donor feel valued, celebrated, and affirmed in their decision to make a gift to Focus on the Family we might be able to impact and improve subsequent giving and overall revenue from the donor in the future. To do this, we developed a treatment experience on the donation confirmation page that thanked the donor by name, celebrated the donor in the copy we used, reinforced the value proposition of their gift by sharing two testimonials, and even had confetti explode on the page as soon as the donor landed on the page. We tested this approach for over six months to really assess the impact that we might be able to have on giving over a longer period of time. Half of the audience during those six months experienced the control confirmation page and the other half experienced the “Super, Happy, Thankful” treatment experience.
experiments.keyLearnings:
To analyze the winner, we looked at donors based upon the initial treatment they saw and removed any recurring donations since they were automated. We then looked at the likelihood of a donor to make a subsequent gift, the number of subsequent gifts, and their change in revenue (did their subsequent giving increase). In all of those situations, there was no statistically significant difference between the control and treatment.
Even though we didn’t see an impact on future gifts and revenue, the treatment did a better job in thanking and celebrating the donor – which is important. Maybe the one joyful, celebratory experience on the donation confirmation page wasn’t enough to impact giving, but could we create that same experience in a series of emails every single month for the donor and have an impact on giving? We’d like to test it.

experiments.title: The impact of direct mail cultivation on overall revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Does sending cultivation and stewardship-focused post cards increase revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/01/2018 - 06/11/2019
For the complete writeup, visit https://app.winstonknows.com/e/z7Nd.
Hillsdale College was looking for new ways to help retain existing donors. Knowing the importance of good cultivation and stewardship, we wanted to focus on methods that could improve the frequency and method for communicating impact and gratitude to the donors. One hypothesis was to send post cards throughout the year that both thanked them for their support and reminded them of the impact their gift.
To do this, we isolated 350K donors and split it randomly in such a way that there would be equal representation for each donor lifecycle segment. Half of these donors would receive four post cards throughout the year in addition to the normal communication stream. The control group would just get the standard communications.
experiments.keyLearnings: We did not see a statistically significant change to overall revenue. We did observe a 1.8% increase to donor retention / reactivation but the decrease in revenue per donor resulted in minimal difference between overall revenue.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign and donation bracketing affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: How will a radical redesign and donation bracketing affects revenue?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/29/2019
Jerusalem Prayer Team had a successful micro-donation acquisition campaign to raise money to feed Holocaust survivors. However, they wanted to see if they could increase conversion or revenue through additional landing page testing. They created a treatment that centered the value proposition at the top, included a picture of a Holocaust survivor, put the copy on a white background for more legible reading, and then reoriented the gift array. Previously, the gift array offered $3 and $90, with a pre-filled $30 ask in the open field. The treatment page offered the $3 gift, along with a $45 gift and a $90 gift. The hypothesis was that the landing page elements would increase conversion and the bracketing might increase average gift. They launched an A/B test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment produced a 67% increase in revenue that had a valid level of confidence, though with the small overall sample size of donors, this was not fully valid. This showed that the page elements did not, in fact, increase conversion, but that the bracketing did increase the average gift enough to validate the separation between the control and the treatment.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign of a main donation page affected conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding third-party validation and more context about the organization in the copy increase conversion rates on the donation page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/12/2019 - 05/07/2019
The Texas Public Policy Foundation (TPPF) is testing their donation pages to increase conversion rates. They wondered if talking more about Texas and how TPPF can help Texans and the rest of America be more free would make a bigger impact on donations. They decided to take a radical redesign approach to their main donation page. They added a header with a call-to-action, more copy that explained what TPPF does and who they help, removed links that took people to a different page, and added an image and quote of a famous Texan for third-party validation.
experiments.keyLearnings: The results from this experiment were surprising. The radical redesign caused a directional 38.4% decrease in revenue, but with no statistical validity. The original donation page had a better conversion rate, even with the less-inclusive copy and the links to another site. However, because the confidence level is not at or above 95%, this experiment is not statistically valid. TPPF should continue to run experiments on their main donation page, but overall conversions combined will make it difficult to validate a result.

experiments.title: How asking for support to extend our reach did not improve donor conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: Will asking Freedom Pledge signers to help us by funding our efforts to find more people like them outperform asking them to become a “donor activist”?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/26/2019 - 05/11/2019
As a part of Americans for Prosperity’s Freedom Pledge offer, we wanted to see if we could motivate donors to give towards helping us reach more people to band together with them to create a movement, as opposed to a straight-forward “become a donor activist” appeal to see if we could lift the donor conversion rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Now that this version of the offer’s message is turned off, we turned off this experiment.
With only a 28.4% level of confidence, we did notice a developing trend of -14.3% on this approach. We did, however, also notice a +20% increase in revenue (with a level of confidence of 62%) with this approach.
Further experimentation may be required to determine the overall viability of asking this audience to fund reach, as opposed to asking them to become a “donor activist”.
It should be noted that this was related to the “original” Freedom Pledge positioning/language, which has been replaced, where overall, that messaging appeared to drive a higher level of instant donor conversion motivation when compared to the new messaging that we are required to use with the offer.

experiments.title: How targeting direct mail prospects with digital advertising affected direct mail revenue
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does targeting direct mail prospects with branding ads and content offers via Facebook ads increase direct mail revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/17/2019 - 12/31/2018
Missionary Oblates had significant investments in their direct mail program and wanted to see if they could optimize the return by investing in digital marketing that would target these prospects in the time period that they received a direct mail letter.
They created a control audience that was excluded from seeing any ads, and a treatment audience that would see a rotation of brand and eBook offering ads (but no donation-centric ads) for a two week period before the mail piece hit mailboxes and for two weeks after, the prime time in which a donor would respond. They optimized the ads for reach—aiming to show ads to as large a percentage of the target group as possible. The goal of this test was to lift direct mail revenue—not to add an additional segment of online revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment was not valid. We’d like to test this again at a different time of year, when the motivation of donors isn’t necessarily as high as it is at year end.

experiments.title: How using a primary issue on the main donation page increased donor conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: Can moving a “defeat cronyism” -focused message into a single column layout outperform the two-column, generic message of “restore economic freedom” message?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/21/2019 - 04/21/2019
We wanted to experiment with improving the results of the primary donation form. In reviewing the “control” experience, we theorized that we could lift performance by addressing two issues:
Creating a more “issue-focused” message (defeating cronyism), and
Moving to a single column layout (instead of a two-column layout).
experiments.keyLearnings:
Although the experiment ran for more than 3-months and we only achieved a 47.9% level of confidence, we did see a +15.0% increase in donor conversion rate.
We did notice that the treatment also saw a +60% increase in revenue (with a 71.1% level of confidence), as well.
Although neither treatment officially validated, we believe this information is directional enough (having run for 3-months) to move forward with the treatment donation page. It would appear that the primary issue the organization is largely focused upon solving (ending cronyism) is more inspirational, driving a higher conversion rate and average gift/revenue than the control message.

experiments.title: How suggesting impacting in the giving array impacts revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Does aligning the gift array to specific impact increase overall revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/06/2016 - 04/08/2016
For the Hillsdale moneybomb dedicated to raising funds for the DRTV campaign, we had a unique opportunity for the college. We were able to assign impact to the amount of money given; for $70, a donor could help secure a single DRTV spot.
Since we are not often able to assign specific impact to a gift, we decided to test what aligning the gift array to this impact would have on revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test had a limited amount of time that we could run so we were not able to reach statistical validity. What we observed was that the gift handles that tied the gift to the DRTV spot received less revenue per visitor.
After examining the actual gifts given, we found that we lost smaller gift donors with the treatment. This would be the result of the gift array starting at $70 instead of $25 which is our control. This would not normally be an issue if we can significantly raise the average gift but, unfortunately, this treatment did not do that.

experiments.title: How Donation Page copy test between pro-life vs. fiscal angle on defunding Planned Parenthood
experiments.researchQuestion:
Does including the term “pro-life,” mentioning that we work with “pro-life organizations” and that we are for stopping all abortions resonate with our donors and increase gifts and revenue?
OR
Does only focusing on goal of stripping federal taxpayer dollars from going to Planned Parenthood increase gifts and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/29/2015 -
We have some limited knowledge that those who want to defund Planned Parenthood don’t necessarily identify themselves as pro-life. They are fiscally conservative and not necessarily social conservative. We want to drill into this and see how much of our base identifies this way. This will help guide messaging moving forward- helping with what terms to use and what terms to avoid.

experiments.title: How communicating value with the gift array affects revenue and member acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Which donation form will produce the most revenue and new members for the organization?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/29/2015 - 08/27/2015
The Texas State Historical Association is a membership-based organization with varying levels of benefits at each member level. The idea is to incentivize members to increase their giving in order to raise the average gift.
The most common donation form listed out each membership level and the associated benefits. This design led to a long donation form that was heavy on the copy. Our concern was that this structure produced additional friction to the giving process which would, in turn, lower conversion.  We wanted to find a way to decrease the size of the form while still communicating the value of each membership level. We decided to test a new form that utilized stylized buttons and a single, dynamic paragraph to describe the associated benefits of their selected level.
experiments.keyLearnings: The new treatment with stylized buttons produced no significant lift to either average gift or conversion. We were able to reduce friction on the form but, by hiding the benefits of each membership level, we equally reduced the incentive. These two factors offset each other which resulted a no significant change.

experiments.title: How the donation premium can affect both conversion rate and average gift
experiments.researchQuestion: Which premium will increase the overall revenue from existing members?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/13/2015 - 09/30/2015
As part of Texas State Historical Association’s fiscal year end campaign, we wanted to find a way to improve the average gift of existing members in order to boost revenue. The majority of existing members were acquired using the premium of the 2014-15 Texas Almanac. The update to this book was going to be released in the coming months so we had a feeling it would be a popular premium offer to use with the donor base.
However, this book is normally offered for a gift of $50 which meant it wouldn’t do much to raise the average gift. We decided to test another popular book as a premium and increase the minimum gift to $100 or more.
experiments.keyLearnings: The Along Forgotten River premium offer reduced the donor conversion rate of the page by 50%. However, it was also able to increase the average gift by 64%. These two factors cancelled out the combined impact on revenue and produced no statistically significant change in either direction.  This does prove to us that if we can find the right premium offer, we should be able to increase the average gift enough to boost revenue.

experiments.title: How the premium’s minimum gift amount affects revenue and member acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Which offer will acquire the greatest amount of revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/06/2015 - 01/11/2016
Prior to the move to the $100 minimum gift amount for the Texas Almanac, we wanted to get an idea of how a $100 minimum gift premium would impact the revenue and number of new members acquired. With the Handbook of Texas Music eBook offer, we had the opportunity to test this out by offering the Almanac for $50 and the Handbook of Texas Music for $100.  While not a true test of the impact of moving the impact to $100, the idea was that we could get an idea of the potential ramifications.
experiments.keyLearnings: With this experiment, we did not see any significant change in either conversion rate or average gift. Based upon these results, it would seem the premium was not the primary motivation for members to join.

experiments.title: How clarifying the value proposition affects revenue on a donation page.
experiments.researchQuestion: Which landing page will increase revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/01/2016 - 03/08/2016
In response to Hillary Clinton’s plans to repeal the Hyde Amendment, SBA List created a petition in support of the Hyde Amendment. Traffic was pushed to the petition page through email and Facebook advertising. Once someone signed the petition they were taken to a thank you page with a donation ask.
SBA List wanted to do a radical redesign of the donation page to try and increase revenue. The control was a page layout they had been using previously. For the treatment, they tested a one column approach with a new headline and body copy that reinforced and clarified the value proposition. They also presented fewer giving arrays and a new call-to-action on the button. They did an A/B split test to determine which page would perform better.
experiments.keyLearnings: They were not able to get a large enough sample size to validate the test. Though the initial data they were able to get did show the one column treatment performing better because of a higher average gift, it is not enough data to come to a conclusion with those results. A similar test will be done for a future campaign with a larger sample size to validate the results.

experiments.title: How the inclusion of graphical data in an email affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Does adding a statistical image that reinforces the message increase the number clicks generated for the landing page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/16/2015 - 04/20/2015
As part of their monthly April appeal, Jews for Jesus wanted to validate the work being put into different aspects of the monthly fundraising campaigns.  One of these elements was a visual that was placed in the email to help drive interest in the topic and statistically reinforce the topic of the campaign. The goal of the experiment was to determine if the content needed the external reinforcement provided by the graph.
experiments.keyLearnings: With no statistically significant lift to the number of visitors to the page, it becomes evident that the image of the video and content of the email is enough to pique the interest of the email file.  The additional image of the graph did not have any influence on the visitors’ choice with regards to the call to action.

experiments.title: How quotes made along side the donation form affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Will 3rd party quotes lower anxiety enough to improve upon the donor conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/23/2015 - 06/29/2015
During their a campaign to help make up for their financial shortfall, Jews for Jesus wanted to find a way to increase the conversion rate for the campaign landing page. A previous experiment had yielded a 252.6% lift by simplifying the donation form. It had reduced friction by removing key elements of the donation form which provided the lift.
For this new experiment, we placed quotes in support of Jews for Jesus from well known figures in the Christian community next to the donation form. By adding the quotes, we hoped to add 3rd party credibility which would, in turn, lower the potential donors’ anxiety.
experiments.keyLearnings: In this situation, the 3rd party quotes did not have any statistically significant impact on the conversion rate of the page. It would seem that by the time donors made it down to the donation form, they had been significantly convinced of the credibility of Jews for Jesus that they did not need any additional information.

experiments.title: How high visitor motivation affects donation page experiments
experiments.researchQuestion: Which donation page will produce the greatest conversion rate and average gift?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/02/2015 - 07/20/2015
In an effort to lift the conversion rate and average gift of their primary donation page, Jews for Jesus launched a radical redesign of their existing design. The goal was to boost the value communicated on the page with additional copy, lower anxiety with quotes from 3rd party sources, and modify the donation form to reduce friction.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this test for nearly two months, there was no statistically significant difference between the two treatments when analyzing both conversion rate and revenue.
After examining the results in greater detail, we saw that both treatments had a conversion rate of ~65%; this was information not available before the launch of the experiment. A conversion rate that is this high is rare for an organization the size of Jews for Jesus. This would seem to indicate that only the most highly motivated donors are making it to the donation page.
Based upon these observations, the next logical step in optimization is to look earlier in the donation funnel to find ways to drive more visitors to the page.

experiments.title: How adding a login option back to the donation form affects conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: Which donation page will lead to the most revenue and donors?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/13/2015 - 10/26/2015
After two previous unsuccessful attempts to lift the revenue coming into Jews for Jesus primary donation page, we decided to take a step back and try to get a better understanding of the donors. The page converts at a high rate (~60% to ~70%) which means we are dealing with a highly motivated audience. It also means these visitors are likely existing donors which means they may not need as much convincing and may find value in elements that would cause friction with new donors.
After examining our previous treatment, we realized that by removing the login feature, we may have inadvertently made it harder for existing donors to give. On a donation page visited by primarily nondonors, this feature would have caused friction in the process but for existing donors, it would make it easier.  We decided to relaunch the previous value proposition test but add the Login feature back into the form.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Our previous tests had decreased the conversion rate of the page. With this latest test, we were not able to statistically increase or decrease the results. What this means is that the login feature was impacting the donors’ likelihood to give.  It also means we have not yet discovered a value proposition that would prompt existing donors to give more generously.
In addition to our efforts to drive more traffic to this page, our next experiment will test a new value proposition in order to find a message that resonates better with the Jews for Jesus audience.

experiments.title: How a radical treatment to the primary donation page affects average gift size
experiments.researchQuestion: Which landing page treatment will produce the greatest lift to donor conversion rate or revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/29/2015 - 12/04/2015
On the Jews for Jesus main donation page, the default language is a simple headline with a call to action. We had the hypothesis that if we reduced the friction associated with the form, added language that expanded upon the primary value proposition, and inserted quotes from well known figures in the Christian movement, we could improve the already high average gift value.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment produced no significant lift to either conversion rate or revenue per visitor. It would seem that we have still not found a stronger way to communicate Jews for Jesus’s primary value proposition to their already highly motivated donors.

experiments.title: How the copy on the page affects conversion of a highly motivated audience
experiments.researchQuestion: Will recognizing and thanking the visitors for their previous action increase the donations and revenue to the subsequent page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/20/2015 - 09/14/2015
The Media Research Center (MRC) was running a petition to the major news networks to increase their coverage of the Planned Parenthood scandal. After the initial signature, the visitors were sent to a second landing page and given the opportunity to make a gift. The copy on the page continued the messaging on the initial petition page and targeted the outrage the visitors would be feeling at the lack of media coverage for the Planned Parenthood videos.
After reviewing this copy, we realized that what the follow on page never did is acknowledge the visitor’s prior actions, specifically the signing of the petition. We decided to create a new treatment that would first thank the visitors for signing the petition and then lead in to what else they can do to help solve the problem (i.e. give a gift).
experiments.keyLearnings: After a month and a half of running the experiment, the new treatment produced no statistically significant change in conversion or revenue. It is our hypothesis is that this lack of change goes back to a previous experiment where we learned the motivation of the visitors to this page was extremely high. The copy on the page was not the reason the majority of donors gave so any changes made would not have any significant impact.

experiments.title: How a change in tone of the landing page affects the performance
experiments.researchQuestion: Which landing page treatment will yield the most donors and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/24/2015 - 10/09/2015
As part of their campaign to kick off the election season, the Media Research Center wanted to find the best possible messaging tone to drive an increase in donations and revenue. A previous experiment using member benefits had not produced any significant lift so we decided to take a more radical approach at test the tone used on the page.
Our hypothesis was that landing page that more clearly laid out the problem and emphasized how the potential donor (instead of the organization) could help solve it would convert better. We also removed the 3rd party credibility indicators since, after further consideration, those may have confused the message.
experiments.keyLearnings: Neither landing page was able to produce a significant lift to either conversion rate or revenue. This would suggest that the tone of the message was not influencing the campaign as much as the core message itself. For future campaigns, it would be best to take a more radical approach and test the entire messaging platform before getting to the final days of the campaign.

experiments.title: How the messaging approach affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Which messaging approach will get the most donors and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/24/2015 - 10/09/2015
In the final days of the Media Research Center’s high urgency campaign, we wanted to see if a new approach to messaging could increase the conversion rate for the existing donor segment.  The existing copy took a stewardship approach of listing out the recent accomplishments of MRC and crediting the donor for making it happen.
The new treatment removed the accomplishments and instead highlighted recent examples of media bias.  It also added a premium offer for a gift of $100 or more and removed 3rd party credibility indicators that we hypothesized were actually working against us.
experiments.keyLearnings: The radical redesign of the landing page did not produce any statistically significant lift to donor conversion or revenue. Upon further reflection, the donor conversion rate of the page was near 25% for both treatments. By the time the test was launched, the visitor motivation was already significantly high enough that the messaging approaches didn’t have much impact.

experiments.title: Web Donation Form (Buttons vs. No Buttons)
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding buttons with a predefined choice increase donations and donation/visitor?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/06/2015 - 11/12/2015
We were looking to increase donations on our main donation form. We believed that giving people a pre-defined choice helped decrease anxiety and thus lead to more donations & higher revenue/visitor.
experiments.keyLearnings: We did not achieve confidence on the increase in donations, although the numbers were clearly higher.  Donation/visitor was visibly higher during this test.

experiments.title: Insider Insights – Positive (Us) vs Negative (InProv)
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a positive spin, including a prayer, cause more people to donate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/17/2015 - 12/19/2015
We were trying to see if creating a more positive spin of this week’s Insider’s newsletter would create more donations/ increase donation/visitor.
*Because this test was written internally, we did not have the ability to sign the email with Jonathan’s name. Therefore, the positive version was also written using “we” and “us” vs “I”.
experiments.keyLearnings: We found that the number of donors was down by a very small margin, but overall brought in more revenue and had a higher revenue/visitor.

experiments.title: One Column vs Two Column Donation Form (WEB)
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a simpler form give us more donations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/31/2016 - 04/11/2016
We are looking for ways to simplify our donation form to increase donations and revenue. This form was inspired by a form we found at another nonprofits website. Their form designed exactly the same was their highest performing form. We went with this design to see if it would outperform our current design.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Although this form looked really nice and was modern in design, it failed to do better than our normal form. In fact, revenue for our regular form was double what this form pulled in.
A few things we see:
The picture could be distracting for people who just want to donate (causing friction)
A new form with this many changes could have made regular donors less apt to donate (anxiety)
The form was not simple enough. There might have been too much friction.
We did not add enough value. Although we added our value proposition and text that is known to convert, it did not add enough value.

experiments.title: How running Facebook ads to Membership Card campaign targeting impacted donor conversion rates from non-members
experiments.researchQuestion: Does adding a third channel in Facebook advertising (alongside email and direct mail channels) improve response rates or revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/01/2019 - 03/31/2019
We wanted to continue experimentation with running Facebook ads alongside focused campaign messaging used this time to promote their Membership Card campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With only a 63.2% level of confidence, we were unable to validate this experiment.
Directionally, we did note a -7.8% decrease in non-member donor conversion rate.
Overall revenue decreased by -11.9% within this treatment, as well.
Our hypothesis is that the non-member segment splits may have more to do with the likelihood that a donor will be reactivated with the third channel of outreach (online advertising).
Note: Offline gifts were not included as a part of this assessment. This included online transactions only, and was run through the first half of the final day of the campaign. Additional updates will be forthcoming.

experiments.title: How Facebook ads impacted a campaign’s lapsed member conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding a 3rd channel of touches increase donor conversion rate for reactivating lapsed members?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/12/2019
During The Heritage Foundation’s Kickoff Campaign, we wanted to run Facebook ads designed to remind lapsed members to renew their annual membership to see if it would boost response rates by adding a 3rd channel (digital ads) along side regularly scheduled email and direct mail messages.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment did not validate for this donor segment, but directionally, we see alignment with the non-member and member segments that both validated towards increasing segment response rates when serving ads.
It’s worth noting that similarly to the non-member experiment we ran, the total cost to run these ads to lapsed members generated a 4.2 ROI against the increase in revenue generated from the “with ads” segment. Simply put, 100% of the ad expense to increase responses from this audience was covered and generated an amount equal to slightly more than 3x the spend in additional revenue free and clear of any expense.
Final Conclusion:Further experimentation is required with future campaigns for the lapsed segment, but directionally these results align with active and non-member segments seeing an increase in response rates with ROI free and clear of the incremental expenses associated with adding the 3rd messaging channel of Facebook ads.

experiments.title: How strengthening the value proposition on the primary donation page impacts donor conversion?
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: Would strengthening the value proposition on the primary donation page increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/14/2019 - 03/29/2019
The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate’s recently changed the kind of donation platform they were on and saw an increase in donor conversion just by improving the donor experience with the new platform. Now, they wanted to test the messaging to see if they could increase donor motivation even more. To do this, they added a headline that reinforced the value proposition of the page, strengthened the messaging, removed elements of copy that didn’t enhance the value proposition and added a clear call-to-action.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running the experiment for more than two months, we saw no difference in donor conversion. This can be attributed to the overall low donor conversion on the page. We propose testing other elements on the page that might increase overall revenue rather than increase donor conversion. The next experiment we will run in an effort to do this is testing around the suggested gift array.

experiments.title: How did the use of an exit popup increase donor conversion rates from The Daily Signal visitors?
experiments.researchQuestion:
We had two questions:
How did the use of an exit popup increase donor conversion rates?
Which message would convert the most donors (stand with either (a) the president, or (b) Heritage)?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/02/2019
On The Daily Signal’s year-end fundraising page in 2018, we installed an exit popup with two messages, where one was focused on “Stand with the President” while the other was “Stand with Heritage”.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Although the experiment did not validate, we gleaned some insight from the experiment.
Exit Popup Tactic Observations:
It’s also worth noting that the landing page secured a total of 27 transactions without firing the “exit popup”, and that the “exit popup” regardless of messaging variant secured an additional 42 gifts combined between the two message types. That means that the exit popup seemingly secured an additional 55% more gifts from donation page viewers who were attempting to leave the page. Subsequently, the revenue from the “exit popup audience” drove an incremental 58% in additional revenue to the audience that did not see the popup.
This supports the idea that the exit popup does indeed work to secure additional transactions and revenue.
Messaging Observations:
Although the “Stand with Trump” message actually had a 14% higher click rate on the affirmative button (40 / 350 = 11.4% CTR) compared to the “Stand with Heritage” message (which was 38 / 391 = 9.7% CTR).
However, we actually saw that the “Stand with Heritage” message drove an increase of 8.4% for donor conversions (5.9% vs. 5.4%).
Finally, The “Stand with Heritage” also had a 12.6% higher average gift value ($45.81 vs. $40.67) compared to the “Stand with Trump” message.
Final Conclusion:
With only 21% of the required sample size collected during this experiment, we propose continuing to test which messages work best to drive donor conversions, but that it would certainly appear that installing an exit popup on donation pages can have a profound impact as a tactic to convert donors and drive incremental revenue that otherwise would leave the donation page without interruption.

experiments.title: How a vanity URL impacted online giving from a direct mail appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: Will pushing donors to online giving increase revenue overall?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/22/2019
What Was Tested
We tested adding a box to the bottom of the copy and RD that said: “Give ONLINE at: AllianceDefendingFreedom.org/donate”.
We also added the same message to the CRE.
