experiments.keyLearnings: We used the Vanity URL /Donate. I know this is a standard Vanity URL which makes me question if we properly tracked the donations from this campaign. We saw an insignificant decrease in response rate, which makes me question if these donors went online instead. Average gift and net income were also down.

experiments.title: How a higher gift array affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How will a gift array with higher values affect conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/05/2019 - 03/14/2019
CaringBridge had used a giving infrastructure that had separate mobile and desktop donation pages. As a result, certain optimizations had not been rolled out to both experiences, resulting in slight differences between the two pages. In previous tests, CaringBridge had “unified” the copy on the page by testing out of all negative impact.
However, the mobile version still had another gift array that started at $25, unlike the others that started at $50. As their final “unification” test, CaringBridge wanted to test the $50 array that was found everywhere else to make sure there was no impact on revenue.
The control had an array of $25, $50, $100, $250. They launched a treatment with the array found on the other pages: $50, $100, $250, Other.
experiments.keyLearnings: After more than 700 transactions, the new gift array had no significant difference in conversions, which gave CaringBridge the all-clear to launch the unified experience. This process, though it took time, is the proof-positive way to launch new experiences. CaringBridge could have easily just launched the same copy and donation array everywhere, but there could have been serious ramifications in revenue. This series of unification tests shows that there was no risk at all.

experiments.title: How a mid-dollar premium impacts overall revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Will using a $50 premium increase overall revenue to the page?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/18/2019
The instant donation page for Buckner’s 7 Scripture offer had seen a significant increase in conversion with the implementation of Purchase targeting on Facebook and the switch to a Shoes for Orphan Souls as the donation ask. However, with the change to Shoes, we saw our average gift hovering around $25 (the cost to provide a pair of shoes.) In an effort to increase this average gift, we decided to run a test of offering a premium for a gift of $50 or more. Our belief is that by positioning this $50 ask as the first number they see, we would be able to increase overall revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running for a month, we saw that the two pages had nearly identical conversion rates and the premium offer had a slightly higher average gift. However, this average gift was something that began trailing off over time for the treatment. Our hypothesis is that the premium (a calendar) became less relevant the further we got into the year. With the offer being less relevant, people started giving $10 gifts instead of the $50 which brought the average down to a similar level as the control.
A big takeaway from this experiment is that we were able to lift average gift when the premium is both relevant and timely without negatively impacting overall donor conversion.

experiments.title: How an exit popup did not impact donor conversion rates during a campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: Would adding an exit popup to the Kickoff campaign landing page improve donor conversion rates?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/01/2019 - 02/25/2019
During the 2019 Kickoff Campaign for The Heritage Foundation, we tried experimenting with a more emotional appeal for the visitor to activate/renew their membership, but did not see any noticeable lift in the donor conversion rate. So, we wanted to test something more tactical by adding an exit popup to intercept the visitor before they left to ask them to reconsider.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With only a 77% level of confidence, we only saw a negligible difference between the two treatments (-3%) in instant donor conversion rate.
Although the popup was displayed 1,847 times on the treatment, and 756 (or 41% of those shown the popup) of those people clicked on the button that read: “Yes, I will stand with Heritage!” — it somehow showed a decrease in instant donor conversion rates.
In digging into the data specifically, we noticed the following, as well:
Revenue/Visitor: +1.3% (68.9% LoC)
Avg. Gift: +3.79%
Even with those observations, it appears that the exit popup overall had a very negligible impact during the course of the campaign. The visitor motivation to give a gift on these pages is largely programmed via the emails (or ads) designed to drive them there. That said, experimentation with increasing motivation is likely not going to work, and tactical elements (like an exit popup) may not be effective to drive a noticeable split between the treatment and control during high conversion campaigns like this.
There are other tactical elements to experiment with during campaigns like this, but perhaps the most effective experimentation is likely going to be running tests with the motivation (which is driven from the emails / ads used to drive page traffic).

experiments.title: How longer form calendar year-end copy impacted donor conversion rate.
experiments.researchQuestion: Will longer form copy increase donor conversion rate compared to the standard shortened version?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/17/2018
When building the landing page for HAFA’s calendar year-end efforts to finish 2018, we built the “control” from a close derivative from what was used in the previous year (with updates really to the issues that were pertinent heading into the upcoming year).
The treatment draft unpacked the priorities that were being addressed in a series of emails to increase relevancy between the messaging being delivered in the inbox, such that when someone clicked one of the emails (regardless of which one it was in the series), that there would be a bullet point on the landing page addressing that particular issue as a “top priority” for the upcoming year.
Because of this approach, it increased the amount of copy on the landing page by about 20-25%. So, we wanted to experiment with whether or not the longer form copy could perform similarly or better than the shortened control language.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With the experiment only achieving a 39.3% confidence score value, further experimentation is required to fully understand the impact of the longer form copy on donor conversion rates.
Even so, we did see a slightly higher donor conversion rate (a +5% increase) on the treatment draft.
Considering we didn’t validate on donor conversions, we certainly would not have validated this experiment for revenue lift, but it is at least worth noting that we did see an improvement in revenue for the treatment draft over the control by an increase of 19.5%, as well.

experiments.title: How the donation selection area can communicate tangible impact
experiments.researchQuestion: Will variable text around the donation amount field better communicate the impact of larger gifts and thus, increase revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/22/2018 - 09/07/2018
For Buckner International’s Shoes for Orphan Souls fundraising campaign, we wanted to find the best way to visualize the kind of impact a gift will have. In this situation, $25 would fund a pair of shoes for a child in need. Historically, we had placed text on the donation buttons but the buttons were usually far lower than the average gift amount.
We had the hypothesis that if we removed the buttons all together and created variable copy around the textfield that visitors entered their information, we would be able to secure larger gifts. We decided to test this out during the fundraising campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for the entire campaign, we saw there was no statistically significant difference between either of the two forms with regards to conversion rate or revenue. It it likely that both treatments adequately communicated the impact of lower dollar gifts (less than $100) and that the larger dollar gifts are not as concerned about the tangible impact of their gift. They are giving because they believe in the overall mission.

experiments.title: How a longer value proposition on a donation page affects conversion on mobile devices
experiments.researchQuestion: How will a longer value proposition on a donation page affects conversion on mobile devices?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/02/2019
CaringBridge’s donation process had always had a separate page for mobile devices because of some legacy technology. Instead of loading a responsive page, the CaringBridge site used redirects to send mobile users to a separate page entirely. Over time, this page had not kept up with all the optimizations that had been implemented on the desktop page. CaringBridge decided to test the two pages against each other to see the cost and benefit of rolling both pages into one responsive page, which would keep them from having to keep two pages updated and in sync.
The first major difference in the two pages was the gift array. This was tested independently, and had no impact on conversion or revenue. The next major change was the value proposition and layout of the form. The mobile page had always had a shorter value proposition because it was assumed that they had less “real estate” on the page to hold such copy. The team decided to test a mobile version of the exact same donation page that desktop users saw to see the impact.
experiments.keyLearnings: There was no change—and even a slight directional increase with the new copy. This gave CaringBridge the confidence to revert to a single, mobile-optimized donation page for their tribute funnel. Going forward, they will monitor desktop and mobile results independently to see how future experiments affect conversion on each.

experiments.title: How problem-centric language affects donor conversion at year-end
experiments.researchQuestion: How will problem-centric language affects donor conversion at year-end?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/22/2018 - 12/30/2018
Hillsdale College was trying to increase conversion for their donation pages during their year-end campaign. They had previously seen that problem-centric language, or explaining the challenge they were trying to solve, had increased conversion in a fiscal-year-end campaign. However, they were unsure how that would affect results at a very different time of year, so they decided to test it.
They created a page that started with the problem, rather than positioning Hillsdale as the solution and launched an A/B test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The problem-centric language produced an 8.4% decrease in conversion, though it didn’t reach statistical validity. However, due to the time-sensitive nature of the year-end ask, they decided to continue using the control as not to lose any revenue during peak times.
Previous testing has shown that problem-centric language can increase conversion, but a calendar year-end campaign might not be the best time to try it.

experiments.title: Does expanding the value proposition in the email drive more revenue?
experiments.researchQuestion: Does expanding the value proposition in the email drive more donations for the non-donor audience?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/09/2018
During Hurricane Florence, BGEA experimented with expanding the value proposition within the body of the email it was sending to raise funds for their Rapid Response Team to minister to victims of the disaster. This was the second email in a longer series and was intended to make a direct ask for support. Our hypothesis was that many of the non-donors may not yet understand the reason to make a gift to BGEA and would require more explanation to help them toward a donation. To test this hypothesis, a second email was developed that explained more about the history and purpose of the Rapid Response Team.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a ~60% level of confidence, we learned that an expanded value proposition centered around the history of the Rapid Response Team did not motivate more non-donors to make a gift. However, we also learned that the longer copy did not really negatively impact donor conversion given the low level of significance.
This would indicate the potential for further testing of the expanded value proposition concept but using different reasons to make a gift. There is potential to focus on the increasing need that comes from multiple natural disasters that are coming in the near future or how this ministry fits into the overall mission of BGEA.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign of an in-site ask affected clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: How will a radical redesign of an end-of-article call-to-action affect clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/01/2018 - 05/15/2018
Leadership Institute had seen a large jump in donation traffic through the implementation of an end-of-article call-to-action on their CampusReform website. They wanted to test to see if improved copy and layout could increase clickthrough rate from the site and hopefully increase overall donation revenue. They created a treatment that added increased persuasive copy, a third-party quote, and statistics to bolster the value proposition. They also made a softer ask in the button, asking the reader to “learn more” rather than “support Campus Reform now”
experiments.keyLearnings: Ultimately, there was no valid difference in clickthrough rate. After receiving several hundred impressions per CTA, the treatment showed a slight decrease in clickthroughs, but nowhere near validity. The team went back to the drawing board to try to find something more radical that would positively increase clicks.

experiments.title: How modifying the donation pathway impacted giving
experiments.researchQuestion: Will linking to the donation widget increase overall donor conversion compared to linking to a separate donation page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/12/2018 - 03/02/2018
Since the launch of their homepage donation widget (found in the middle of the homepage), Alliance Defending Freedom had seen a significant lift in the amount of revenue that was coming from visitors landing on the homepage. Historically, they had also included a donate link in the banner at the top of the homepage but his button linked to a separate landing page.
We had the hypothesis that linking to the widget may actually cause less friction than linking to a separate page. We decided to test this with one of the monthly appeals.
experiments.keyLearnings: At the conclusion of this experiment, we saw minimal difference between the overall donations to the two variants. When digging into the details, we found that we were able to increase donations to the widget by 72% but this just shifted donations away from the normal donation page; it did not result in net new donations. This just proved that the jump did not eliminate friction within the donation process; it just provides an additional opportunity for giving.

experiments.title: How asking for a recurring gift vs. a single gift affects conversion in a “Dear Reader” experiment
experiments.researchQuestion: Does asking for a recurring donation affect conversion in a Dear Reader experiment?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/16/2018 - 01/25/2018
Through previous testing, Care Net found that by inserting a “Dear Reader” feature at the end of their blog posts, they were able to increase the number of people to their donation page. During the month of January, Care Net made an asserted effort to acquire more recurring donors. To support this effort, they tested adding a recurring focused ask in the “Dear Reader” feature instead of a one-time donation.
experiments.keyLearnings: You can see in the data that we weren’t able to validate the experiment in the short time that we ran it. We needed to turn off this experiment earlier than planned resulting in a small sample size. Because the Dear Reader feature gets more people to the donation page, we recommend testing this recurring gift focus again because of the potential revenue impact it could have.

experiments.title: How the method of ad optimization impact offline conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Is it better for donors conversion to get more people to see the brand ads or is it better to get less visibility but more engagement with the ads?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/27/2017 - 12/20/2017
Alliance Defending Freedom wanted to increase the results in their direct mail program during the calendar year end push. One way they did this was by incorporating online brand advertising to the recipients of their direct mail letters. The donors and prospects were shown a series of brand ads that linked to select blog posts on the organization’s website. This methodology was based on past experiments where we found that brand advertising can influence offline behaviors.
For this campaign, we wanted to discover the most impactful method for displaying the ads. In Facebook’s platform, we have the ability to optimize the ad campaign for a handful of different metrics. In this situation, we wanted to know if it was better to optimize for impressions (i.e. get more people to see the ads) or for clicks (i.e. get more people to engage with the ads).
To determine this, we split randomly split the direct mail file and setups two ad campaigns that differed only in how Facebook was optimizing the ad spend.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Once the ads had finished running, we found that we were able to get several hundred more donors and prospects to click on the ad when optimizing for clicks and several thousand more ad impressions when optimizing for that metric. Despite these differences, we found that there was no statistically significant difference in performance between the two optimization methodologies. We also evaluated the segments on revenue and saw the same results.
It would seem that both methods of optimization allowed enough donors to see the ads to have the same impact on conversion.

experiments.title: How using a countdown clock in an email appeal impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How would a countdown clock in an email appeal impact conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/07/2017 - 06/20/2017
While the experiment didn’t validate, the results were leaning towards being valid.
The treatment with the countdown clock decreased clicks by 5% and donations by 13%. What this initial data tells us is that the presence of the countdown clock didn’t motivate more people to convert, but possibly created friction in the giving process. There may have been a disconnect between the countdown clock counting down to Harvest America and the call-to-action of the email – which was to give to the fundraising campaign. The clock may also have taken away from the more casual, personal style and tone of the email that we know donors respond best to.
experiments.keyLearnings: Harvest Ministries had a high-urgency fundraising campaign prior to their  large-scale evangelism event, Harvest America. They sent a series of emails to their house file to acquire donations and revenue. In an effort to increase clicks and donations from their email, they added a countdown clock in their email counting down to the end of the campaign. This element was added to create a greater sense of urgency and motivate more people to click-through and give. They split their email file and sent the control email without the countdown clock and the treatment email with the countdown clock.

experiments.title: How year-end messaging and urgency indicators affect donation page revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: How will specific year-end messaging and urgency indicators affect donation page revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/21/2017 - 01/03/2018
Compassion International wanted to run a test on their main donation page at the end of year. Typically, people came to this page to make year-end gifts, with traffic and revenue ramping up sharply in December. However, the copy on the page remained the same all year, with nothing special done to reflect the year-end deadline.
They wanted to test copy and urgency indicators to see if they could positively affect motivation and generate more revenue from the page during the last half of December.
They crafted a treatment page with a new headline: “Make your year end donation today”. They added copy that referenced the December 31st deadline, added a story to show the impact of the donation, and added a countdown clock to visually show urgency in the sidebar.
They launched an A/B test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: The year-end treatment page showed a directional lift in revenue (14.1% increase), but it was unable to achieve statistical validity. This outlines the difficulty of testing at a time of the year where there is already high motivation. The treatment page might produce a lift, but because conversion is higher than normal on the control, there wasn’t enough separation to determine a winner.

experiments.title: How personalizing the donation page headline affected conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will personalizing the headline to include the potential donor’s name increase conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/06/2016 - 08/02/2016
In an experiment with another NextAfter partner, we had found that personalizing the headline of a donation page increased the likelihood to give by 20%. With Con101 being the biggest source of acquisition for Hillsdale, we wanted to know if the same change would have the same level of impact for them. We set up a test where the only change on the page was customizing the headline to include the potential donor’s first name.
experiments.keyLearnings: After 2 months of running the test, we did not see any significant lift to revenue or conversion rate when we personalized the donation page headline. This is another good example that there is no such thing as “best practices” in the nonprofit world. What works for one organization is not necessarily applicable to everyone. This is why we must test each and every hypothesis to ensure and changes have the intended impact.

experiments.title: How a year-end premium affects conversion, average gift, and revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Will the addition of a premium at year-end increase conversion and average gift?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/21/2015 - 12/26/2015
Hillsdale College was in the middle of their year-end fundraising campaign. A key part of the campaign was a survey about their flagship publication Imprimis, which ended with a donation ask. They were interested to see the impact of adding a premium (the Hillsdale calendar) to this message.
Premiums had proven to work in the past, but motivation is often much higher at the end of the year. They launched a test to see the impact of the premium on conversion and average gift, using “revenue per visitor” as the success metric.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While there appeared to be a slight directional lift from the premium, it was not statistically significant and did not reach a valid level of confidence. This tells us that year-end might not be the right time to offer a premium — or the calendar was the wrong premium to add.
This will lead to further premium testing throughout the year to see if it can lift conversion during times where visitors might be less motivated to give.

experiments.title: How the gift array options impacted Leaders Club memberships
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a lower gift array of $3, $10, & $15 increase the conversion rate and overall revenue to the Leaders Club program?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/21/2016 - 11/10/2016
For a recent Leaders Club campaign, we wanted to test the standard gift array of $5, $15, & $25 to see if we could lower the mental friction associated with the high monthly ask. Our hypothesis was that if we can reduce the initial ask amount, we could get more donors which would lift the overall revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings: The lower gift array did not have any statistically significant impact on a donor’s likelihood to join the Leaders Club or their gift amount once they joined. It is recommended that we maintain the current control of $5, $10, and $15 for future campaigns.

experiments.title: How a donation ask based upon the content of the article impact donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will aligning the content of the ask to the content of the article increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/27/2017 - 09/11/2017
The “Dear Reader” ask had become a significant source of revenue for The Heritage Foundation. Over a span of a year, we had tested a variety of aspects include positioning, aligning with year end, and individual value props. However, with the diversity of content on The Daily Signal, we had the hypothesis that if we aligned the content of the ask to the content of the article, we could increase the relevance of the ask which would impact donor conversion. We decided to test this out by writing 8 different value propositions that would be shown based upon the article the visitor was reading.
experiments.keyLearnings: After over a month of collecting data, we did not see any statistically significant impact come from targeting the content of the ask to the content of the article. This mirrors the findings that we had seen with similar tests run on the Heritage.org homepage when we tried to align the message to popular topics in the news. In those instances, the topical asks either had negative or no impact on donor conversion when compared to the standard organizational value proposition.

experiments.title: How showing impact of a gift on a donation page affects revenue per donor
experiments.researchQuestion: How does the layout of the donation page affect donor conversion, and revenue per donor?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/06/2017 - 12/05/2017
Buckner International wanted to increase instant donations on their 7 Scriptures acquisition offer, and wanted to test how redesigning the donation page would affect donor conversion- and also if people would give bigger gifts if they more clearly understood the impact.
The elements tested were the design of the confirmation of offer download and how it flowed into the body copy, as well as a rewritten headline and some body copy.
experiments.keyLearnings: While this experiment did not validate at a level of confidence of 95%, we saw a trend towards higher donor conversion and higher revenue per visitor with the redesigned page. This is likely due to a more user-friendly format as well as a clearer value proposition in the headline and body copy on the page.

experiments.title: How an additional donation ask impacts revenue
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will asking for an additional gift right before checkout increase overall revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/13/2017 - 05/19/2017
In an effort to optimize the Oblates online store, we had reached out to the store developers, DonorDirect. In our communication with them, we asked about a variety of features that were on our “wish list” of actions we’d like to be able to take. One of these actions was the ability to turn on an additional ask right before checkout. It turns out that not only was this possible, it was easy to do!
Before moving ahead, we wanted to measure the impact this kind of feature would have on giving. We decided to split test visitors to ensure the additional gift would not have any negative impact on overall revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running for a full 12 months, we were not able to increase revenue by a statistically significant amount. We did see a directional lift of 16.6% and it did not have any significant impact on the conversion rate. Based upon these results, we have rolled out the feature to the entire audience.
This test taught us to important concepts:
Just because we ask, doesn’t mean people will give. We need to provide a compelling reason to provide an additional gift and we need to make it an easy process to complete.
The motivation of the audience as this stage of checkout is high. The conversion rate of this page is 90% which means we can test out some long, value-heavy copy that may increase the length of the page without having a significant impact on conversion giving the motivation of the audience.

experiments.title: Personal Note email vs. Quick Donate email

rows.name: Urgent message with quick donate links

experiments.researchQuestion: The question we set out to solve was whether or not our control of a personal casual note (in this case a text message) from the CEO outperforms an urgent message with “quick donate” links that include suggested gift amounts starting at $15 going to $300.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/24/2015 - 07/24/2015
We have had much success in email copy tests with person casual tone of voice email being the winner in renewal emails, solicitation emails, and new membership emails. We have also had success with using the “quick donate” tactic on emails that are responses to what’s happening Hill and how Heritage Action is engaging in the fight.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We sent two versions of the email as a test. One had a soft ask to find out more. The other had a series of hard asks that said “quick donate $15; quick donate $25; etc”.
As expected, the soft ask sent more than 4x more traffic: 916 visitors vs. 208. But the quick-donate version had a conversion rate nearly 5x higher: 19.7% vs. 4.0%.
Result: more gifts and more revenue from the appeal with dramatically less traffic.
Moving forward we will continue testing the quick donate tactic.

experiments.title: How the cost of a premium affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a premium boost the overall conversion and revenue of a donation landing page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/16/2015 - 06/24/2015
As part of the a name acquisition campaign, Dallas Theological Seminary was giving away a free eBook in exchange for a visitor’s email address.  After the successful email acquisition, visitors were sent to a contextualized donation page.  In an effort to boost conversion on this follow up donation ask, we wanted to see if offering a relevant premium (a hardcover book on a similar topic) would boost conversion.
As a result, we created two landing pages, with one that had a premium offer for a gift of any amount.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The donation page with the premium offer did not have any statistically significant impact on either conversion rate or revenue. Since this book had an associated cost to the seminary, it didn’t make sense to continue offering the premium to donors.
For many organizations, premium offers will often have a positive impact on donor conversion and average gift but there are times where, as in this case, the impact was negligible. This is why it is so critical to test any changes to a donation form to ensure the impact with worth the cost.

experiments.title: How different value propositions on an in-site donation ask affect clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will different approaches to the prospect-level value proposition increase clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/17/2017 - 08/03/2017
CaringBridge’s user interface offered several different opportunities to give. One of these was internally called the “donate box”, and contained a personalized message that was placed in the updates section on an individual user’s journal page.
The control message had a simple straightforward ask: “Honor [FirstName] with a donation to CaringBridge. You make [FirstName]’s website possible”.  They wanted to test different messages to see if they could radically increase clickthrough rate, and with that increase in traffic, increase conversion rate and revenue.
They developed three treatments:
The first treatment reversed the order of the messages, focusing on the site itself: “[FirstName]’s CaringBridge site is supported by generous donors like you. Make a donation to CaringBridge in honor of [FirstName].”
The second treatment showed the impact of the site: Help [FirstName] stay connected to family and friends. Make a donation to CaringBridge to keep [FirstName]’s site up and running.”
The third treatment made an emotional appeal that offered the chance for the user to provide more than just funds for the author: “Show your love and support for [FirstName].Make a donation to CaringBridge to keep [FirstName]’s site up and running.”
Since CaringBridge has large amounts of traffic, they launched a 4-way test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: After several weeks of testing, none of the copy variants produced any significant lift over the control. While this was disappointing, a further review of the data found that one of the variants produced a significant increase in revenue after the click. This showed that the value propositions might not produce a result immediately, but might inspire a higher motivation to give.

experiments.title: How increasing perceived security on a donate button affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will moving the donate button inside the secure box increase conversions?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/14/2017 - 08/15/2017
CaringBridge had performed extensive testing on their “tribute” donation form, which produced the majority of their online fundraising revenue. One experiment was able to increase the conversion rate on the page by increasing perceived security, adding a box around the credit card information. However, this box left the donate button outside of the box. Their team wondered if this was leaving conversions on the table due to the button being perceived as “insecure”.
They launched a version of the page that moved the donate button into the “secure box” and launched an A/B test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: This test failed to produce a valid result in either direction. While the secure box produced a lift, simply moving the donate button inside the box was not enough of a difference-maker to affect conversion positively or negatively.

experiments.title: How emotion-driven copy affects landing page conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will emotive copy increase donation revenue on a tribute donation page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/03/2017 - 08/31/2017
CaringBridge had previously run an experiment on the journal pages of their site that produced a big lift  in revenue when “emotive” language was shown. They wanted to run an additional test to see if the same sort of emotive language would produce an increase in revenue on the tribute donation page. Since the the winning copy from the previous experiment wouldn’t deploy for a few weeks, they had a chance to test the copy on the tribute page independently. They hypothesized that if they could also get a lift on the donation page, then they might be able to have a compound lift from the two areas of improvement.
They created a treatment donation page that utilized the “love and support” language and launched an A/B test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment page failed to produce any significant difference from the control donation page. This shows that while the emotive language might be a great motivator to start the gift process, it doesn’t have a significant effect at finishing the donation process. It also may show a relationship between the emotive language in the donate box and the standard control language on the donation page—these two copy variations may be working in tandem to produce a higher revenue total.

experiments.title: How using clarity in the call-to-action can impact traffic to a donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: Which giving button copy would increase traffic to the donation page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/25/2017 - 10/11/2017
Care Net wanted to increase the volume of traffic going to their primary donation page. One of the ways to try and do this is to test the kind of copy that was being used on the “donate” button of their homepage. Their current donate button currently used value proposition focused language, “Save Babies Now”. They hypothesized that a treatment version with language that more directly reflected where the button would take them once they clicked on it might increase traffic to the donation page and ultimately have a positive impact on donations and revenue. They treatment button copy said “Support Care Net”.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment copy had a slight increase in conversion, but the results weren’t statistically valid. The experiment ran for over a month and would need a more radical treatment with a larger sample size to validate.
Even though the test didn’t validate, we can hypothesize that with the slight increase in conversion from the button that said “Support Care Net”, the copy presented more clarity with the end-users action. They knew exactly where they would be taken if they clicked on the button. By saying “Save Babies Now” in the control, the copy may have been too vague and created a higher level of friction in the mind of the end-user – where would they be taken if they clicked on that button? What if they weren’t ready to “save a baby now”?
When we test copy in a call-to-action, it is good to keep in mind that clarity trumps persuasion.

experiments.title: How an offer with low appeal affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Which donation page treatment will receive the highest amount of revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/09/2016 - 08/19/2016
During Buckner’s Shoes for Orphan’s Souls campaign, we ran a series of experiments with the goal of boosting donor conversion. One of the experiments was to run Facebook ads asking directly for a gift to the campaign. The normal flow for Facebook acquisition is to offer a premium in exchange for an email address followed by a donation ask. However, with this specific campaign, we thought a gift may be an appealing enough offer that the initial email acquisition was not necessary.
To make sure we got the most out of this campaign, we created two landing page treatments to test for the offer. The goal was to ensure we were going with the right message for the audience. The control included some additional language around Buckner International and the broader mission while the treatment focused specifically on the Shoes aspect of the ministry.
experiments.keyLearnings: The results of this experiment spoke more towards the general appeal of the offer than the individual results of the pages. After spending $200 on ads, we did not net a single donor. As a result, we shut down the offer before we could achieve a valid sample size and result. This concept, “general Buckner value prop” vs. “Shoes specific value prop” is a concept worth testing again next August.

experiments.title: How adding a gift array to a year end donation page affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How does adding gift array buttons to a donation form affect donor conversion and average gift?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/21/2017 - 01/03/2018
Buckner International was running their year-end campaign and wanted to test how adding a gift array to their year-end donation page would affect donor conversion and gift value. All other elements of the page remained the same.
experiments.keyLearnings: We saw a 10% decrease to conversion rate (not statistically significant) but a 42% increase to revenue per visitor when we removed the buttons during year end (not statistically significant). Given the low significance, this is something that would need to be re-tested in future appeals before making any definitive statements. However, based upon these results, it may make sense to segment high dollar donors ($100+) and provide them with a different giving experience than lower level and non-donors. Basically, use the buttons to remove friction for the less motivated audience and then allow the higher donors to choose their own level of generosity. Again, this is a hypothesis that will need to be revalidated with a future test.

experiments.title: How donor page redesign affects visitor motivation
experiments.researchQuestion: Which donation page will result in the greatest conversion rate and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/20/2016 - 06/13/2016
After the Safe Bathroom campaign initially launched, we noticed that the donation page had a relatively low conversion rate of 1% to 2%. After examining the donation page, we decided to run a radical redesign that would reduce the length of the page while reinforcing the reason they initially signed the pledge that led them to this page.  This radical redesign would also bring in imagery from the original page to try to reinforce that original value proposition.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment is a good example of why it is necessary to give experiments enough time before making any quick decisions. At the initial launch of the experiment, the Control seemed to have a significantly higher conversion rate but, as time went on, this difference leveled out. By the time we ended this experiment, there was less than a 70% level of confidence in either treatment meaning neither showed significant signs of improvement over the other.
This test largely focused on tactical aspects of the page such as form length and imagery. In the end, those changes were not enough to sway the visitors to give a gift.

experiments.title: How the addition of Apple Pay impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will the addition of Apple Pay decrease the friction associated with the donation process enough to increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/24/2017 - 04/18/2017
The Heritage Foundation is consistently looking for new ways to make the donation process both easier and better for their members. The Apple Pay option had recently gone mainstream with its incorporation into both iPhones and Mac web browsers. This one-click giving option made it significantly easier for visitors that had the service set up; for those that did not, they would not have seen any difference between the forms.
However, before rolling out the new feature, we wanted to measure its impact on the donor audience. Since this new feature was only applicable to Safari users on a Mac, we targeted the experiment to that audience.
experiments.keyLearnings: The new Apple Pay feature did not have any statistically significant impact on donor conversion or revenue. Our hypothesis is that this is likely due to the slow adoption of Apple Pay for Mac and Apple users. The portion of users that will be implementing Apple Pay will only be increasing in coming month so we decided to roll out the new form for visitors moving forward.

experiments.title: How low visitor motivation affected value proposition arguments
experiments.researchQuestion: Which value proposition will lead to the highest number of gifts and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/06/2015 - 07/03/2015
Heritage was running an email acquisition campaign using a petition against the government’s Common Core initiative. As part of the process, signers to the petition would be sent to a donation page following their signature.  The standard practice for these types of campaigns was to create a landing page thanking them for their signature and asking them to make a gift to Heritage based upon the high-level value proposition of Heritage.
We had a hypothesis that if we could focus the language on the page on how Heritage is specifically impacting this topic (Common Core), it would lead to a better conversion rate.  As a result, we created an experiment to test this hypothesis.
experiments.keyLearnings: By the time the campaign was ended, we did not have enough conversions to be able to reach a statistically significant conclusion.  In this situation, there was a low motivation to give a gift for visitors coming to the page. As a result, neither of the value propositions tested were able to significantly impact the overall conversion of the page.

experiments.title: How Imprimis’s value proposition affects conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: Which value proposition will inspire the most donations and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/25/2015 - 08/17/2015
The standard Imprimis donation page does not receive enough traffic each month to validate any tests. However, with the Imprimis survey that was sent out in August of 2015, we were able to drive enough visitors to possibly validate an experiment.
We created an experiment that tested two unique value propositions. The first is the current control which talks about the mission of the college and how Imprimis is connected to that. The new treatment utilized the cost metrics of Imprimis and framed it in terms of what it costs to send to both the donor and other like-minded people.
experiments.keyLearnings: Neither treatment created enough of a lift to validate the experiment. This proved to us that both had equal appeal in the mind of the donor. We will continue forward with the existing control.

experiments.title: How the minimum premium gift amount impacted revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Which price point for the premium will result in the greatest lift to revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/14/2016 - 07/26/2016
In one of the more successful donor acquisition campaigns for Heritage, we were offering a copy of The Guide to the Constitution for a gift of $100 or more. To see if we could improve the revenue made from the campaign, we created a treatment that offered the same premium for a minimum of $125 gift. We targeted the offer at some rented lists.
experiments.keyLearnings: We saw a 20% decrease to conversion rate and a 25% increase to average gift as a result of the higher minimum gift amount. This meant slightly more revenue (not statistically significant) but since this campaign was targeted at primarily donor acquisition, it did not make sense to make slightly more revenue while losing 20% of new donors. As a result, we then moved on to testing a $75 minimum gift to see how that would impact revenue and donor acquisition.

experiments.title: How the imagery of a page affected the campaign performance
experiments.researchQuestion: What impact does adding imagery to the page have on donations and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/17/2015 - 09/28/2015
For the September money bomb for Hillsdale College, we wanted to find a way to increase the overall performance of the landing page. We had the hypothesis that by adding supporting imagery that reinforced the messages being communicated on the page, we could make the content more engaging and easier to understand. In this situation, we were using references to the Constitution and quoting notable historical figures such as Thomas Jefferson.  We decided to set up a new treatment that would include images of those elements along with the text.
experiments.keyLearnings: Adding the imagery to the page had not significant impact on the overall performance of the page. We will often debate the amount of influence design has on the overall conversion of the page. In this situation, the design elements did not increase value proposition, lower friction, or reduce anxiety; as a result, that made no significant impact on the likelihood of a donor to convert.

experiments.title: How a modified value proposition affected campaign performance
experiments.researchQuestion: Which value proposition will have the biggest impact on revenue and conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/28/2015 - 09/30/2015
In the final days of the September money bomb for Hillsdale College, we were looking for ways to improve results. After reading the original copy on the page, we decided there were areas that we could add additional clarity to. Our hypothesis was that by adding the additional clarity, we could improve the understanding of the value proposition which would lead to increased conversion rate on the page.
experiments.keyLearnings: The modifications made to the copy on the page had no statistically significant impact on either revenue or conversion rate. In this situation, it would appear that the changes were not radical enough to make a major impact. It is also worth noting that at the end of a money bomb campaign such as this, the motivation of the visitors is often high enough that subtle changes will not have a major impact.

experiments.title: How the landing page design affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Which design will allow for the greatest number of gifts and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/24/2015 - 09/25/2015
A recent experiment was done to bring older landing pages up to the latest levels of optimization. However, what this experiment proved is that a design that works for one page may have the adverse reaction on another.
Based upon these results, we decided to revalidate the main Heritage donation page to ensure that we were using a design that was achieving the best possible results for us.
experiments.keyLearnings: After 90 days, this test had not yet reached statistical significance so we decided to turn it off in order to move to the next experiment. However, based upon a level of confidence above 85%, we can classify this as “approaching validity.” This result reinforces that the current simple design of the main donation page is effective with the current amount of copy.

experiments.title: How adding a Donate Now button at the top affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Does adding a donate button at the top of the page for returning visitors increase their likelihood to give?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/12/2015 - 07/01/2015
For their fiscal year end campaign, Hillsdale College wanted to find a way to make it easier for their donors to make a gift.  The landing page being used contained a large amount of copy in order to fully communicate the college’s unique value proposition however this copy also added friction to the page.
We had a hypothesis that if we could add a way for returning visitors to skip this copy, by placing a Donate Now button at the top, we could reduce the overall friction of the page.  The concern with this approach is that this may ultimately reduce the average gift of the donations since they are not seeing the full value proposition.
We decided to run an experiment targeting just return visitors to the page and testing two different copy versions of the button versus no button at all.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Showing the buttons to returning visitors did not make a significant impact on either conversion rate or revenue.
When digging deeper into the results, we found that only 2% to 5% of returning visitors even clicked on the buttons.  This may speak more to their location outside of the eye path of the page than their effectiveness at reducing friction for the donor.  It may be worth running this experiment again but placing the button more in the eye path of the visitors.

experiments.title: How the minimum gift amount affects donation form results
experiments.researchQuestion: Which minimum gift amount will result in the greatest amount of revenue and donors?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/07/2015 - 06/24/2015
For the Heritage Foundation, the minimum level for membership technically begins at $25. However, the renewal page for their members has a minimum donation amount of $2 which is often utilized by visitors.  In an effort to lift the average gift, we decided to implement a new page that would make the minimum gift amount $25 to match the membership levels.  When doing this, we want to be sure to monitor both donor conversion and overall revenue to ensure this new $25 minimum didn’t significantly impact the number of donors acquired.
experiments.keyLearnings: Both revenue per visitor and conversion rate of the two different pages were nearly identical and neither achieved statistical validity. We learned from this test that, with regards to membership renewal, the minimum gift did not factor into most donors’ decision to give.
